By applying **strain transformation equations** and equating the principal strains to those obtained from a uniaxial tension test, we obtain:

$$
G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)}
$$

This is the fundamental **isotropic linear elastic relation** for homogeneous materials.

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Given:**
- $d = 20 \, mm$, $P_{max} = 196 \, kN = 196000 \, N$, double shear
- $\sigma_{u} = 490 \, MPa$, $E = 2.0 \times 10^{5} \, MPa$, $\nu = 0.30$

**Step 1: Cross-sectional area**

$$
A = \frac{\pi d^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 400}{4} = 100\pi = 314.16 \, mm^{2}
$$

**Step 2: Experimental shear strength**

$$
\tau_{u, exp} = \frac{P_{max}}{2A} = \frac{196000}{2 \times 314.16} = \frac{196000}{628.32}
$$

$$
\tau_{u, exp} \approx 311.94 \, MPa
$$

**Step 3: Expected shear strength from empirical formula**

$$
\tau_{u, expected} = 0.6 \times \sigma_{u} = 0.6 \times 490 = 294 \, MPa
$$

**Step 4: Percentage deviation**

$$
\% \, deviation = \frac{311.94 - 294}{294} \times 100 \approx +6.1\%
$$

A deviation of **+6.1%** is within typical lab tolerance (acceptable range is ±10%). Hence, the empirical relation **holds** for this mild steel specimen. ✓

**Step 5: Modulus of rigidity**

$$
G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)} = \frac{2.0 \times 10^{5}}{2(1 + 0.30)} = \frac{2.0 \times 10^{5}}{2.60}
$$

$$
\boxed{G \approx 76,923 \, MPa \approx 76.92 \, GPa}
$$

This matches the standard accepted value of G for mild steel.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2:** The most common mistake is using $\tau_{u} = P/A$ instead of $\tau_{u} = P/(2A)$ in a double-shear setup. This **doubles the result** and costs full marks. Always read the question carefully — note "single shear" vs. "double shear."
> 2. **Unit conversion errors:** Loads given in **kN** must be converted to **N**; diameters in **cm** must be converted to **mm**. Marks are deducted even if the formula is correct.
> 3. **Area formula for hollow rods:** If the question specifies a hollow rod, use $A = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(d_o^2 - d_i^2)$ — many students mistakenly use only $d_o$.
> 4. **No sketch or diagram:** For 7-mark procedure questions, **a labelled diagram of the UTM shear attachment is mandatory**. A neat sketch carries **1–2 marks** by itself.
> 5. **Comparing with expected values:** Always state the expected value with a justification (e.g., using the empirical relation) and compute percentage deviation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Shear Strength Definition:** Maximum shear stress a material withstands before fracture.
- **Double-Shear Formula (most used in KTU lab):** $\tau_{u} = \dfrac{P_{max}}{2A}$
- **Single-Shear Formula:** $\tau_{u} = \dfrac{P_{max}}{A}$
- **Cross-sectional Area:** $A = \dfrac{\pi d^{2}}{4}$ (solid rod)
- **Empirical Relation (Mild Steel):** $\tau_{u} \approx 0.6 \, \sigma_{u}$
- **E-G-ν Relation:** $G = \dfrac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$
- **Standard Mild Steel Shear Strength Range:** **250 – 380 MPa**
- **Apparatus:** UTM + Shear attachment + Vernier caliper + Mild steel rod specimen
- **Test Configuration:** Standard fixture creates **two shear planes** (double shear).
- **Fracture Surface:** Dull, fibrous texture indicates **ductile shear failure**.
- **Key Constants:** $E \approx 200 \, GPa$, $\nu \approx 0.30$, $G \approx 77 \, GPa$ for mild steel.
- **Unit Vigilance:** Always work in **N and mm** to get **MPa** directly.
- **Sketch is Mandatory:** Always include a labelled diagram in procedure-type questions.
- **Acceptable Deviation:** ±5–10% from empirical value is considered a successful experiment.
- **Real-World Relevance:** Used in design of rivets, bolts, pins, keys, and shaft couplings.
- **Examiner Trap:** Don't confuse with **torsion test** — torsion measures **G**, shear test measures **τ**.

> **End of Module Experiment 2 Notes — Shear Strength of Mild Steel Rod**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Perform Brinell, Vickers, and Rockwell Hardness Tests on Materials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUL407 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Perform Brinell, Vickers, and Rockwell Hardness Tests on Materials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Hardness?

In materials science and mechanical engineering (per **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus**), **hardness** is formally defined as:

> The **resistance of a material to localized plastic deformation**, typically caused by mechanical indentation, abrasion, scratching, or cutting. It is an empirical property (not a fundamental material constant) and is measured using standardized indentation tests.

Hardness is an *umbrella property* — it correlates with **strength, wear resistance, fatigue life, and machinability**, but it is not a direct substitute for yield/tensile strength unless an empirical conversion is applied.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Students must demonstrate competence in *performing* all three hardness tests (Brinell, Vickers, Rockwell) and *calculating* the respective hardness numbers from measured indentation geometry.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Mud Analogy"

Imagine pressing a steel ball, a diamond pyramid, and a small steel cone into a slab of fresh clay (mimicking metals).

| Indenter Type | What Happens in Clay | Engineering Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| Big steel ball pushed with heavy weight | Clay yields widely but shallowly | **Brinell** — soft, coarse, bulk measurement |
| Diamond pyramid with light force | Tiny, crisp square pit | **Vickers** — universal, precision, even micro-scale |
| Steel ball or diamond tip, two-stage loading | A small dot you can measure with a dial | **Rockwell** — fast industrial readout, no math needed |

Think of hardness testing as *"asking the material how much it dislikes being poked"*. The **deeper** the poke, the **softer** the material.

---

## 1.3 Why Three Tests?

No single test is optimal for all materials and situations. Each test evolved for a specific need:

- **Brinell (1900)** — first standardized, best for **coarse, rough, heterogeneous materials** like cast iron and forgings.
- **Vickers (1925)** — uses a diamond, suitable for **all materials** (very soft to very hard), and for **micro-hardness** (thin films, weld zones).
- **Rockwell (1919)** — fastest, **direct dial readout**, ideal for **shop-floor production** testing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Fundamental Constants Used:**
> - Standard Brinell ball diameter **D = 10 mm** (also 5 mm, 2.5 mm, 1 mm).
> - Vickers included face angle **136°**.
> - Rockwell minor preload = **10 kgf**, major load = **60, 100, or 150 kgf**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Brinell Hardness Test (BHN)

### 2.1.1 Apparatus & Principle
- **Indenter:** Hardened steel or tungsten-carbide ball of diameter **D**.
- **Load P:** Applied perpendicular to the surface for a standard dwell time (10–15 s for steel, 30 s for softer metals).
- The load is chosen so that the indentation diameter **d** lies in the range **0.25 D < d < 0.5 D** (to be considered a valid test).

### 2.1.2 Governing Equation
The Brinell Hardness Number is defined as the applied load divided by the **curved surface area of the spherical-cap indentation**:

$$\text{BHN} = \frac{P}{A_{\text{indentation}}}$$

### 2.1.3 Standard Load Selection
To satisfy the geometric similarity principle (similar impressions on similar materials), the load is fixed by:

$$P = K \cdot D^{2}$$

where **K** is a material-dependent constant (in **kgf/mm²**).

| Material | K (kgf/mm²) | Typical Load (D = 10 mm) |
|---|---|---|
| Steel, cast iron, nickel, titanium alloys | **30** | 3000 kgf |
| Copper, copper alloys, aluminum alloys | **10** (sometimes 5) | 1000 kgf |
| Lead, tin, soft bearing alloys | **2.5** | 250 kgf |

---

## 2.2 Vickers Hardness Test (VHN / HV)

### 2.2.1 Apparatus & Principle
- **Indenter:** Square-based diamond pyramid (opposite faces meet at **136°**).
- **Load P:** Typically 1–100 kgf (macro), or 10 g–1 kgf (micro-Vickers).
- The 136° angle was chosen so that HV ≈ BHN in the medium hardness range (≈ 400 BHN).

### 2.2.2 Governing Equation
$$\text{VHN} = \frac{P}{A_{\text{pyramid}}}$$

The projected area of indentation:

$$A = \frac{d^{2}}{2 \sin(136^{\circ}/2)} = \frac{d^{2}}{2 \sin 68^{\circ}} = \frac{d^{2}}{1.8544}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\text{VHN} = \frac{1.8544 \, P}{d^{2}}}$$

where **d** is the average of the two diagonals.

---

## 2.3 Rockwell Hardness Test (HR)

### 2.3.1 Apparatus & Principle
- **Two-stage loading** makes Rockwell unique:
  1. **Minor preload (10 kgf)** — seats the indenter, eliminates surface roughness.
  2. **Major load** — applied to make the indent.
  3. **Major load removed** — elastic recovery occurs; the **permanent depth** is measured.
- The dial gauge reads hardness directly: deeper indent ⇒ lower number.

### 2.3.2 Governing Equation
$$\text{HR} = E - \frac{h}{S}$$

| Parameter | Regular Rockwell | Superficial Rockwell |
|---|---|---|
| E (constant) | 100 (diamond), 130 (ball) | 100 |
| S (scale unit) | 0.002 mm | 0.001 mm |
| Major load | 60, 100, 150 kgf | 15, 30, 45 kgf |

### 2.3.3 Common Rockwell Scales

| Scale | Indenter | Major Load (kgf) | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **HRA** | Diamond cone | 60 | Very hard materials, cemented carbides |
| **HRB** | 1/16″ steel ball | 100 | Soft steels, brass, aluminum |
| **HRC** | Diamond cone | 150 | Hardened steels, tool steels (most common) |
| **HRD** | Diamond cone | 100 | Case-hardened steel |
| **HRF** | 1/16″ steel ball | 60 | Annealed brass, thin soft sheets |
| **HR15N, 30N, 45N** | Diamond cone | 15/30/45 | Superficial — thin case, nitriding |
| **HR15T, 30T, 45T** | 1/16″ ball | 15/30/45 | Superficial — thin soft sheet |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Always quote Rockwell hardness with its scale letter.** Saying "the hardness is 60" is meaningless; it must be **60 HRC** or **60 HRB**.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Test | Hardness Number Formula | Indenter | Load | Measuring Quantity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Brinell (BHN)** | $\text{BHN} = \dfrac{2P}{\pi D \bigl(D - \sqrt{D^{2}-d^{2}}\bigr)}$ | Steel/WC ball, dia. D | $K D^{2}$ | Indent diameter **d** |
| **Vickers (VHN/HV)** | $\text{VHN} = \dfrac{1.854\,P}{d^{2}}$ | Diamond pyramid, 136° | 1–100 kgf | Mean diagonal **d** |
| **Rockwell (HR)** | $\text{HR} = E - h/S$ | Diamond cone / ball | 60/100/150 kgf | Permanent depth **h** |

| Parameter | Units (SI equivalents used in modern labs) |
|---|---|
| Load P | kgf (commonly) or N (1 kgf ≈ 9.81 N) |
| Diameter / Diagonal | mm |
| Hardness Number | kgf/mm² (dimensionless in practice) |

---

## 2.5 Engineering Utility & Real-World Applications

- **Quality control** of incoming raw stock (billets, sheets) on the shop floor.
- **Heat treatment verification** — BHN/HRC/HV jump sharply after quenching, tempering, nitriding.
- **Weld integrity** — micro-Vickers traverses across weld fusion zones (HAZ hardness mapping).
- **Material selection** — gear teeth, ball bearings, cutting tools, dies.
- **Failure analysis** — comparing indentation patterns between service-exposed and virgin samples.
- **Empirical strength estimation** — for steels, $\sigma_{UTS}\text{(MPa)} \approx 3.45 \times \text{HB}$ (within ±10 % for HB < 450).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Brinell Hardness Number

**Step 1 — Setup the geometry.** A ball of diameter **D** indents a flat specimen to depth **h**. The indent is a spherical cap whose chord (the indentation diameter) is **d**.

From chord–radius geometry (Pythagoras in a vertical cross-section):

$$\left(\frac{d}{2}\right)^{2} + \bigl(R - h\bigr)^{2} = R^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Simplify.** Let $R = D/2$:

$$\frac{d^{2}}{4} + \left(\frac{D}{2} - h\right)^{2} = \frac{D^{2}}{4}$$

Expanding the square:

$$\frac{d^{2}}{4} + \frac{D^{2}}{4} - Dh + h^{2} = \frac{D^{2}}{4}$$

Cancel $\dfrac{D^{2}}{4}$ from both sides:

$$\frac{d^{2}}{4} - Dh + h^{2} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Solve for h.** Treat as a quadratic in **h**:

$$h^{2} - D\,h + \frac{d^{2}}{4} = 0$$

By the quadratic formula:

$$h = \frac{D \pm \sqrt{D^{2} - d^{2}}}{2}$$

The physically meaningful root (the *shallow* spherical cap) is:

$$h = \frac{D - \sqrt{D^{2} - d^{2}}}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Curved surface area of the spherical cap.** For a sphere of diameter D, the lateral surface area of a cap of height **h** is:

$$A_{\text{cap}} = \pi D\, h$$

Substituting **h**:

$$A_{\text{cap}} = \pi D \cdot \frac{D - \sqrt{D^{2} - d^{2}}}{2} = \frac{\pi D \bigl(D - \sqrt{D^{2}-d^{2}}\bigr)}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the definition.** Brinell Hardness Number = Load / Area:

$$\text{BHN} = \frac{P}{A_{\text{cap}}} = \frac{2P}{\pi D \bigl(D - \sqrt{D^{2} - d^{2}}\bigr)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Insight:** Because **h** depends on both **D** and **d**, BHN is independent of the ball diameter provided the geometric-similarity rule (0.25 D < d < 0.5 D) is followed — this is why different ball sizes give consistent BHN values for the same material under matched K-factor loading.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Vickers Hardness Number

**Step 1 — Indenter geometry.** A square-based diamond pyramid with face angle $\theta = 136°$ between *opposite faces*. The angle between a face and the vertical axis is $\theta/2 = 68°$.

**Step 2 — Projected area of indent.** Let **d** = mean diagonal length of the square impression. The horizontal projected area is:

$$A = \frac{d^{2}}{2}$$

Wait — for a square of side **s**, area is $s^{2}$, but here the diagonal of that square is **d**, so side $s = d/\sqrt{2}$, and:

$$A_{\text{square}} = s^{2} = \frac{d^{2}}{2}$$

This is the **projected area** on the specimen surface.

**Step 3 — Relation between face area and projected area.** The actual slanted face area $A_{\text{face}}$ relates to the projected area by:

$$A_{\text{face}} = \frac{A_{\text{projected}}}{\cos(\theta/2)} = \frac{d^{2}}{2 \cos 68°}$$

But conventionally Vickers uses the **projected area** (load divided by projected contact area) which matches the geometric definition:

$$A_{\text{projected}} = \frac{d^{2}}{2 \sin(\theta/2)} \cdot \sin(\theta/2) \cdot \cos(\theta/2) \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \frac{d^{2}}{2}$$

The accepted Vickers formula is derived from the **actual slanted pyramid face area**:

$$\boxed{\text{VHN} = \frac{2 P \sin(136°/2)}{d^{2}} = \frac{1.8544\,P}{d^{2}}}$$

where $\dfrac{1}{\sin 68°} \approx 1.054$ and $2 \times 0.8819 / 1.054 \approx 1.854$.

**Numerical anchor (used by all Vickers machines):**

$$\sin 68° = 0.9272, \quad 2 \sin 68° = 1.8544$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Rockwell Number

**Step 1 — Two-stage loading depth.** Let $h_{0}$ = depth under minor load (10 kgf). After applying major load, depth becomes $h_{1}$. After removing major load (elastic recovery), depth becomes $h$.

The *permanent* (plastic) increase in depth due to the major load is:

$$\Delta h = h - h_{0}$$

**Step 2 — Dial-gauge geometry.** The dial is calibrated so that **each 0.002 mm of permanent depth** corresponds to **one Rockwell point** (on regular scales; 0.001 mm on superficial scales). The convention is set so that:

$$\text{HR} = E - \frac{\Delta h}{S}$$

with $E = 100$ for diamond indenter, $E = 130$ for ball indenter, $S = 0.002$ mm (regular) or $S = 0.001$ mm (superficial).

**Step 3 — Worked verification.** Suppose a diamond-cone test gives $\Delta h = 0.080$ mm.

$$\text{HRC} = 100 - \frac{0.080}{0.002} = 100 - 40 = 60$$

> The result is **60 HRC** — a typical hardened tool-steel value. ✔

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (BHN)

> A Brinell test on a steel sample uses a **D = 10 mm** hardened-steel ball and a load **P = 3000 kgf**. The measured indentation diameter is **d = 4.5 mm**. Compute the BHN.

**Step 1 — Compute the geometric term.**

$$D^{2} - d^{2} = 10^{2} - 4.5^{2} = 100 - 20.25 = 79.75$$

$$\sqrt{D^{2} - d^{2}} = \sqrt{79.75} = 8.9303 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the denominator.**

$$D - \sqrt{D^{2} - d^{2}} = 10 - 8.9303 = 1.0697 \text{ mm}$$

$$\pi \cdot D \cdot (\cdots) = \pi \times 10 \times 1.0697 = 33.605 \text{ mm}^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Apply formula.**

$$\text{BHN} = \frac{2 \times 3000}{33.605} = \frac{6000}{33.605} \approx 178.5 \text{ kgf/mm}^{2}$$

> ✔ **BHN ≈ 179** — consistent with a medium-carbon annealed steel.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (VHN)

> A Vickers test uses load **P = 30 kgf**. The two diagonals are measured as **d₁ = 0.420 mm** and **d₂ = 0.428 mm**. Compute VHN.

**Step 1 — Mean diagonal.**

$$\bar{d} = \frac{0.420 + 0.428}{2} = 0.424 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Apply formula.**

$$\text{VHN} = \frac{1.854 \times 30}{(0.424)^{2}} = \frac{55.62}{0.1798} \approx 309.3 \text{ kgf/mm}^{2}$$

> ✔ **VHN ≈ 309** — typical of a hardened low-alloy steel.

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (Rockwell)

> A Rockwell C test gives a permanent depth increment **Δh = 0.064 mm**. Compute the HRC value.

**Step 1 — Apply formula (diamond, regular scale).**

$$\text{HRC} = 100 - \frac{0.064}{0.002} = 100 - 32 = 68$$

> ✔ **68 HRC** — typical of a fully hardened tool steel (e.g., D2 after tempering).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Flow: Generic Hardness Test Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start: Receive Specimen] --> surfA[Prepare Surface: Grind and Polish]
    surfA --> selectA{Select Test Type}
    selectA --> brinellA[Brinell: Steel or WC Ball]
    selectA --> vickersA[Vickers: Diamond Pyramid 136 deg]
    selectA --> rockwellA[Rockwell: Diamond Cone or Steel Ball]
    brinellA --> loadA1[Apply Load P]
    vickersA --> loadA1
    rockwellA --> minorA[Apply Minor Load 10 kgf]
    minorA --> majorA[Apply Major Load]
    loadA1 --> dwellA[Dwell 10 to 15 sec]
    majorA --> dwellA
    dwellA --> unloadA[Remove Load]
    unloadA --> measureA{Measure Indentation}
    brinellA --> measureA
    vickersA --> measureA
    measureA --> calcA[Compute Hardness Number]
    rockwellA --> calcA
    calcA --> reportA[Report Value with Scale]
    reportA --> stopA[End]
```

## 4.2 Indenter Geometry Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BALL["Brinell Indenter"]
        b1["Hardened Steel or Tungsten Carbide"]
        b2["Diameter D: 10 mm or 5 mm or 2.5 mm"]
    end
    subgraph DIAMOND_V["Vickers Indenter"]
        v1["Square Diamond Pyramid"]
        v2["Face Angle: 136 degrees"]
        v3["Produces Square Indent"]
    end
    subgraph CONE_BALL["Rockwell Indenters"]
        r1["Diamond Cone (Brale)"]
        r2["120 degree cone, rounded tip"]
        r3["Steel Ball: 1/16 inch or 1/8 inch"]
    end
    BALL --> COMMON["Specimen Surface"]
    DIAMOND_V --> COMMON
    CONE_BALL --> COMMON
```

## 4.3 Brinell Test — Sequential Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA1[Place Specimen on Anvil] --> stepA2[Raise Specimen to Contact Ball]
    stepA2 --> stepA3[Apply Selected Load Smoothly]
    stepA3 --> stepA4[Maintain Load for Dwell Time]
    stepA4 --> stepA5[Remove Load Lower Anvil]
    stepA5 --> stepA6[Measure Indent Diameter d at Two Perpendicular Directions]
    stepA6 --> stepA7{Valid Range Check: 0.25 D less than d less than 0.5 D}
    stepA7 -->|Yes| stepA8[Compute BHN via Formula]
    stepA7 -->|No| stepA9[Repeat with Correct Load or Ball]
    stepA8 --> stepA10[Record BHN Value]
```

## 4.4 Rockwell Test — Two-Stage Loading Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepB1[Place Specimen on Anvil] --> stepB2[Raise to Contact Indenter]
    stepB2 --> stepB3[Apply Minor Load 10 kgf]
    stepB3 --> stepB4[Set Dial to Zero Reference]
    stepB4 --> stepB5[Apply Major Load: 60 100 or 150 kgf]
    stepB5 --> stepB6[Dwell 2 to 6 seconds]
    stepB6 --> stepB7[Remove Major Load Only]
    stepB7 --> stepB8[Read Dial Directly: HR Value]
    stepB8 --> stepB9[Report Hardness with Scale Letter]
```

## 4.5 Comparison Matrix: Brinell vs Vickers vs Rockwell

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    compareA[Choose Test Based on Requirements]
    compareA -->|Coarse rough bulk| branchA[Use Brinell]
    compareA -->|All hardness thin films micro| branchB[Use Vickers]
    compareA -->|Fast shop floor readout| branchC[Use Rockwell]
    branchA --> outA[Ball Indenter Load 3000 kgf]
    branchB --> outB[Diamond Pyramid 136 deg]
    branchC --> outC[Diamond Cone or Ball Direct Readout]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Define hardness. List the three indentation hardness tests covered in the KTU 2024 syllabus and state one distinguishing feature of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Hardness is the resistance of a material to localized plastic deformation caused by mechanical indentation, scratching, or abrasion.

**Three tests (1.5 marks, 0.5 each):**
1. **Brinell** — uses a hardened steel / tungsten-carbide ball; suitable for coarse, rough materials.
2. **Vickers** — uses a square diamond pyramid with 136° face angle; suitable for all materials and hardness ranges.
3. **Rockwell** — uses a diamond cone or steel ball with two-stage loading; gives a direct dial readout, fastest industrial test.

**Distinguishing application (0.5 mark):**
Rockwell is preferred for shop-floor production; Vickers for micro-hardness of thin layers; Brinell for cast/forged components.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**State the standard loads and indenter used in the Brinell hardness test for (a) steel, (b) copper, and (c) soft bearing alloys.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Material | K (kgf/mm²) | Load for D = 10 mm |
|---|---|---|
| (a) Steel | 30 | **3000 kgf** (with 10 mm steel or WC ball) |
| (b) Copper | 10 | **1000 kgf** (with 10 mm ball) |
| (c) Soft bearing alloys | 2.5 | **250 kgf** (with 10 mm ball) |

(1 mark per row). The relationship $P = K D^{2}$ must be mentioned for full marks.

---

## PART B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks each)

### ▶ Question A — CHOICE 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the **Brinell hardness test**. Derive the expression for the Brinell hardness number. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Brinell test on a copper specimen uses a 10 mm steel ball and a load of 1000 kgf. The indentation diameter measured is **3.5 mm**. Compute the BHN and comment on the validity of the test. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a) [7 marks]

**Sketch + description (3 marks):**
- Indenter: hardened steel / tungsten-carbide ball of diameter D (10 mm standard).
- Load P applied perpendicular to the prepared specimen for a dwell time (15–30 s).
- Indent diameter d measured in two perpendicular directions with a microscope; mean is taken.

**Derivation (4 marks):**
Let R = D/2, h = depth of indent, d = chord (indent diameter).

Geometry gives:
$$h = \frac{D - \sqrt{D^{2} - d^{2}}}{2}$$

Curved spherical-cap area:
$$A = \pi D h = \frac{\pi D \bigl(D - \sqrt{D^{2}-d^{2}}\bigr)}{2}$$

Hardness = Load / Area:
$$\boxed{\text{BHN} = \frac{2P}{\pi D\bigl(D - \sqrt{D^{2}-d^{2}}\bigr)}}$$

Mention the load rule $P = K D^{2}$ and the validity range $0.25 D < d < 0.5 D$ for full marks.

#### Model Solution — (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Geometric term (2 marks):**
$$D^{2} - d^{2} = 100 - 12.25 = 87.75$$
$$\sqrt{87.75} = 9.367 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Denominator (1 mark):**
$$\pi D \bigl(D - \sqrt{D^{2}-d^{2}}\bigr) = \pi \times 10 \times (10 - 9.367) = \pi \times 6.33 = 19.88 \text{ mm}^{2}$$

**Step 3 — BHN (2 marks):**
$$\text{BHN} = \frac{2 \times 1000}{19.88} \approx 100.6 \text{ kgf/mm}^{2}$$

**Step 4 — Validity check (2 marks):**
Validity range requires $0.25 D < d < 0.5 D$ ⇒ $2.5 < d < 5.0$ mm.
Since $d = 3.5$ mm satisfies this, the test is **valid**.

**Comment:** BHN ≈ 100 — consistent with an annealed copper alloy.

---

### ▶ Question B — CHOICE 2 [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a)** Explain the **Vickers hardness test** with its principle, indenter, formula derivation, and advantages over Brinell. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Vickers test gives mean diagonal $\bar{d} = 0.35$ mm under load $P = 20$ kgf. Compute VHN. If the same material is tested by Rockwell C and the dial reads **52 HRC**, what is the permanent depth increment? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a) [7 marks]

**Principle (1.5 marks):** Load P is applied through a square-based diamond pyramid (face angle 136°). The two diagonals of the square indent are measured under a microscope; mean diagonal $\bar{d}$ is computed.

**Indenter (1 mark):** Diamond pyramid, included face angle **136°**; chosen so that HV ≈ HB in the medium-hardness range.

**Derivation (3 marks):**
Slanted face area: $A_{\text{face}} = \dfrac{d^{2}}{2 \sin 68°}$
Since $\text{VHN} = P / A_{\text{face}}$:
$$\text{VHN} = \frac{2 P \sin 68°}{d^{2}} = \frac{1.854\,P}{d^{2}}$$

**Advantages over Brinell (1.5 marks):**
1. One indenter covers the entire hardness range (very soft to very hard).
2. Suitable for micro-hardness testing (loads as low as 10 g).
3. Accurate diagonal measurement eliminates edge errors.
4. Indent is small and often non-destructive on finished parts.

#### Model Solution — (b) [7 marks]

**Part 1 — VHN calculation (3 marks):**
$$\text{VHN} = \frac{1.854 \times 20}{(0.35)^{2}} = \frac{37.08}{0.1225} \approx 302.7 \text{ kgf/mm}^{2}$$

**Part 2 — Permanent depth from 52 HRC (4 marks):**
For Rockwell C, $E = 100$, $S = 0.002$ mm.

Using $\text{HRC} = E - \dfrac{\Delta h}{S}$:

$$52 = 100 - \frac{\Delta h}{0.002}$$

$$\frac{\Delta h}{0.002} = 48$$

$$\Delta h = 48 \times 0.002 = 0.096 \text{ mm}$$

**Permanent depth increment = 0.096 mm** ✔

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Losers**
> 1. **Forgetting the scale letter** in Rockwell: writing "60" instead of "60 HRC" → lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Using P in Newtons without conversion** to kgf in BHN/VHN → answer off by 9.81× → 0 marks for numerical.
> 3. **Not checking the validity range** $0.25 D < d < 0.5 D$ for Brinell → lose the validity comment marks.
> 4. **Confusing projected area with slanted face area** in Vickers derivation → formula written as $2P/d^{2}$ instead of $1.854 P/d^{2}$ → lose 2 marks.
> 5. **Reporting $\Delta h$ instead of HR or vice versa** → loses marks in the two-stage Rockwell question.
> 6. **Mixing up $E = 100$ (diamond) and $E = 130$ (ball)** in Rockwell derivation → arithmetically wrong.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Hardness = resistance to localized plastic deformation** — empirical, not fundamental.
- **Brinell** uses a ball of diameter **D**; formula is $\text{BHN} = \dfrac{2P}{\pi D(D - \sqrt{D^{2}-d^{2}})}$; valid only when $0.25 D < d < 0.5 D$.
- **Load rule for Brinell:** $P = K D^{2}$ with $K = 30$ (steel), $10$ (Cu/Al), $2.5$ (very soft).
- **Vickers** uses a 136° diamond pyramid; formula is $\text{VHN} = \dfrac{1.854\,P}{d^{2}}$ where $d$ is the mean diagonal.
- Vickers is **universal** — works on all materials and supports micro-hardness (loads down to 10 g).
- **Rockwell** uses **two-stage loading** (10 kgf minor + 60/100/150 kgf major); HR is read directly off the dial.
- For diamond cone: $E = 100$; for steel ball: $E = 130$. Regular scale: $S = 0.002$ mm; superficial scale: $S = 0.001$ mm.
- **Always append the scale letter:** HRC, HRB, HRA, HR15N, HR30T, etc.
- **Common scale selection:**
  - Soft brass / aluminum → **HRB**
  - Hardened tool steel → **HRC**
  - Carbides, very hard → **HRA**
  - Thin case / nitrided layer → **HR15N / HR30N**
- **Empirical link:** For steels with HB < 450, $\sigma_{UTS}\text{(MPa)} \approx 3.45 \cdot \text{HB}$.
- **Specimen preparation:** Surface must be flat, smooth, free from oxide and decarburized layers; minimum thickness ≥ 10× indent depth (BHN) or 1.5× diagonal (Vickers).
- **Minimum spacing between indents:** ≥ 3× indent diameter (BHN) or 3× diagonal (Vickers), ≥ 3× indent diameter from the edge.
- **Brinell → Vickers → Rockwell** roughly correlate but are NOT identical — always use the same test for comparison series.
- **KTU viva favourites:** "Why 136°?", "Why minor load in Rockwell?", "What if d > 0.5 D in Brinell?", "Difference between projected and contact area in Vickers?".
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determine the Torsional Rigidity of Mild Steel, Copper, and Brass Rods
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUL407 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determine the Torsional Rigidity of Mild Steel, Copper, and Brass Rods

# Module Experiment 2: Torsional Rigidity of Mild Steel, Copper, and Brass Rods

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Torsional Rigidity** of a circular shaft is defined as the product of the **Modulus of Rigidity (G)** and the **Polar Moment of Inertia (J)** of the cross-section about its longitudinal axis. It quantifies the inherent ability of a structural member to resist angular deformation (twisting) when subjected to an external twisting moment (torque) about its longitudinal axis.

Mathematically, the torsional rigidity is expressed as:

$$GJ = \frac{T \cdot L}{\theta}$$

where:
- $T$ = Applied twisting moment (Torque) in **N·m**
- $L$ = Effective gauge length of the specimen in **m**
- $\theta$ = Angle of twist in **radians**
- $G$ = Modulus of Rigidity in **N/m²** or **Pa**
- $J$ = Polar Moment of Inertia of the cross-section in **m⁴**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The torsional rigidity $GJ$ is a geometric-material property of the shaft. It must NOT be confused with the **Modulus of Rigidity (G)** alone, which is purely a material property. The torsional rigidity incorporates both material behavior ($G$) and geometric resistance ($J$).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are holding a **chocolate bar** at both ends and twisting the two ends in opposite directions, just like wringing out a wet towel. 

- If the chocolate bar is **thin**, it twists easily and snaps quickly → **Low Torsional Rigidity**.
- If the chocolate bar is **thick**, it resists the twisting motion → **High Torsional Rigidity**.
- If the chocolate is made of **soft material (chocolate itself)**, it twists easily → **Low Modulus of Rigidity (G)**.
- If the chocolate is made of **hard material (toffee-like)**, it resists twisting → **High Modulus of Rigidity (G)**.

Thus, **Torsional Rigidity = Material Resistance (G) × Geometric Resistance (J)**. A drill bit made of hardened steel can transmit enormous torque without twisting because it has both a high $G$ value and a substantial $J$ due to its diameter.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Significance:** Torsional rigidity is critical in designing drive shafts of automobiles, propeller shafts of ships, turbine rotors in power plants, and even the tiny twisted wires in robotic surgical instruments. Without adequate torsional rigidity, these components would undergo excessive twisting, leading to catastrophic failure.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Theory of Torsion in Circular Shafts

When a circular shaft of uniform cross-section is subjected to a torque $T$ about its longitudinal axis, the following fundamental relationships govern its mechanical behavior:

### 2.1.1 Assumptions of Pure Torsion Theory
- The shaft material is **homogeneous, isotropic, and linearly elastic** (obeys Hooke's Law in shear).
- The cross-section remains **plane and circular** after twisting (no warping).
- **Radial lines remain radial and straight**; only their angular orientation changes.
- The twist is uniform along the gauge length (pure torsion).

### 2.1.2 Shear Stress Distribution

The shear stress at any radial distance $r$ from the center of the shaft is given by:

$$\tau = \frac{T \cdot r}{J}$$

The **maximum shear stress** occurs at the outer surface (where $r = R$, the radius):

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{T \cdot R}{J} = \frac{16 T}{\pi d^3}$$

where $d$ is the diameter of the solid circular shaft.

### 2.1.3 Angle of Twist

The total angle of twist $\theta$ over a gauge length $L$ is:

$$\theta = \frac{T \cdot L}{G \cdot J}$$

### 2.1.4 Polar Moment of Inertia (Solid Shaft)

For a solid circular cross-section of diameter $d$:

$$J = \frac{\pi d^4}{32}$$

### 2.1.5 Polar Moment of Inertia (Hollow Shaft)

For a hollow circular cross-section with outer diameter $D$ and inner diameter $d$:

$$J = \frac{\pi \left(D^4 - d^4\right)}{32}$$

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units (SI) | KTU Exam Tip |
|---|-----------|---------|------------|--------------|
| 1 | Torsional Rigidity | $GJ = \dfrac{T \cdot L}{\theta}$ | N·m²/rad | Primary quantity to find |
| 2 | Modulus of Rigidity | $G = \dfrac{T \cdot L}{J \cdot \theta}$ | N/m² or Pa | Compare with standard values |
| 3 | Polar Moment of Inertia (Solid) | $J = \dfrac{\pi d^4}{32}$ | m⁴ | Convert diameter to meters |
| 4 | Max Shear Stress | $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{16 T}{\pi d^3}$ | N/m² or Pa | Occurs at outer surface |
| 5 | Angle of Twist (radians) | $\theta = \dfrac{\theta^\circ \cdot \pi}{180}$ | rad | Always convert degrees to radians |
| 6 | Applied Torque | $T = W \cdot r$ | N·m | From dead weights on pulley |
| 7 | Hollow Shaft Polar Moment | $J = \dfrac{\pi \left(D^4 - d^4\right)}{32}$ | m⁴ | Only if specimen is hollow |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unit Conversion Vigilance:** Diameter $d$ must be in **meters (m)** before substituting into the formula. A common KTU board exam pitfall is using $d$ in mm, which gives erroneous values of $J$ that are $10^{-12}$ times too small. Always convert: $1 \text{ mm} = 10^{-3} \text{ m}$.

## 2.3 Experimental Principle

The experiment uses a **Torsion Testing Machine** (such as the Amsler-type or a custom-built dead-weight torsion apparatus). The principle is:

1. One end of the specimen is rigidly fixed in a fixed chuck.
2. The other end is gripped in a rotating chuck connected to a **pulley-and-weight system** or a **moment arm**.
3. Known torques are applied incrementally by adding dead weights on a calibrated moment arm.
4. The resulting angle of twist is measured using a **dial gauge** or **vernier scale** attached to the rotating end.
5. A graph of **Torque (T) vs. Angle of Twist (θ)** is plotted; the slope gives $\dfrac{T}{\theta}$.
6. Since $GJ = \dfrac{T \cdot L}{\theta}$, the slope multiplied by gauge length $L$ directly yields **Torsional Rigidity $GJ$**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry:** Design of drive shafts and axle shafts.
- **Aerospace:** Helicopter rotor shafts, control rod torsional stiffness.
- **Civil Engineering:** Torsional analysis of beams in earthquake-resistant structures.
- **Power Transmission:** Design of torque tubes, couplings, and torsion springs.
- **Manufacturing:** Calibration of torque wrenches and verification of material properties.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Angle of Twist Formula

### Step 1: Consider an Elementary Slice

Take an infinitesimal cylindrical slice of the shaft of thickness $dx$ at a distance $x$ from the fixed end. The cross-section has radius $R$ and diameter $d$.

Let the shear strain at radius $r$ be $\gamma$. From the geometry of twist (a radial line rotates through an angle $d\theta$):

$$\gamma = r \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dx}$$

### Step 2: Apply Hooke's Law in Shear

The shear stress at radius $r$ is:

$$\tau = G \cdot \gamma = G \cdot r \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dx}$$

### Step 3: Compute the Internal Torque

The internal resisting torque $T$ equals the integral of shear stress times the moment arm over the cross-sectional area:

$$T = \int_A \tau \cdot r \, dA$$

Substituting $\tau$:

$$T = \int_A G \cdot r \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dx} \cdot r \, dA = G \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dx} \int_A r^2 \, dA$$

### Step 4: Recognize the Polar Moment of Inertia

The integral $\int_A r^2 \, dA$ is, by definition, the **Polar Moment of Inertia** $J$:

$$J = \int_A r^2 \, dA$$

For a solid circular cross-section:

$$\begin{aligned} J &= \int_0^R r^2 \cdot (2\pi r) \, dr \\ &= 2\pi \int_0^R r^3 \, dr \\ &= 2\pi \cdot \frac{R^4}{4} \\ &= \frac{\pi R^4}{2} \end{aligned}$$

Substituting $R = \dfrac{d}{2}$:

$$\begin{aligned} J &= \frac{\pi}{2} \cdot \left(\frac{d}{2}\right)^4 \\ &= \frac{\pi}{2} \cdot \frac{d^4}{16} \\ &= \frac{\pi d^4}{32} \end{aligned}$$

### Step 5: Derive the Angle of Twist Equation

From the equation $T = G \cdot \dfrac{d\theta}{dx} \cdot J$:

$$\frac{d\theta}{dx} = \frac{T}{G \cdot J}$$

Integrating over the entire length $L$:

$$\theta = \int_0^L \frac{T}{G \cdot J} \, dx = \frac{T \cdot L}{G \cdot J}$$

### Step 6: Solve for Torsional Rigidity

Rearranging for $GJ$:

$$\boxed{GJ = \frac{T \cdot L}{\theta}}$$

This is the master equation used in the experiment.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Worked Numerical Example

**Problem (KTU Style):** A mild steel rod of diameter **20 mm** and gauge length **300 mm** is subjected to a torque of **30 N·m**. The angle of twist observed is **2.5°**. Calculate:
(a) The polar moment of inertia $J$
(b) The torsional rigidity $GJ$
(c) The modulus of rigidity $G$
(d) The maximum shear stress $\tau_{max}$

### Part (a): Polar Moment of Inertia

**Step 1:** Convert diameter to meters.

$$d = 20 \text{ mm} = 20 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 0.020 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into the formula.

$$\begin{aligned} J &= \frac{\pi d^4}{32} \\ &= \frac{\pi \times (0.020)^4}{32} \\ &= \frac{\pi \times 1.6 \times 10^{-7}}{32} \\ &= \frac{5.0265 \times 10^{-7}}{32} \\ &= 1.5708 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^4 \end{aligned}$$

**Result:** $J = 1.5708 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^4$

### Part (b): Torsional Rigidity

**Step 1:** Convert angle of twist to radians.

$$\begin{aligned} \theta &= 2.5^\circ \times \frac{\pi}{180} \\ &= 2.5 \times 0.017453 \\ &= 0.04363 \text{ rad} \end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into the formula.

$$\begin{aligned} GJ &= \frac{T \cdot L}{\theta} \\ &= \frac{30 \times 0.300}{0.04363} \\ &= \frac{9.0}{0.04363} \\ &= 206.28 \text{ N·m}^2 \end{aligned}$$

**Result:** $GJ = 206.28 \text{ N·m}^2$

### Part (c): Modulus of Rigidity

$$\begin{aligned} G &= \frac{GJ}{J} \\ &= \frac{206.28}{1.5708 \times 10^{-8}} \\ &= 1.3134 \times 10^{10} \text{ N/m}^2 \\ &\approx 13.13 \text{ GPa} \end{aligned}$$

**Result:** $G = 13.13 \text{ GPa}$

### Part (d): Maximum Shear Stress

$$\begin{aligned} \tau_{max} &= \frac{16 T}{\pi d^3} \\ &= \frac{16 \times 30}{\pi \times (0.020)^3} \\ &= \frac{480}{\pi \times 8.0 \times 10^{-6}} \\ &= \frac{480}{2.513 \times 10^{-5}} \\ &= 1.9099 \times 10^{7} \text{ N/m}^2 \\ &\approx 19.10 \text{ MPa} \end{aligned}$$

**Result:** $\tau_{max} \approx 19.10 \text{ MPa}$

## 3.3 Comparative Analysis Matrix for the Three Materials

| Property | Mild Steel | Copper | Brass |
|----------|------------|--------|-------|
| Standard $G$ (GPa) | **75 – 80** | **40 – 45** | **35 – 40** |
| Density (kg/m³) | 7850 | 8960 | 8500 – 8700 |
| Color | Grey-black | Reddish | Yellow-gold |
| Typical Behavior | Stiffer, higher rigidity | Moderate rigidity | Least rigid (generally) |
| Expected Rank (High → Low) | 1st | 2nd | 3rd |

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** Mild steel should exhibit the **highest torsional rigidity**, followed by copper and then brass. This is because the modulus of rigidity $G$ of mild steel is roughly twice that of copper/brass.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Torsion Testing Machine Setup

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Fixed Chuck] --> B[Mild Steel Specimen]
    A2[Fixed Chuck] --> C[Copper Specimen]
    A3[Fixed Chuck] --> D[Brass Specimen]
    B --> E[Rotating Chuck]
    C --> E
    D --> E
    E --> F[Pulley Wheel]
    F --> G[Steel Wire with Dead Weights]
    G --> H[Weight Hanger]
    E --> I[Dial Gauge for Angle Measurement]
    E --> J[Pointer on Angular Scale]
    I --> K[Record theta for each T]
    K --> L[Plot T vs theta Graph]
    L --> M[Find Slope T by theta]
    M --> N[Calculate GJ using T times L by theta]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT[INPUT SYSTEM]
        I1[Dead Weights in Newtons]
        I2[Pulley Radius in meters]
        I3[Specimen Diameter d in mm]
        I4[Gauge Length L in mm]
    end

    subgraph PROCESS[PROCESSING CORE]
        P1[Compute Torque T equals W times r]
        P2[Measure Angle of Twist theta in degrees]
        P3[Convert theta to radians]
        P4[Convert d and L to SI units]
    end

    subgraph COMPUTE[COMPUTATIONAL MODULE]
        C1[Calculate Polar Moment J equals pi d4 by 32]
        C2[Calculate Torsional Rigidity GJ equals T L by theta]
        C3[Calculate Modulus G equals GJ by J]
        C4[Calculate Max Shear Stress tau max equals 16 T by pi d3]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[OUTPUT DISPLAY]
        O1[Tabulated Values of GJ]
        O2[Bar Chart Comparison of G]
        O3[T versus theta Graph]
        O4[Result Validation against Standards]
    end

    I1 --> P1
    I2 --> P1
    I3 --> P4
    I4 --> P4
    P1 --> C1
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> C2
    P4 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    P1 --> C4
    C4 --> OUTPUT
    C3 --> OUTPUT
    C2 --> O1
    C3 --> O2
    P2 --> O3
    O1 --> O4
```

## 4.3 Schematic Layout of the Torsion Testing Apparatus

```
       ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────┐
       │                                              │
       │   FIXED END            SPECIMEN              │
       │   ┌─────┐          ═══════════════          │
       │   │CHUCK│══════════│   ROD (d, L) │═════════│════ ROTATING END
       │   └─────┘          ═══════════════          │     ┌─────────┐
       │                                              │     │ DIAL    │
       │                                              │     │ GAUGE   │
       │                                              │     └─────────┘
       │                                              │          │
       │                                              │          ▼
       │                                              │     ┌─────────┐
       │                                              │     │ ANGULAR │
       │                                              │     │ SCALE   │
       │                                              │     └─────────┘
       │                                              │          │
       │                                              │          ▼
       │                                              │     ┌─────────┐
       │                                              │     │ PULLEY  │
       │                                              │     └─────────┘
       │                                              │          │
       │                                              │     DEAD │ WEIGHTS
       └──────────────────────────────────────────────┘          ▼
                                                          ╔═══════╗
                                                          ║   W   ║
                                                          ╚═══════╝
```

## 4.4 Procedure Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Measure diameter d and length L of specimen] --> S2[Step 2: Fix specimen between chucks]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Set dial gauge to zero]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Apply initial small torque to remove slack]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Apply known weight W gradually]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Record angle of twist theta]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Increase load incrementally]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8: Repeat for at least 5 to 6 readings]
    S8 --> S9[Step 9: Plot T versus theta graph]
    S9 --> S10[Step 10: Find slope of best fit line]
    S10 --> S11[Step 11: Calculate GJ using slope times L]
    S11 --> S12[Step 12: Repeat for Copper and Brass]
    S12 --> S13[Step 13: Compare values and tabulate results]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: Define torsional rigidity. State its SI unit.**

**Model Answer:**
Torsional rigidity of a shaft is defined as the product of the Modulus of Rigidity ($G$) and the Polar Moment of Inertia ($J$) of the cross-section. It represents the resistance of the shaft to twisting deformation under an applied torque.

$$\text{Torsional Rigidity} = GJ = \frac{T \cdot L}{\theta}$$

**SI Unit:** N·m²/rad (Newton-meter squared per radian)

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: Differentiate between modulus of rigidity and torsional rigidity.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Modulus of Rigidity ($G$) | Torsional Rigidity ($GJ$) |
|-----------|---------------------------|---------------------------|
| Definition | Ratio of shear stress to shear strain within elastic limit | Product of $G$ and polar moment of inertia $J$ |
| Depends on | Material only | Both material and geometry |
| SI Unit | N/m² or Pa | N·m²/rad |
| Nature | Material property | Structural/Geometric property |

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each - Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: (a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the experimental setup to determine the torsional rigidity of a mild steel rod. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A solid circular shaft of diameter 25 mm and length 400 mm is subjected to a torque of 50 N·m. If the modulus of rigidity of the material is 80 GPa, determine: (i) the angle of twist in degrees, and (ii) the maximum shear stress developed in the shaft. **(7 marks)**

#### Solution to Part (a):

**Apparatus Required:**
1. Torsion Testing Machine with fixed and rotating chucks
2. Mild steel specimen of standard dimensions
3. Dead weights and hanger
4. Pulley with calibrated radius
5. Dial gauge / vernier scale for angle measurement
6. Vernier caliper and measuring tape

**Experimental Procedure:**
1. Measure the diameter $d$ of the specimen at three different locations using a vernier caliper; take the average.
2. Measure the gauge length $L$ between the two chucks.
3. Fix the specimen firmly in the torsion testing machine between the fixed chuck and the rotating chuck.
4. Attach the pulley and the dial gauge to the rotating end.
5. Apply a small initial torque to remove any slackness in the system; set the dial gauge to zero.
6. Add dead weights $W$ in increments on the hanger. The torque is calculated as $T = W \times r$ (where $r$ is the pulley radius).
7. Note the corresponding angle of twist $\theta$ from the dial gauge.
8. Take at least 5-6 readings by progressively increasing the load.
9. Plot a graph of Torque $T$ (y-axis) versus Angle of Twist $\theta$ (x-axis). The graph should be a straight line.
10. The slope of this line gives $\dfrac{T}{\theta}$, from which torsional rigidity is calculated as:

$$GJ = \frac{T \cdot L}{\theta} = \text{Slope} \times L$$

#### Solution to Part (b):

**Given Data:**
- $d = 25 \text{ mm} = 0.025 \text{ m}$
- $L = 400 \text{ mm} = 0.4 \text{ m}$
- $T = 50 \text{ N·m}$
- $G = 80 \text{ GPa} = 80 \times 10^9 \text{ N/m}^2$

**Step 1: Calculate Polar Moment of Inertia**

$$\begin{aligned} J &= \frac{\pi d^4}{32} \\ &= \frac{\pi \times (0.025)^4}{32} \\ &= \frac{\pi \times 3.906 \times 10^{-7}}{32} \\ &= 3.834 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^4 \end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Calculate Torsional Rigidity**

$$\begin{aligned} GJ &= 80 \times 10^9 \times 3.834 \times 10^{-8} \\ &= 3067.96 \text{ N·m}^2 \end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Calculate Angle of Twist (in radians)**

$$\begin{aligned} \theta &= \frac{T \cdot L}{G \cdot J} \\ &= \frac{50 \times 0.4}{3067.96} \\ &= 0.006519 \text{ rad} \end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Convert to Degrees**

$$\begin{aligned} \theta^\circ &= 0.006519 \times \frac{180}{\pi} \\ &= 0.3735^\circ \end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: Calculate Maximum Shear Stress**

$$\begin{aligned} \tau_{max} &= \frac{16 T}{\pi d^3} \\ &= \frac{16 \times 50}{\pi \times (0.025)^3} \\ &= \frac{800}{\pi \times 1.5625 \times 10^{-5}} \\ &= \frac{800}{4.909 \times 10^{-5}} \\ &= 1.6297 \times 10^7 \text{ N/m}^2 \\ &\approx 16.30 \text{ MPa} \end{aligned}$$

**Final Answers:** (i) $\theta = 0.3735^\circ$, (ii) $\tau_{max} = 16.30 \text{ MPa}$

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: (a)** Derive the expression for the angle of twist of a circular shaft subjected to a torque. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In an experiment to determine the torsional rigidity of a brass rod, the following observations were recorded:

| Load (N) | Angle of Twist (°) |
|----------|---------------------|
| 50 | 1.2 |
| 100 | 2.4 |
| 150 | 3.6 |
| 200 | 4.8 |
| 250 | 6.0 |

The pulley radius is 100 mm and the gauge length is 350 mm. Calculate: (i) The torsional rigidity, and (ii) The modulus of rigidity if the rod diameter is 18 mm. **(7 marks)**

#### Solution to Part (a):

**Detailed Derivation:**

Consider a solid circular shaft of radius $R$ and length $L$ fixed at one end and free at the other. Let a torque $T$ be applied at the free end.

**Step 1:** Consider an elementary cylindrical slice of thickness $dx$ at a distance $x$ from the fixed end.

**Step 2:** Let the slice twist through an infinitesimal angle $d\theta$. Consider a fiber at radius $r$ from the axis. The fiber's top surface moves by an arc length $r \cdot d\theta$ while its bottom remains stationary (over thickness $dx$).

**Step 3:** The shear strain $\gamma$ at radius $r$ is:

$$\gamma = \frac{r \cdot d\theta}{dx}$$

**Step 4:** By Hooke's Law in shear:

$$\tau = G \cdot \gamma = G \cdot r \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dx}$$

**Step 5:** The resisting torque from this stress distribution is:

$$T = \int_A \tau \cdot r \, dA = \int_A G \cdot r \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dx} \cdot r \, dA$$

**Step 6:** Since $\dfrac{d\theta}{dx}$ is constant across the cross-section:

$$T = G \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dx} \int_A r^2 \, dA = G \cdot J \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dx}$$

**Step 7:** Rearranging:

$$\frac{d\theta}{dx} = \frac{T}{G \cdot J}$$

**Step 8:** Integrating over the entire length $L$:

$$\theta = \int_0^L \frac{T}{G \cdot J} \, dx = \frac{T \cdot L}{G \cdot J}$$

$$\boxed{\theta = \frac{T \cdot L}{G \cdot J}}$$

#### Solution to Part (b):

**Given Data:**
- Pulley radius $r = 100 \text{ mm} = 0.1 \text{ m}$
- Gauge length $L = 350 \text{ mm} = 0.35 \text{ m}$
- Rod diameter $d = 18 \text{ mm} = 0.018 \text{ m}$

**Step 1: Calculate Torque for Each Load**

The torque $T = W \times r$ where $r$ is the pulley radius:

$$\begin{aligned} T_1 &= 50 \times 0.1 = 5 \text{ N·m} \\ T_2 &= 100 \times 0.1 = 10 \text{ N·m} \\ T_3 &= 150 \times 0.1 = 15 \text{ N·m} \\ T_4 &= 200 \times 0.1 = 20 \text{ N·m} \\ T_5 &= 250 \times 0.1 = 25 \text{ N·m} \end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Convert Angles to Radians**

$$\begin{aligned} \theta_1 &= 1.2 \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 0.02094 \text{ rad} \\ \theta_2 &= 2.4 \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 0.04189 \text{ rad} \\ \theta_3 &= 3.6 \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 0.06283 \text{ rad} \\ \theta_4 &= 4.8 \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 0.08378 \text{ rad} \\ \theta_5 &= 6.0 \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 0.10472 \text{ rad} \end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Calculate T/θ Ratio**

$$\begin{aligned} \left(\frac{T}{\theta}\right)_1 &= \frac{5}{0.02094} = 238.73 \text{ N·m/rad} \\ \left(\frac{T}{\theta}\right)_2 &= \frac{10}{0.04189} = 238.73 \text{ N·m/rad} \\ \left(\frac{T}{\theta}\right)_3 &= \frac{15}{0.06283} = 238.73 \text{ N·m/rad} \\ \left(\frac{T}{\theta}\right)_4 &= \frac{20}{0.08378} = 238.73 \text{ N·m/rad} \\ \left(\frac{T}{\theta}\right)_5 &= \frac{25}{0.10472} = 238.73 \text{ N·m/rad} \end{aligned}$$

The slope is consistent: $\dfrac{T}{\theta} = 238.73 \text{ N·m/rad}$

**Step 4: Calculate Torsional Rigidity**

$$\begin{aligned} GJ &= \frac{T \cdot L}{\theta} = 238.73 \times 0.35 \\ &= 83.56 \text{ N·m}^2 \end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: Calculate Polar Moment of Inertia**

$$\begin{aligned} J &= \frac{\pi d^4}{32} \\ &= \frac{\pi \times (0.018)^4}{32} \\ &= \frac{\pi \times 1.04976 \times 10^{-7}}{32} \\ &= 1.0306 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^4 \end{aligned}$$

**Step 6: Calculate Modulus of Rigidity**

$$\begin{aligned} G &= \frac{GJ}{J} \\ &= \frac{83.56}{1.0306 \times 10^{-8}} \\ &= 8.108 \times 10^9 \text{ N/m}^2 \\ &\approx 8.11 \text{ GPa} \end{aligned}$$

**Final Answers:** (i) $GJ = 83.56 \text{ N·m}^2$, (ii) $G = 8.11 \text{ GPa}$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch (Loses 2-3 marks):** Students frequently forget to convert diameter from mm to meters. A 25 mm diameter entered as 25 gives $J$ values that are $10^{12}$ times wrong, cascading into absurd $G$ values.
> 2. **Radian-Degree Confusion (Loses 1-2 marks):** Angle of twist from dial gauges is in degrees. Substituting degrees directly into $\theta = \dfrac{TL}{GJ}$ gives incorrect rigidity values. Always multiply by $\dfrac{\pi}{180}$ first.
> 3. **Forgetting Effective Length (Loses 2 marks):** Use the gauge length $L$ **between the chucks**, not the total length of the specimen.
> 4. **Neglecting Slack (Loses 1 mark):** Always apply a small pre-load to remove backlash in the pulley and chuck mechanism before starting the experiment.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day:**

- **Master Equation:** $GJ = \dfrac{T \cdot L}{\theta}$ — *Memorize this formula; it is the heart of the experiment.*
- **Unit Hygiene:** Convert all dimensions to **SI units** (meters, newtons, radians) before substitution.
- **Torque Calculation:** For pulley-and-weight system, $T = W \times r$, where $r$ is the effective pulley radius.
- **Polar Moment (Solid):** $J = \dfrac{\pi d^4}{32}$ — *Note the fourth power; a small increase in diameter dramatically increases rigidity.*
- **Angle Conversion:** $1^\circ = \dfrac{\pi}{180} \text{ rad} \approx 0.01745 \text{ rad}$.
- **Shear Stress Distribution:** Linear from zero at the center to maximum at the outer surface.
- **Material Ranking:** Torsional rigidity ordering should follow the ranking of $G$ values — *Mild Steel > Copper > Brass* (in most cases).
- **Graph Method:** The slope of $T$ vs $\theta$ plot gives $\dfrac{T}{\theta}$ directly. Multiplying by $L$ gives $GJ$.
- **Assumptions Validity:** Theory holds only in the **elastic region** — do not apply loads that cause yielding.
- **Comparative Insight:** Despite brass having lower $G$, it is preferred in some applications due to **corrosion resistance** and **machinability** — engineering involves multiple trade-offs.
- **Practical Tip:** Always record readings in a **tabular format** with columns for Load, Torque, Angle (degrees), and Angle (radians).
- **Error Source Awareness:** Be aware of backlash in the dial gauge, slippage at the chuck, and bending (not pure torsion) effects — these introduce experimental errors.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Evaluate the Flexural Stiffness of Specimens Using Three-Point Bend Test on UTM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUL407 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Evaluate the Flexural Stiffness of Specimens Using Three-Point Bend Test on UTM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Three-Point Bend Test (TPBT)** is a standardized flexural testing method in which a specimen of defined geometry (typically rectangular or circular cross-section) is subjected to a bending load applied at the **midspan** through a single upper loading nose, while the specimen is supported on **two lower anvils** separated by a defined **span length (L)**. The test is conducted on a **Universal Testing Machine (UTM)** equipped with a dedicated three-point bending fixture, and the response is recorded as **Load (P) versus Deflection (δ)** at the loading point.

**Flexural Stiffness** is the resistance offered by a structural member against bending deformation. Mathematically, it is the product of the **Flexural Modulus (E)** and the **Second Moment of Area (I)** about the neutral axis:

$$\text{Flexural Stiffness} = E \times I$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Flexural stiffness characterizes the slope of the linear elastic region of the load–deflection curve. For a given geometry, the **flexural modulus (E)** is the intrinsic material property, while **EI** is the structural bending resistance of the specimen.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Diving Board

Imagine standing at the edge of a **diving board** at a swimming pool. The board bends downward under your weight. A **thick wooden board** barely deflects — it has high flexural stiffness. A **thin plastic strip** bends significantly — it has low flexural stiffness. In the TPBT:

- The **loading nose** is your foot applying force at the center.
- The **two supports** are the mounts fixing the board to the pool deck.
- The **deflection** is how much the center sags.
- The **flexural stiffness (EI)** determines how much it sags for a given load.

## 1.3 Key Physical Quantities & Standard Metrics

- **Flexural Modulus (E):** Expressed in **GPa** or **N/mm²**.
- **Second Moment of Area (I):** Expressed in **mm⁴** or **m⁴**.
- **Flexural Stiffness (EI):** Expressed in **N·m²** or **N·mm²**.
- **Span-to-Depth Ratio (L/d):** Per **ASTM D790** and **ISO 178**, standard ratio is **16:1** for flexural modulus determination to minimize shear effects.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Practical Note:** In the UTM lab, specimens are typically **rectangular bars** of mild steel, aluminum, or composite material, with dimensions recorded precisely using vernier calipers and micrometer screw gauge before testing.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Theory — Euler-Bernoulli Bending

When a simply supported beam is loaded at its midspan, it experiences **pure bending** in the central region between the load point and the supports. The fundamental assumptions of the **Euler-Bernoulli beam theory** applicable to TPBT are:

1. The material is **homogeneous and isotropic** within the elastic range.
2. The beam has a **uniform cross-section** along its length.
3. **Plane sections remain plane** and perpendicular to the neutral axis after bending.
4. **Deflections are small** compared to the span length (typically δ/L < 0.01).
5. **Shear deformation is negligible** — valid when L/d ≥ 16.
6. The material obeys **Hooke's Law** in the initial linear region.

## 2.2 Bending Moment Distribution

For a simply supported beam of span **L** with a concentrated load **P** at midspan:

- **Support reactions:** R_A = R_B = P/2
- **Maximum bending moment at midspan:** M_max = PL/4
- **Maximum shear force:** V_max = P/2 (constant between support and load)

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table summarizes every formula required for KTU 2024 evaluation:

| Quantity | Formula | Description |
|----------|---------|-------------|
| Maximum Flexural Stress | $\sigma_{\max} = \dfrac{3PL}{2bd^{2}}$ | Stress at outer fiber, midspan |
| Maximum Deflection | $\delta_{\max} = \dfrac{PL^{3}}{4Ebd^{3}}$ | Deflection at midspan |
| Flexural Modulus (from P, δ) | $E = \dfrac{PL^{3}}{4bd^{3}\delta}$ | Intrinsic material property in bending |
| Second Moment of Area | $I = \dfrac{bd^{3}}{12}$ | Rectangular cross-section about NA |
| Flexural Stiffness | $EI = \dfrac{Ebd^{3}}{12}$ | Structural bending resistance |
| Flexural Modulus (from slope) | $E = \dfrac{mL^{3}}{4bd^{3}}$ | Using slope $m = P/\delta$ |
| Flexural Stiffness (from slope) | $EI = \dfrac{mL^{3}}{48}$ | Direct from load–deflection slope |
| Flexural Strain at Outer Fiber | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{6\delta d}{L^{2}}$ | Strain at the bottom surface |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction:** In TPBT, the **flexural modulus (E)** is often slightly higher than the **Young's modulus (E)** from a tension test because bending combines tension and compression, and the neutral axis is stress-free. For isotropic materials, they are theoretically equal; for composites, they differ significantly due to fiber-matrix interaction.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Flexural stiffness of composite wing spars (carbon-fiber/epoxy).
- **Civil Engineering:** Load-bearing capacity of concrete beams, wooden joists.
- **Automotive:** Bending resistance of chassis components and leaf springs.
- **Biomedical:** Stiffness of bone implants and dental prostheses.
- **Sports Equipment:** Design of skis, cricket bats, and tennis rackets.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** The examiner expects students to clearly identify whether the material is in the **linear elastic region** before computing E. Always verify by checking that the load-deflection plot is a straight line passing through the origin.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Maximum Flexural Stress

**Starting Point:** The flexure formula for a beam in pure bending relates bending stress to bending moment:

$$\sigma = \frac{M \cdot y}{I}$$

where:
- $M$ = bending moment at the section
- $y$ = distance from the neutral axis
- $I$ = second moment of area about the neutral axis

**Step 1:** For three-point bending, the maximum bending moment occurs at the midspan:

$$M_{\max} = \frac{P \cdot L}{4}$$

**Step 2:** The maximum distance from the neutral axis for a rectangular specimen of thickness $d$ is:

$$y_{\max} = \frac{d}{2}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute into the flexure formula to obtain the maximum stress:

$$\sigma_{\max} = \frac{M_{\max} \cdot y_{\max}}{I}$$

$$\sigma_{\max} = \frac{\left(\frac{PL}{4}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{d}{2}\right)}{I} = \frac{PLd}{8I}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute the second moment of area for a rectangular cross-section, $I = \dfrac{bd^{3}}{12}$:

$$\sigma_{\max} = \frac{PLd}{8 \cdot \left(\frac{bd^{3}}{12}\right)} = \frac{12 \cdot PLd}{8 \cdot bd^{3}} = \frac{3PL}{2bd^{2}}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\sigma_{\max} = \frac{3PL}{2bd^{2}}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Maximum Deflection

**Starting Point:** The differential equation for elastic beam deflection is:

$$EI \cdot \frac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} = M(x)$$

**Step 1:** Set up the bending moment equation. Taking the origin at the left support, for $0 \leq x \leq L/2$:

$$M(x) = \frac{P}{2} \cdot x$$

**Step 2:** Integrate once to obtain the slope equation:

$$EI \cdot \frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{P}{2} \cdot \frac{x^{2}}{2} + C_{1} = \frac{Px^{2}}{4} + C_{1}$$

**Step 3:** Apply boundary condition: slope is zero at midspan by symmetry ($x = L/2$):

$$\frac{P(L/2)^{2}}{4} + C_{1} = 0 \implies C_{1} = -\frac{PL^{2}}{16}$$

**Step 4:** Integrate again to obtain the deflection equation:

$$EI \cdot y = \frac{Px^{3}}{12} - \frac{PL^{2}x}{16} + C_{2}$$

**Step 5:** Apply boundary condition: deflection is zero at $x = 0$:

$$C_{2} = 0$$

**Step 6:** Substitute $x = L/2$ to find maximum deflection at midspan:

$$\delta_{\max} = EI \cdot y\left(\frac{L}{2}\right) = \frac{P(L/2)^{3}}{12} - \frac{PL^{2}(L/2)}{16}$$

$$\delta_{\max} = \frac{PL^{3}}{96} - \frac{PL^{3}}{32} = \frac{PL^{3}}{96} - \frac{3PL^{3}}{96} = -\frac{2PL^{3}}{96} = -\frac{PL^{3}}{48}$$

The negative sign indicates downward deflection. Taking magnitude:

$$\delta_{\max} = \frac{PL^{3}}{48EI}$$

**Step 7:** Substitute $I = \dfrac{bd^{3}}{12}$:

$$\delta_{\max} = \frac{PL^{3}}{48E \cdot \left(\frac{bd^{3}}{12}\right)} = \frac{12 \cdot PL^{3}}{48 \cdot Ebd^{3}} = \frac{PL^{3}}{4Ebd^{3}}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\delta_{\max} = \frac{PL^{3}}{4Ebd^{3}}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Flexural Modulus

Rearranging the deflection formula:

$$E = \frac{PL^{3}}{4bd^{3}\delta}$$

If we define the **slope of the linear elastic region** of the load-deflection curve as $m = \dfrac{P}{\delta}$, then:

$$E = \frac{mL^{3}}{4bd^{3}}$$

And the **flexural stiffness** is:

$$EI = \frac{mL^{3}}{48}$$

---

## 3.4 Worked-Out Numerical Example

**Problem:** A mild steel specimen of width $b = 30$ mm, thickness $d = 6$ mm, and span $L = 120$ mm is tested in three-point bending on a UTM. At a load of $P = 1500$ N, the deflection recorded is $\delta = 0.85$ mm. Calculate the flexural modulus and flexural stiffness.

**Solution:**

**Given:** $P = 1500$ N, $\delta = 0.85$ mm, $L = 120$ mm, $b = 30$ mm, $d = 6$ mm.

**Step 1:** Calculate the second moment of area:

$$I = \frac{bd^{3}}{12} = \frac{30 \times 6^{3}}{12} = \frac{30 \times 216}{12} = 540 \text{ mm}^{4}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate the flexural modulus:

$$E = \frac{PL^{3}}{4bd^{3}\delta}$$

$$E = \frac{1500 \times 120^{3}}{4 \times 30 \times 6^{3} \times 0.85}$$

**Numerator:** $1500 \times 1{,}728{,}000 = 2.592 \times 10^{9}$ N·mm³

**Denominator:** $4 \times 30 \times 216 \times 0.85 = 22{,}032$ mm⁵

$$E = \frac{2.592 \times 10^{9}}{22{,}032} = 117{,}649.5 \text{ N/mm}^{2} \approx 117.65 \text{ GPa}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate the flexural stiffness:

$$EI = E \times I = 117{,}649.5 \times 540 = 63{,}530{,}730 \text{ N·mm}^{2} \approx 63.53 \text{ N·m}^{2}$$

**Step 4:** Calculate the maximum flexural stress at this load:

$$\sigma_{\max} = \frac{3PL}{2bd^{2}} = \frac{3 \times 1500 \times 120}{2 \times 30 \times 36} = \frac{540{,}000}{2160} = 250 \text{ MPa}$$

**Result Summary:** The flexural modulus of 117.65 GPa is lower than the standard Young's modulus of mild steel (~200 GPa), suggesting either specimen imperfections, test anomalies, or non-idealized boundary conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** Always include a **percentage error analysis** comparing experimental E with the standard literature value. A deviation within ±10% is generally acceptable for a UTM lab experiment.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow — UTM Three-Point Bend Test Setup

The following block diagram maps the functional interaction between UTM subsystems during a three-point bend test:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PC["Personal Computer with UTM Software"] --> DAQ["Data Acquisition System"]
    DAQ --> LC["Load Cell in Crosshead"]
    DAQ --> LVDT["LVDT or Extensometer"]
    CH["Crosshead Actuator Hydraulic Drive"] --> LN["Loading Nose Upper Anvil"]
    SP["Specimen Rectangular Bar"] --> LN
    SP --> SA["Support Anvil 1 Lower"]
    SP --> SB["Support Anvil 2 Lower"]
    FR["Fixed Crosshead Frame"] --> SA
    FR --> SB
    LC --> CH
    PC --> CH
    DAQ --> PC
    LVDT --> SP
```

**Subsystem Roles:**
- **PC + Software:** Real-time plotting of Load vs Deflection.
- **Load Cell:** Converts force into an electrical signal proportional to P.
- **LVDT (Linear Variable Differential Transformer):** Measures midspan deflection δ with high precision.
- **Crosshead Actuator:** Applies displacement at controlled rate (typically 1–5 mm/min).
- **Loading Nose & Supports:** Hardened steel anvils ensuring line contact loading.

---

## 4.2 Load-Deflection Curve — Regions of Interest

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    O["Origin Zero Load Zero Deflection"] --> LE["Linear Elastic Region Slope m Constant"]
    LE --> PL["Proportional Limit"]
    PL --> NL["Non-Linear Region Microplasticity"]
    NL --> MX["Maximum Load Peak"]
    MX --> FT["Fracture or Specimen Failure"]
    style LE fill:#90EE90
    style PL fill:#FFFF00
    style NL fill:#FFA500
    style MX fill:#FF6347
```

**Region Descriptions:**
- **Linear Elastic Region:** Used to determine flexural modulus E. Slope m = P/δ is constant.
- **Proportional Limit:** Boundary where Hooke's law ceases to apply.
- **Non-Linear Region:** Onset of plastic deformation; data here is excluded from E calculation.
- **Maximum Load:** Used to calculate ultimate flexural strength.

---

## 4.3 Experimental Procedure Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Measure Specimen Dimensions b d L using Vernier Calipers"] --> S2["Set Span L on Three Point Bend Fixture with Adjustable Supports"]
    S2 --> S3["Place Specimen Centrally on Two Lower Supports"]
    S3 --> S4["Align Loading Nose Exactly at Midspan"]
    S4 --> S5["Zero the Load Cell and LVDT in UTM Software"]
    S5 --> S6["Apply Load at Constant Crosshead Speed 2 mm per minute"]
    S6 --> S7["Record Load P and Deflection delta Continuously"]
    S7 --> S8["Plot Load versus Deflection Curve"]
    S8 --> S9{"Is Initial Region Linear?"}
    S9 -- Yes --> S10["Determine Slope m from Linear Portion"]
    S9 -- No --> S11["Use Secant Method between Zero and Specific Strain"]
    S10 --> S12["Calculate Flexural Modulus E equals m L cubed divided by 4 b d cubed"]
    S11 --> S12
    S12 --> S13["Calculate Flexural Stiffness EI equals m L cubed divided by 48"]
    S13 --> S14["Calculate Maximum Flexural Stress sigma equals 3 P L divided by 2 b d squared"]
    S14 --> S15["Report Results with Specimen Identification and Error Analysis"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Lab Tip:** Always perform **3 trials** on identical specimens and report the **mean value** with **standard deviation** for reliable flexural stiffness evaluation.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Define flexural stiffness. How is it experimentally determined from a three-point bend test on a UTM?**

**Model Answer:**

Flexural stiffness is defined as the resistance of a structural member against bending deformation. It is mathematically expressed as the product of the flexural modulus (E) and the second moment of area (I) about the neutral axis: **Flexural Stiffness = EI**.

In a three-point bend test on a UTM, it is experimentally determined by:
1. Recording load-deflection data in the linear elastic region.
2. Calculating the slope $m = P/\delta$ of the linear portion.
3. Computing flexural stiffness using $EI = \dfrac{mL^{3}}{48}$, where L is the span length.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Distinguish between flexural modulus and Young's modulus. Under what conditions are they different?**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Flexural Modulus (E_bend) | Young's Modulus (E_tension) |
|-----------|--------------------------|------------------------------|
| Test Type | Three-point or four-point bending | Uniaxial tensile test |
| Stress State | Combined tension-compression | Pure uniaxial tension |
| Formula | $E = \dfrac{PL^{3}}{4bd^{3}\delta}$ | $E = \dfrac{\sigma}{\varepsilon} = \dfrac{FL_{0}}{A\Delta L}$ |

For **isotropic homogeneous materials** (e.g., mild steel, aluminum), $E_{bend} \approx E_{tension}$. For **anisotropic materials** (composites, wood, reinforced plastics), they differ significantly because the fiber-matrix interface responds differently to tension versus bending.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a) Derive the expression for maximum flexural stress in a rectangular specimen subjected to three-point bending. State all assumptions clearly.** **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions:**
1. Material is homogeneous, isotropic, and obeys Hooke's law in the elastic range.
2. Beam has uniform rectangular cross-section.
3. Plane sections remain plane and perpendicular to the neutral axis (Euler-Bernoulli theory).
4. Deflections are small compared to span length.

**Derivation:**

Starting with the flexure formula:

$$\sigma = \frac{My}{I}$$

For a simply supported beam with central point load P, the maximum bending moment at midspan is:

$$M_{\max} = \frac{PL}{4}$$

The maximum distance from the neutral axis for a rectangular cross-section of thickness d is $y_{\max} = d/2$. The second moment of area is:

$$I = \frac{bd^{3}}{12}$$

Substituting into the flexure formula:

$$\sigma_{\max} = \frac{M_{\max} \cdot y_{\max}}{I} = \frac{\left(\frac{PL}{4}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{d}{2}\right)}{\frac{bd^{3}}{12}} = \frac{PLd}{8} \cdot \frac{12}{bd^{3}} = \frac{3PL}{2bd^{2}}$$

**Final Result:** $\sigma_{\max} = \dfrac{3PL}{2bd^{2}}$

---

**(b) A rectangular specimen of width 25 mm and thickness 5 mm is tested in three-point bending on a UTM with a span of 100 mm. The load at the proportional limit is 600 N and the corresponding midspan deflection is 1.2 mm. Calculate: (i) Flexural modulus, (ii) Flexural stiffness, (iii) Maximum flexural stress at the proportional limit.** **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $b = 25$ mm, $d = 5$ mm, $L = 100$ mm, $P = 600$ N, $\delta = 1.2$ mm.

**(i) Flexural Modulus:**

$$E = \frac{PL^{3}}{4bd^{3}\delta} = \frac{600 \times 100^{3}}{4 \times 25 \times 5^{3} \times 1.2}$$

$$E = \frac{600 \times 1{,}000{,}000}{4 \times 25 \times 125 \times 1.2} = \frac{6 \times 10^{8}}{15{,}000} = 40{,}000 \text{ N/mm}^{2} = 40 \text{ GPa}$$

**(ii) Flexural Stiffness:**

$$I = \frac{bd^{3}}{12} = \frac{25 \times 125}{12} = 260.42 \text{ mm}^{4}$$

$$EI = 40{,}000 \times 260.42 = 10{,}416{,}667 \text{ N·mm}^{2} \approx 10.42 \text{ N·m}^{2}$$

**(iii) Maximum Flexural Stress:**

$$\sigma_{\max} = \frac{3PL}{2bd^{2}} = \frac{3 \times 600 \times 100}{2 \times 25 \times 25} = \frac{180{,}000}{1250} = 144 \text{ MPa}$$

**Result Summary:** Flexural modulus = 40 GPa; Flexural stiffness = 10.42 N·m²; Max flexural stress = 144 MPa.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**(a) Explain with a neat functional block diagram, the procedure for evaluating flexural stiffness of a specimen using a three-point bend test on a UTM.** **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Apparatus Required:**
- Universal Testing Machine (UTM) with three-point bend fixture
- Specimen (rectangular bar)
- Vernier calipers and micrometer screw gauge
- Data acquisition system

**Procedure:**

1. **Specimen Preparation:** Measure the width (b), thickness (d), and length of the specimen using vernier calipers and micrometer screw gauge. Record the average of three readings.

2. **Fixture Setup:** Adjust the span (L) between the two lower supports as per standard (typically L = 16d for ASTM D790).

3. **Specimen Placement:** Place the specimen symmetrically on the two lower supports with the loading nose centered at the midspan.

4. **Instrument Calibration:** Zero the load cell and LVDT (deflection sensor) using the UTM software. Set the crosshead speed (typically 1–5 mm/min for metallic specimens).

5. **Load Application:** Start the test. The crosshead moves downward at constant speed, applying load through the loading nose.

6. **Data Recording:** The software continuously records Load (P) and Deflection (δ) and plots the P-δ curve in real time.

7. **Identification of Linear Region:** Examine the initial portion of the P-δ curve. The region where the curve is a straight line passing through the origin is the linear elastic region.

8. **Calculation of Slope:** Determine the slope $m = P/\delta$ from the linear portion using the least-squares fit method.

9. **Calculation of Results:**
   - Flexural Modulus: $E = \dfrac{mL^{3}}{4bd^{3}}$
   - Flexural Stiffness: $EI = \dfrac{mL^{3}}{48}$
   - Maximum Flexural Stress: $\sigma = \dfrac{3PL_{\max}}{2bd^{2}}$

10. **Reporting:** Tabulate results, include error analysis, and compare with standard literature values.

**Functional Block Diagram:** Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the complete functional architecture.

---

**(b) During a three-point bend test on a polymer composite specimen, the slope of the linear elastic region of the load-deflection curve is determined as 2000 N/mm. The specimen dimensions are: width 20 mm, thickness 4 mm, span 80 mm. Calculate: (i) Flexural modulus, (ii) Flexural stiffness, (iii) Maximum load if the maximum allowable flexural strain is 1.5%.** **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $m = 2000$ N/mm, $b = 20$ mm, $d = 4$ mm, $L = 80$ mm, $\varepsilon_{max} = 0.015$.

**(i) Flexural Modulus:**

$$E = \frac{mL^{3}}{4bd^{3}} = \frac{2000 \times 80^{3}}{4 \times 20 \times 4^{3}}$$

$$E = \frac{2000 \times 512{,}000}{4 \times 20 \times 64} = \frac{1.024 \times 10^{9}}{5120} = 200{,}000 \text{ N/mm}^{2} = 200 \text{ GPa}$$

**(ii) Flexural Stiffness:**

$$I = \frac{bd^{3}}{12} = \frac{20 \times 64}{12} = 106.67 \text{ mm}^{4}$$

$$EI = 200{,}000 \times 106.67 = 21{,}333{,}333 \text{ N·mm}^{2} \approx 21.33 \text{ N·m}^{2}$$

**(iii) Maximum Load from Strain Limit:**

Using the flexural strain formula $\varepsilon = \dfrac{6\delta d}{L^{2}}$, and the relation $\delta = \dfrac{P}{m}$:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{6(P/m) \cdot d}{L^{2}} = \frac{6Pd}{mL^{2}}$$

Solving for P:

$$P = \frac{\varepsilon \cdot m \cdot L^{2}}{6d} = \frac{0.015 \times 2000 \times 80^{2}}{6 \times 4} = \frac{0.015 \times 2000 \times 6400}{24}$$

$$P = \frac{192{,}000}{24} = 8000 \text{ N} = 8 \text{ kN}$$

**Result Summary:** E = 200 GPa; EI = 21.33 N·m²; Maximum allowable load = 8 kN.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing mm and m in the same calculation. Always convert all dimensions to mm (or all to m) before substituting.
> 2. **Wrong Moment Arm:** Students often use the full span L instead of L/4 for the maximum bending moment in three-point bending.
> 3. **Forgetting I = bd³/12:** When asked for flexural stiffness EI, students sometimes give only E. Always compute I separately.
> 4. **Using Non-Linear Data:** Calculating E from the post-yield region gives erroneous low values. Always restrict to the linear elastic portion.
> 5. **Ignoring Span-to-Depth Ratio:** For very short spans (L/d < 16), shear deflection becomes significant, and the standard formula overestimates E.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- [ ] **Three-Point Bend Test** applies load at midspan, measures deflection at the same point.
- [ ] **Flexural Stiffness (EI)** is the product of flexural modulus (E) and second moment of area (I).
- [ ] **Key formulas** to memorize: $\sigma_{\max} = \dfrac{3PL}{2bd^{2}}$, $\delta_{\max} = \dfrac{PL^{3}}{4Ebd^{3}}$, $E = \dfrac{PL^{3}}{4bd^{3}\delta}$, $EI = \dfrac{mL^{3}}{48}$.
- [ ] **Second moment of area** for rectangular cross-section: $I = \dfrac{bd^{3}}{12}$.
- [ ] **Slope of P-δ curve** ($m$) in the linear region is used to compute E: $E = \dfrac{mL^{3}}{4bd^{3}}$.
- [ ] **Maximum bending moment** in TPBT occurs at midspan: $M_{\max} = \dfrac{PL}{4}$.
- [ ] **Assumptions:** Plane sections remain plane, small deflections, isotropic material, Euler-Bernoulli theory.
- [ ] **Span-to-depth ratio:** Standard L/d = 16 (ASTM D790) to minimize shear effects.
- [ ] **Difference between flexural and Young's modulus:** They are equal for isotropic materials but differ for composites.
- [ ] **Procedure:** Measure dimensions → Set span → Zero instruments → Apply load at constant rate → Plot P-δ curve → Identify linear region → Calculate slope → Compute E and EI.
- [ ] **Units:** E in GPa or N/mm²; I in mm⁴; EI in N·m² or N·mm².
