- [ ] **Error Analysis:** Always compare experimental E with standard literature value; ±10% deviation is acceptable.
- [ ] **Flexural stress** at outer fiber is maximum; neutral axis has zero stress.
- [ ] **Flexural strain** at outer fiber: $\varepsilon = \dfrac{6\delta d}{L^{2}}$.
- [ ] **Practical Tip:** Use vernier calipers for width, micrometer screw gauge for thickness (precision required).
- [ ] **KTU Lab Viva Question:** "Why is the flexural modulus sometimes higher than Young's modulus?" — Answer: In bending, one surface is in tension and the other in compression; the neutral axis is stress-free, leading to a combined stress state.
- [ ] **KTU Lab Viva Question:** "What is the role of the LVDT in TPBT?" — Answer: It precisely measures midspan deflection δ without contact.
- [ ] **KTU Lab Viva Question:** "Why is the span-to-depth ratio kept at 16:1?" — Answer: To ensure pure bending conditions and minimize shear deformation effects.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determine the Notch Toughness of Materials Using Izod and Charpy Impact Tests
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUL407 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determine the Notch Toughness of Materials Using Izod and Charpy Impact Tests

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Notch Toughness** is defined as the ability of a material to absorb energy and plastically deform in the presence of a stress concentration (notch) before fracture occurs under dynamic/impact loading conditions. It is a measure of a material's resistance to **brittle fracture** when subjected to a sudden, high-velocity load.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** laboratory curriculum, this property is experimentally determined using two standardized impact tests governed by **ASTM E23** and **IS 1757** standards:

- **Izod Impact Test** — A cantilever-type (vertical) impact test where the specimen is held as a vertical cantilever and struck on the same face as the notch.
- **Charpy Impact Test** — A simply-supported (horizontal) beam-type test where the specimen is supported at both ends and struck on the face opposite to the notch.

The energy absorbed during fracture, measured in **Joules (J)**, is termed the **Impact Energy** or **Impact Strength** and serves as the quantitative indicator of notch toughness.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The two standard notch geometries used in KTU-affiliated labs are the **V-notch** (45° included angle, 2 mm deep, 0.25 mm root radius) and the **U-notch** (keyhole type, 5 mm deep, 1 mm root radius). V-notch is preferred for brittle materials; U-notch for ductile materials.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have two identical drinking glasses — one made of regular glass and the other of toughened (tempered) glass. You tap both with a small hammer at the same velocity.

- The **regular glass** shatters instantly with very little energy absorbed → **Low Notch Toughness** (brittle).
- The **toughened glass** may chip, deform at the impact zone, and absorb significant energy before any crack propagates → **High Notch Toughness** (ductile).

The **Izod and Charpy tests** simulate this "hammer tap" in a controlled, repeatable, and quantifiable manner. The pendulum's loss of potential energy after breaking the specimen = the energy the material absorbed while fracturing. This absorbed energy is the **Notch Toughness**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why notches?** A notch acts as a **stress concentrator** (theoretical stress concentration factor $K_t \to \infty$ at a perfectly sharp crack tip). Real engineering components always contain scratches, holes, fillets, or inclusions that act as natural notches. Impact testing therefore evaluates the material in its **worst-case service condition**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following are the standard reference metrics used universally in impact testing laboratories:

- **Standard Specimen Length ($L$):** $55 \text{ mm}$
- **Standard Specimen Cross-Section:** $10 \text{ mm} \times 10 \text{ mm}$ for Charpy; $10 \text{ mm} \times 10 \text{ mm}$ for Izod (with a 75 mm length)
- **Standard Striking Velocity:** $5 \text{ m/s}$ to $5.5 \text{ m/s}$
- **Gravitational Acceleration ($g$):** $9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- **Standard Test Temperature:** $27 \pm 2 \text{ }^\circ\text{C}$ (room temperature)
- **Impact Energy Range:** $0 \text{ J}$ to $300 \text{ J}$ (typical for pendulum capacities of 300 J)
- **Pendulum Mass ($m$):** Typically $20 \text{ kg}$ for a 300 J machine

> [!TIP]
> Always quote the test temperature in your KTU lab report — material toughness is **temperature-dependent**, and the ductile-to-brittle transition temperature (DBTT) is a critical engineering parameter for ferritic steels.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation of Impact Testing

The impact test is fundamentally an **energy-balance experiment**. The principle of **conservation of energy** governs the entire process:

### 2.1.1 The Operational Logic

1. The pendulum hammer is raised to a fixed initial height $h_1$ (defining initial potential energy $PE_1$).
2. Upon release, the pendulum swings down, converting $PE_1 \to KE$ (kinetic energy) at the bottom of the swing.
3. At the lowest point, the striker contacts the specimen. The specimen fractures, absorbing energy $E_{abs}$.
4. The pendulum continues to swing upward to a new height $h_2$ (residual energy $PE_2$).
5. The dial gauge records the angular swing, which is calibrated to read the absorbed energy directly.

### 2.1.2 Energy Conservation Chain

$$PE_1 = E_{abs} + PE_2 + E_{friction} + E_{air}$$

In well-calibrated KTU lab machines, $E_{friction}$ and $E_{air}$ are negligible (typically $< 0.5\%$ of total energy). Therefore:

$$E_{abs} \approx PE_1 - PE_2 = mg(h_1 - h_2)$$

## 2.2 Detailed Comparison: Izod vs. Charpy

The following are the distinguishing features tested regularly in KTU viva voce:

| Feature | Izod Test | Charpy Test |
|---|---|---|
| Specimen orientation | Vertical (cantilever) | Horizontal (simply supported) |
| Notch position | Facing the striker (on tension side during impact) | Opposite to striker |
| Striking direction | Same side as notch | Opposite side of notch |
| Support type | Cantilever (clamped at one end) | Anvil-supported at both ends |
| Span length ($L_s$) | N/A (cantilever) | $40 \text{ mm}$ between supports |
| Specimen size | $75 \times 10 \times 10 \text{ mm}$ | $55 \times 10 \times 10 \text{ mm}$ |
| Typical use | Plastics, metals, composites | Metals, weldments, HAZ studies |
| Standard | ASTM E23, IS 1757 (Part 1) | ASTM E23, IS 1757 (Part 2) |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is your **exam-ready cheat sheet** for Module Experiment 2 calculations.

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Initial Potential Energy of Pendulum | $PE_1$ | $mgR(1 - \cos\alpha_1)$ | Joules (J) |
| 2 | Final Potential Energy of Pendulum | $PE_2$ | $mgR(1 - \cos\alpha_2)$ | Joules (J) |
| 3 | Energy Absorbed by Specimen | $E_{abs}$ | $mgR(\cos\alpha_2 - \cos\alpha_1)$ | Joules (J) |
| 4 | Impact Strength (Charpy) | $IS_C$ | $E_{abs} / A_{net}$ | $\text{J/mm}^2$ |
| 5 | Impact Strength (Izod) | $IS_I$ | $E_{abs} / A_{net}$ | $\text{J/mm}^2$ |
| 6 | Net Cross-Sectional Area at Notch | $A_{net}$ | $b \times (d - n)$ | $\text{mm}^2$ |
| 7 | Theoretical Striking Velocity | $v$ | $\sqrt{2gR(1-\cos\alpha_1)}$ | $\text{m/s}$ |
| 8 | Strain Energy Rate (toughness index) | $U$ | $E_{abs} / V_{notch\_zone}$ | $\text{J/mm}^3$ |
| 9 | Angle of Swing (initial) | $\alpha_1$ | Measured directly | degrees or radians |
| 10 | Angle of Swing (final, after break) | $\alpha_2$ | Measured directly | degrees or radians |

Where:
- $m$ = mass of pendulum hammer (kg)
- $g$ = acceleration due to gravity = **$9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$**
- $R$ = length of pendulum arm (m)
- $\alpha_1$ = initial lift angle (typically $150^\circ$)
- $\alpha_2$ = final swing angle after fracture
- $b$ = specimen width = **$10 \text{ mm}$**
- $d$ = specimen depth = **$10 \text{ mm}$**
- $n$ = notch depth = **$2 \text{ mm}$** (V-notch standard)
- $A_{net} = 10 \times (10 - 2) = 80 \text{ mm}^2$ for standard V-notch

## 2.4 Engineering Utility in Real-World Applications

Impact test data is **mission-critical** in the following KTU-relevant industries:

- **Pressure vessel design** (ASME Section VIII mandates Charpy testing for low-temperature service)
- **Shipbuilding** (ABS and Lloyd's Register require impact-tested hull plates)
- **Pipeline engineering** (API 5L specifies Charpy energy for line-pipe steels)
- **Bridges in cold climates** (e.g., the Alaska pipeline; Charpy tests at $-46 \text{ }^\circ\text{C}$)
- **Automotive crashworthiness** (modern high-strength steels must meet impact energy thresholds)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Connection:** The **ductile-to-brittle transition temperature (DBTT)** concept is a direct outgrowth of Charpy testing. Below DBTT, BCC metals (like ferritic steels) fail in a brittle, cleavage-dominated manner — a phenomenon responsible for historical disasters like the **Liberty Ship fractures** of WWII.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Energy Absorbed by the Specimen

This is the **central derivation** that every KTU lab viva expects you to reproduce.

### Step 1: Initial Position of the Pendulum

The pendulum hammer of mass $m$ is raised to an initial angle $\alpha_1$ from the vertical, with the center of mass at a height $h_1$ above its lowest position.

The vertical height of the center of mass above the rest position is:

$$h_1 = R - R\cos\alpha_1 = R(1 - \cos\alpha_1)$$

where $R$ is the distance from the pivot to the center of mass of the pendulum.

### Step 2: Initial Potential Energy

The initial gravitational potential energy stored in the pendulum is:

$$PE_1 = mgh_1 = mgR(1 - \cos\alpha_1)$$

### Step 3: Energy at the Moment of Impact

By conservation of energy, at the bottom of the swing, all $PE_1$ converts to kinetic energy (neglecting air drag):

$$KE_{impact} = \frac{1}{2}mv^2 = mgR(1 - \cos\alpha_1)$$

This gives the **striking velocity**:

$$v = \sqrt{2gR(1 - \cos\alpha_1)}$$

### Step 4: Final Position After Fracture

After the specimen fractures, the pendulum continues to swing upward to a final angle $\alpha_2$. The remaining potential energy is:

$$PE_2 = mgR(1 - \cos\alpha_2)$$

### Step 5: Energy Absorbed by the Specimen

By conservation of energy, the energy absorbed equals the difference:

$$E_{abs} = PE_1 - PE_2$$

Substituting:

$$E_{abs} = mgR(1 - \cos\alpha_1) - mgR(1 - \cos\alpha_2)$$

$$E_{abs} = mgR[(1 - \cos\alpha_1) - (1 - \cos\alpha_2)]$$

$$E_{abs} = mgR[\cos\alpha_2 - \cos\alpha_1]$$

$$\boxed{E_{abs} = mgR(\cos\alpha_2 - \cos\alpha_1)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Since $\alpha_2 < \alpha_1$ (the pendulum rises lower after breaking the specimen), $\cos\alpha_2 > \cos\alpha_1$, ensuring $E_{abs} > 0$ as physically required.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Lab Style)

**Problem Statement (Typical KTU Lab Question):**

A Charpy impact test is performed on a standard mild steel specimen ($10 \text{ mm} \times 10 \text{ mm} \times 55 \text{ mm}$) with a **2 mm deep V-notch**. The pendulum hammer has:
- Mass: $m = 21.5 \text{ kg}$
- Effective length: $R = 0.80 \text{ m}$
- Initial lift angle: $\alpha_1 = 150^\circ$
- Final swing angle: $\alpha_2 = 92^\circ$

**Calculate:**
1. The impact energy absorbed by the specimen.
2. The impact strength in $\text{J/mm}^2$.

### Step 1: Convert Angles to Radians (for calculator safety)

$$\alpha_1 = 150^\circ = \frac{150 \times \pi}{180} = 2.6180 \text{ rad}$$

$$\alpha_2 = 92^\circ = \frac{92 \times \pi}{180} = 1.6057 \text{ rad}$$

### Step 2: Calculate the Cosines

$$\cos\alpha_1 = \cos(150^\circ) = -\cos(30^\circ) = -0.8660$$

$$\cos\alpha_2 = \cos(92^\circ) = -\sin(2^\circ) = -0.0349$$

### Step 3: Apply the Energy Formula

$$E_{abs} = mgR(\cos\alpha_2 - \cos\alpha_1)$$

$$E_{abs} = (21.5)(9.81)(0.80)[\,(-0.0349) - (-0.8660)\,]$$

$$E_{abs} = (168.73)\,[\,0.8311\,]$$

$$\boxed{E_{abs} = 140.24 \text{ J}}$$

### Step 4: Calculate Net Cross-Sectional Area

$$A_{net} = b \times (d - n) = 10 \times (10 - 2) = 80 \text{ mm}^2$$

### Step 5: Calculate Impact Strength

$$IS = \frac{E_{abs}}{A_{net}} = \frac{140.24}{80}$$

$$\boxed{IS = 1.753 \text{ J/mm}^2 = 1753 \text{ kJ/m}^2}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Practical Tip:** In the lab, the machine dial directly reads $E_{abs}$ in Joules — you don't need to compute it from $m$, $g$, $R$, $\alpha$. The derivation above is for **viva and written exam purposes only**.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Sources of Error and Corrections

| # | Error Source | Effect on $E_{abs}$ | KTU-Accepted Correction |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Friction in pivot bearings | Over-estimate | Subtract friction loss (≈ 0.5–1 J) |
| 2 | Specimen not centered | Inconsistent fracture | Re-test with proper alignment |
| 3 | Worn V-notch striker | Incorrect stress field | Use calibrated striker per ASTM E23 |
| 4 | Specimen not at exact 27°C | Temperature-dependent scatter | Use temperature-controlled bath |
| 5 | Pendulum not released from rest | Erroneous $\alpha_1$ | Use mechanical release latch |
| 6 | Specimen jamming in supports | Energy loss to friction | Loosen supports, re-test |
| 7 | Multiple impacts (specimen not fractured fully) | Under-estimate | Ensure single, clean fracture |

## 3.4 Ductile-to-Brittle Transition Analysis (Theoretical Extension)

For BCC metals, the Charpy impact energy follows a **sigmoidal (S-curve) relationship** with temperature:

$$E(T) = E_{upper} - \frac{E_{upper} - E_{lower}}{1 + \exp\left[\frac{T - T_{50}}{k}\right]}$$

where:
- $E_{upper}$ = upper-shelf energy (fully ductile, ~300 J)
- $E_{lower}$ = lower-shelf energy (fully brittle, ~10 J)
- $T_{50}$ = transition temperature at $E = (E_{upper} + E_{lower})/2$
- $k$ = curve fitting constant (typically 10–20°C)

> [!NOTE]
> FCC metals (aluminum, copper, austenitic stainless steel) do **not** exhibit DBTT at typical service temperatures — they remain ductile even at cryogenic conditions. This is why LNG tanks use austenitic stainless steel.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram: Impact Test Energy Flow

The following Mermaid block diagram traces the **energy flow** through an impact testing machine, from the operator's input to the final dial reading. This is the **Block-Level Functional Architecture** adaptation as required by the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE protocol for complex physical experiments.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Operator Latches Pendulum at angle alpha1] --> B[Pendulum stores PE1 = mgR(1 - cos alpha1)]
    B --> C[Release Latch Activated]
    C --> D[Free Swing Conversion PE1 to KE_impact]
    D --> E[Striker Contacts Specimen at Notch]
    E --> F{Specimen Fails?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Specimen Absorbs E_abs in Plastic Deformation and Fracture]
    G --> H[Residual KE lifts Pendulum to alpha2]
    H --> I[PE2 = mgR(1 - cos alpha2) is stored]
    I --> J[Pointer locked on calibrated dial]
    J --> K[Dial reading = E_abs in Joules]
    F -- No --> L[Pendulum continues to alpha1 = alpha1 no energy lost]
    L --> M[Result: Zero impact energy recorded]
    K --> N[Lab Report: Calculate IS = E_abs divided by A_net]
    M --> N
```

## 4.2 Schematic Comparison: Izod vs. Charpy Specimen Mounting

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Charpy["CHARPY TEST - Horizontal Beam"]
        C1[Support Anvil at Left] --> C2[Specimen 55mm x 10mm x 10mm with V-notch on Tension Side]
        C2 --> C3[Support Anvil at Right]
        C4[Striker Hammer] --> C5[Impacts face opposite to notch]
        C5 --> C2
    end
    subgraph Izod["IZOD TEST - Vertical Cantilever"]
        I1[Clamp at Bottom] --> I2[Specimen 75mm x 10mm x 10mm with V-notch at top facing striker]
        I2 --> I3[Free Top End]
        I4[Striker Hammer] --> I5[Impacts face containing notch at 22mm from top]
        I5 --> I2
    end
```

## 4.3 System Architecture: Impact Test Lab Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Receive Specimen and Verify Dimensions] --> B[Step 2: Measure Notch Depth using Tool Makers Microscope]
    B --> C[Step 3: Condition Specimen at 27 C for 10 minutes]
    C --> D[Step 4: Mount Specimen in Anvils or Clamp]
    D --> E[Step 5: Verify Pendulum Free Swing - No Specimen Test]
    E --> F[Step 6: Latch Pendulum at alpha1 = 150 degrees]
    F --> G[Step 7: Release Pendulum and Capture Dial Reading]
    G --> H[Step 8: Record E_abs in Joules]
    H --> I[Step 9: Compute Impact Strength IS = E_abs divided by A_net]
    I --> J[Step 10: Repeat for 3 Specimens and Report Mean]
    J --> K[Step 11: Classify Failure Mode - Brittle or Ductile]
    K --> L[Step 12: Cross-check with Hounsfield Tensile Data]
```

## 4.4 Component-Level Diagram of a Pendulum Impact Tester

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Pivot Bearing Assembly] --> P2[Pendulum Arm of Length R]
    P2 --> P3[Striker Head with Hardened Edge]
    P3 --> P4[Striking Radius 8mm conforming to ASTM E23]
    P2 --> P5[Counterweight for Balance]
    P2 --> P6[Friction Plate Assembly]
    P7[Pointer and Dial Gauge] --> P8[Drag Pointer for Maximum Angle]
    P9[Specimen Supports Anvils] --> P10[Anvil Spacing 40mm for Charpy]
    P11[Base Frame] --> P12[Cast Iron Foundation for Vibration Damping]
    P11 --> P13[Enclosure Guard for Safety]
    P14[Lifting Handle] --> P2
    P15[Release Latch Mechanism] --> P2
```

> [!TIP]
> When asked to draw a labeled diagram in your KTU exam, always include: **(1)** Pendulum, **(2)** Striker edge, **(3)** Specimen with notch, **(4)** Supports/Anvils, **(5)** Dial gauge, **(6)** Angle indicator. A neat, well-labeled diagram carries **2 marks** by itself in viva.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> These mirror the **KTU 3-mark quiz / first-series exam** style: direct, definition-based, requiring 1 diagram + 2–3 lines of explanation.

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define Notch Toughness. How is it experimentally determined?**

**Model Answer:**

**Notch Toughness** is the ability of a material to absorb energy and undergo plastic deformation in the presence of a stress concentrator (notch) before fracturing under dynamic loading.

It is experimentally determined by impact tests — primarily the **Izod test** and the **Charpy test** — in which a standardized notched specimen is struck by a pendulum hammer, and the energy absorbed during fracture (in Joules) is recorded.

**Energy Absorbed (E) = m × g × R × (cos α₂ − cos α₁)**

A simple labeled diagram of either test setup should accompany this answer for full marks.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between Izod and Charpy impact tests (any four points).**

**Model Answer:**

| S.No. | Izod Test | Charpy Test |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Specimen is held vertically as a cantilever | Specimen is held horizontally on two anvils |
| 2 | Notch is on the same face as the striker impact | Notch is on the face opposite to the striker impact |
| 3 | Specimen size: 75 × 10 × 10 mm | Specimen size: 55 × 10 × 10 mm |
| 4 | Used for plastics, metals, and composites | Used primarily for metals, weldments, and HAZ studies |
| 5 | One end clamped, other free | Both ends simply supported |

---

## 5.2 PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per **KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Evaluation (ESE)** regulations, Part B questions carry **internal choice**. Two completely independent questions are provided below; the student answers **one**.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the procedure for conducting a **Charpy impact test**. List the standard specimen dimensions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** During a Charpy test on a standard mild steel specimen, the following readings were obtained:

- Mass of pendulum hammer: $m = 22 \text{ kg}$
- Effective pendulum length (pivot to center of mass): $R = 0.85 \text{ m}$
- Initial lift angle: $\alpha_1 = 140^\circ$
- Final swing angle: $\alpha_2 = 96^\circ$
- Specimen cross-section: $10 \text{ mm} \times 10 \text{ mm}$
- V-notch depth: $n = 2 \text{ mm}$

**Calculate the impact energy absorbed and the impact strength of the material.** **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**Diagram:** [See Section 4.4 for the labeled Charpy setup]

**Procedure:**

1. **Specimen preparation:** Cut a standard specimen of dimensions $55 \text{ mm} \times 10 \text{ mm} \times 10 \text{ mm}$. Machine a V-notch (45° included angle, 2 mm depth, 0.25 mm root radius) at the center of the length.
2. **Conditioning:** Hold the specimen at $27 \pm 2 \text{ }^\circ\text{C}$ for at least 10 minutes in a water bath or oven.
3. **Machine setup:** Ensure the pendulum swings freely without friction (verify with a no-specimen trial). The dial should return to zero.
4. **Mounting:** Place the specimen horizontally on the anvils with the notch on the **side opposite** to the striker. Span between supports = **40 mm**.
5. **Testing:** Raise the pendulum to the latch position (typically $\alpha_1 = 150^\circ$). Release the latch. The striker impacts the specimen, fracturing it.
6. **Reading:** The drag pointer on the dial indicates the maximum angle $\alpha_2$ reached after fracture. The dial is calibrated to read $E_{abs}$ directly in Joules.
7. **Repetition:** Test at least 3 specimens and report the mean value.

**Standard Specimen Dimensions (as per IS 1757 / ASTM E23):**
- Length: $55 \text{ mm}$
- Width: $10 \text{ mm}$
- Depth: $10 \text{ mm}$
- V-notch depth: $2 \text{ mm}$
- Notch angle: $45^\circ$ ± $1^\circ$
- Root radius: $0.25 \text{ mm}$

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Given:**
- $m = 22 \text{ kg}$
- $R = 0.85 \text{ m}$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- $\alpha_1 = 140^\circ$
- $\alpha_2 = 96^\circ$

**Step 1: Compute cosines**

$$\cos(140^\circ) = -0.7660$$

$$\cos(96^\circ) = -0.1045$$

**Step 2: Apply the energy formula**

$$E_{abs} = mgR(\cos\alpha_2 - \cos\alpha_1)$$

$$E_{abs} = (22)(9.81)(0.85)\left[(-0.1045) - (-0.7660)\right]$$

$$E_{abs} = 183.45 \times [0.6615]$$

$$E_{abs} = 121.35 \text{ J}$$

**Step 3: Net cross-sectional area**

$$A_{net} = b \times (d - n) = 10 \times (10 - 2) = 80 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 4: Impact strength**

$$IS = \frac{E_{abs}}{A_{net}} = \frac{121.35}{80}$$

$$\boxed{IS = 1.517 \text{ J/mm}^2 = 1517 \text{ kJ/m}^2}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #1:** A common error is to forget to convert angles to a calculator-friendly form OR to use the **wrong cross-sectional area** (using full $10 \times 10 = 100 \text{ mm}^2$ instead of the **notched** area). The notch depth **must be subtracted**. Losing 1–2 marks here is a frequent viva pitfall.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

**(a)** What is the **Ductile-to-Brittle Transition Temperature (DBTT)**? Explain with a sketch of the **Charpy energy vs. temperature curve** for a low-carbon steel. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A specimen of **mild steel** is tested in an **Izod impact testing machine**. The hammer has:
- Mass: $m = 20 \text{ kg}$
- Effective length: $R = 0.75 \text{ m}$
- Initial lift angle: $\alpha_1 = 160^\circ$
- Final swing angle: $\alpha_2 = 90^\circ$
- Specimen cross-section: $10 \text{ mm} \times 10 \text{ mm}$
- V-notch depth: $n = 2 \text{ mm}$

**Calculate the energy absorbed and the impact strength.** **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Definition:**

**Ductile-to-Brittle Transition Temperature (DBTT)** is the temperature below which a material (typically BCC metals like ferritic steels) fails in a brittle manner (cleavage fracture) with low energy absorption, and above which it fails in a ductile manner with high energy absorption.

**Charpy Energy vs. Temperature Curve (ASCII Schematic):**

```
  E (J)
   |
300| - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - -  Upper Shelf (Ductile)
   |                          _______
   |                    _____/
200|                  _/
   |                _/
   |              _/
100|            _/
   |          _/   ---- Transition Region ----
 50|        _/    T_50 = 50% Ductile, 50% Brittle
   |      _/
   |    _/
 10|  _/   - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - Lower Shelf (Brittle)
   |_/________________________________________
    -100   0    50   100   150    T (°C)
```

**Key features of the curve:**

1. **Upper Shelf Energy (USE):** The constant, high-energy region at high temperatures where failure is fully ductile. Energy is absorbed through void coalescence and microvoid formation.
2. **Lower Shelf Energy (LSE):** The constant, low-energy region at low temperatures where failure is fully brittle via transgranular cleavage.
3. **Transition Region:** The sigmoidal region where the fracture mode gradually shifts from cleavage to microvoid coalescence. $T_{50}$ (or DBTT) is conventionally the temperature corresponding to the **mean of (USE + LSE)**.
4. **Engineering significance:** Structures operating above $T_{50}$ are safe; those below are at risk of catastrophic brittle failure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical context for viva:** The **Liberty Ships** of WWII failed catastrophically in the North Atlantic because their A36 hull plates underwent brittle fracture below DBTT. This led to the modern mandatory Charpy testing for shipbuilding steels.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Given:**
- $m = 20 \text{ kg}$
- $R = 0.75 \text{ m}$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- $\alpha_1 = 160^\circ$
- $\alpha_2 = 90^\circ$

**Step 1: Compute cosines**

$$\cos(160^\circ) = -0.9397$$

$$\cos(90^\circ) = 0.0000$$

**Step 2: Apply the energy formula**

$$E_{abs} = mgR(\cos\alpha_2 - \cos\alpha_1)$$

$$E_{abs} = (20)(9.81)(0.75)\left[(0.0000) - (-0.9397)\right]$$

$$E_{abs} = 147.15 \times [0.9397]$$

$$E_{abs} = 138.27 \text{ J}$$

**Step 3: Net cross-sectional area** (same as Charpy: V-notch on 10×10)

$$A_{net} = 10 \times (10 - 2) = 80 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 4: Impact strength**

$$IS = \frac{138.27}{80}$$

$$\boxed{IS = 1.728 \text{ J/mm}^2 = 1728 \text{ kJ/m}^2}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #2:** Students often **forget the unit conversion** between $\text{J/mm}^2$ and $\text{kJ/m}^2$. Remember: $1 \text{ J/mm}^2 = 10^6 \text{ J/m}^2 = 10^3 \text{ kJ/m}^2$. State **both** units in your final answer to secure full marks. Also, ensure the pendulum mass is in **kg** (not grams) — a common slip.

---

## 5.3 Frequently Asked Viva Questions (Bonus)

1. **Why is the specimen notched?** — To simulate worst-case stress concentration and ensure brittle failure mode.
2. **What is the difference between toughness and strength?** — Strength is the stress a material can withstand; toughness is the energy absorbed before failure.
3. **Why does temperature affect impact energy in steels?** — Because it changes the fracture mode from ductile (high energy) to brittle (low energy).
4. **What does a "zero" reading on the dial indicate?** — Either the specimen did not break, or there is mechanical jamming.
5. **Why is impact strength not a true material property?** — Because it depends on specimen geometry, notch sharpness, and test conditions.
6. **What is the role of the drag pointer?** — It records the maximum angle reached by the pendulum after fracture.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Memorize Before Exam):**

- **Definition:** Notch toughness = energy absorbed by a notched specimen under impact loading.
- **Two Tests:** Izod (vertical cantilever) and Charpy (horizontal simply supported).
- **Standard Specimen:** $10 \text{ mm} \times 10 \text{ mm}$ cross-section, $55 \text{ mm}$ (Charpy) or $75 \text{ mm}$ (Izod) length, with a **2 mm deep V-notch** at 45° angle.
- **Master Formula:** $E_{abs} = mgR(\cos\alpha_2 - \cos\alpha_1)$ — derived from **conservation of energy**.
- **Impact Strength:** $IS = E_{abs} / A_{net}$, where $A_{net} = 10 \times 8 = 80 \text{ mm}^2$ for standard V-notch.
- **Units to remember:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$; $1 \text{ J/mm}^2 = 10^3 \text{ kJ/m}^2$.
- **DBTT:** A BCC-metal-specific phenomenon; FCC metals do not exhibit it at engineering temperatures.
- **Failure Modes:** Cleavage (brittle, shiny facets) vs. Microvoid coalescence (ductile, dull fibrous surface).
- **Standards:** IS 1757 (India) and ASTM E23 (International) govern specimen dimensions and test procedure.
- **Mandatory Repetition:** Always test **at least 3 specimens** and report the **mean** ± **standard deviation** for KTU lab reports.
- **Safety:** Always wear **safety goggles** and ensure the **enclosure guard is closed** before pendulum release.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Pro Tip for KTU 2024:** In the lab record, always include: (1) a neat labeled diagram, (2) the calculation of $E_{abs}$ from raw dial reading (with friction correction if specified), (3) computation of $IS$ with proper units, (4) a **Viva voce section** with at least 4–5 answers from the FAQ above, and (5) a brief discussion of fracture surface appearance. This format consistently fetches **full marks** in KTU practical evaluation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Investigate the Effect of Coil Type (Close-Coiled vs. Open-Coiled) and Arrangement (Series vs. Parallel) on Spring Stiffness
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUL407 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Investigate the Effect of Coil Type (Close-Coiled vs. Open-Coiled) and Arrangement (Series vs. Parallel) on Spring Stiffness

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

A **helical spring** is a flexible elastic object made by winding a wire into a tight or loose helix that can store mechanical energy when subjected to an axial load. The two principal classifications used in KTU Module Experiment 2 are:

- **Close-Coiled Helical Spring**: A spring in which the helix angle $\alpha$ (angle between the wire and a plane perpendicular to the spring axis) is very small, typically $\alpha < 10^{\circ}$. The coils are practically touching each other with negligible gap. Axial loading produces primarily **torsional shear stress** in the wire cross-section, while direct shear stress is neglected.

- **Open-Coiled Helical Spring**: A spring in which the helix angle is significant, typically $\alpha > 10^{\circ}$. The coils are visibly separated. Axial loading produces both **torsional shear stress** (due to the perpendicular force component $W\cos\alpha$) and **direct shear/tensile stress** (due to the parallel force component $W\sin\alpha$).

**Spring Stiffness ($k$)** is defined as the axial load required per unit axial deflection, expressed as:
$$k = \frac{W}{\delta} \quad \left[ \frac{\text{N}}{\text{m}} \right]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**: The stiffness $k$ is the **slope of the Load–Deflection curve**. Students must clearly distinguish that *close-coiled* springs have **lower stiffness** (more flexible) and *open-coiled* springs have **higher stiffness** (stiffer), as confirmed by $k = \dfrac{Gd^4}{8D^3 N}$ and $k_{open} = \dfrac{Gd^4}{8D^3 N \cos\alpha}$ respectively.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **steel measuring tape coiled in a circle**:
- If you stretch it gently, the thin steel bends easily — this is **close-coiled** behaviour (coils close, easily twisted).
- If you take the same tape and **stretch it straight** with gaps between the loops — now pulling it apart feels much harder because you are stretching the steel directly rather than twisting it — this is **open-coiled** behaviour.

For **arrangement**, think of two rubber bands:
- **In series** (one after the other): pulling stretches both sequentially, doubling the elongation for the same force → **lower combined stiffness**.
- **In parallel** (side by side): pulling stretches both at once but each carries half the load → **higher combined stiffness**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — Suspension System of a Motorcycle**
> A motorcycle's rear suspension often uses **two coil springs in parallel** to support a heavier load. When two passengers ride the bike, the parallel springs share the load, reducing the deflection by half — a practical demonstration of parallel spring stiffness addition.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Shear Modulus ($G$)** for spring steel: $G \approx 79.3 \text{ GPa} = 79.3 \times 10^3 \text{ N/mm}^2$ (**bold**)
- **Young's Modulus ($E$)** for spring steel: $E \approx 206 \text{ GPa}$
- **Standard Spring Index ($C = D/d$)**: Recommended range $4 \leq C \leq 12$
- **Poisson's ratio ($\nu$)** for steel: $\approx 0.29$
- **Wahl's correction factor ($K_w$)** accounts for curvature and direct shear at curved wires.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Force Resolution in a Helix:**
For a spring wound with helix angle $\alpha$ (measured from the plane perpendicular to the axis), an axial load $W$ resolves into:
- Force perpendicular to wire (creates torque): $W\cos\alpha$
- Force parallel to wire (creates direct tension/compression): $W\sin\alpha$

**Step 2 — Torque on Wire Cross-Section:**
The torque acting about the longitudinal axis of the wire is:
$$T = W \cdot R \cdot \cos\alpha = \frac{WD\cos\alpha}{2}$$
where $R = D/2$ is the mean coil radius.

**Step 3 — Stress Generation:**
For a circular wire cross-section (polar moment of inertia $J = \pi d^4/32$):
- Torsional shear stress: $\tau_t = \dfrac{16T}{\pi d^3} = \dfrac{8WD\cos\alpha}{\pi d^3}$
- Direct shear stress: $\tau_s = \dfrac{4W\sin\alpha}{\pi d^2}$

**Step 4 — Torsional Rigidity and Deflection:**
Using torsion equation $\dfrac{T}{J} = \dfrac{G\theta}{L}$, the angular twist per unit length is $\theta = \dfrac{32T}{G\pi d^4}$. For $N$ active turns, the total wire length is $L = \pi DN/\cos\alpha$, and the cumulative twist gives the axial deflection via $\delta = R\phi$.

**Step 5 — Stiffness Assembly:**
Combining $\delta = \dfrac{W}{k}$ with the closed-form deflection gives the final stiffness expressions shown in the cheat-sheet below.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does helix angle matter?** As $\alpha \to 0$ (close-coiled), $\cos\alpha \to 1$ and the spring becomes maximally flexible (only torsion acts). As $\alpha \to 90^{\circ}$ (vertical wire), $\cos\alpha \to 0$ and stiffness theoretically diverges — the spring behaves like a solid bar in direct compression.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

#### Table 2A — Spring Geometry & Material Parameters

| Parameter | Symbol | Formula / Standard Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mean Coil Diameter | $D$ | Direct measurement | mm |
| Wire Diameter | $d$ | Direct measurement | mm |
| Spring Index | $C$ | $C = D/d$ | dimensionless |
| Active Coils | $N$ | Counted directly | — |
| Helix Angle | $\alpha$ | $\tan\alpha = \dfrac{\text{pitch}}{\pi D}$ | degrees or rad |
| Shear Modulus | $G$ | $79.3 \times 10^3$ (steel) | N/mm² |
| Polar Moment of Inertia | $J$ | $\pi d^4 / 32$ | mm⁴ |

#### Table 2B — Core Formulas: Close-Coiled vs Open-Coiled

| Quantity | Close-Coiled ($\alpha \to 0$) | Open-Coiled (general $\alpha$) | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Deflection $\delta$ | $\dfrac{8WD^3 N}{G d^4}$ | $\dfrac{8WD^3 N \cos\alpha}{G d^4}$ | mm |
| Stiffness $k$ | $\dfrac{G d^4}{8 D^3 N}$ | $\dfrac{G d^4}{8 D^3 N \cos\alpha}$ | N/mm |
| Max Shear Stress $\tau_{max}$ | $\dfrac{8WD}{\pi d^3}$ | $\dfrac{8WD \cos\alpha}{\pi d^3} + \dfrac{4W \sin\alpha}{\pi d^2}$ | N/mm² |
| Wahl Factor $K_w$ | $\dfrac{4C-1}{4C-4} + \dfrac{0.615}{C}$ | Same (independent of $\alpha$) | — |
| Corrected Stress | $\tau_{max} \cdot K_w$ | $\tau_{max} \cdot K_w$ | N/mm² |

#### Table 2C — Series vs Parallel Arrangement (Two Springs)

| Property | Series Arrangement | Parallel Arrangement |
|---|---|---|
| Force | Same through both: $W_1 = W_2 = W$ | Deflection same: $\delta_1 = \delta_2 = \delta$ |
| Deflection | $\delta_{total} = \delta_1 + \delta_2$ | $\delta_{total} = \delta_1 = \delta_2$ |
| Load sharing | $W_1 = k_1 \delta_1$, $W_2 = k_2 \delta_2$ | $W_1 = k_1 \delta$, $W_2 = k_2 \delta$ |
| Equivalent Stiffness $k_{eq}$ | $\dfrac{1}{k_{eq}} = \dfrac{1}{k_1} + \dfrac{1}{k_2}$ $\Rightarrow k_{eq} = \dfrac{k_1 k_2}{k_1 + k_2}$ | $k_{eq} = k_1 + k_2$ |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Suspensions**: Multiple helical springs in parallel combine to support heavier loads without increasing deflection.
- **Railway Buffers**: Nested springs (a form of series arrangement) provide progressive stiffness for impact absorption.
- **Valve Springs in Engines**: Close-coiled springs with high stiffness maintain valve closure at high RPM.
- **Precision Balances**: Spring stiffness determines the sensitivity of weighing scales.
- **Shock Absorbers**: Series–parallel hybrid arrangements give non-linear force–deflection characteristics ideal for crash protection.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Deflection & Stiffness of a Close-Coiled Helical Spring

**Given:**
- Axial load $W$ (N)
- Mean coil diameter $D$ (mm), so mean radius $R = D/2$
- Wire diameter $d$ (mm)
- Number of active coils $N$
- Shear modulus $G$ (N/mm²)

**Step 1 — Identify the loading on the wire:**
The wire is wound into a helix. When an axial load $W$ is applied, each cross-section of the wire experiences a torque about its own longitudinal axis:
$$T = W \times R = \frac{W \cdot D}{2}$$
This is because the force $W$ acts at a perpendicular distance $R$ from the wire axis, creating a pure twisting moment.

**Step 2 — Compute total wire length:**
For $N$ complete turns, the length of wire $L$ traversed along the helix is:
$$L = \pi D N$$
(since one full turn traces a circle of circumference $\pi D$, and the pitch contribution is neglected for close-coiled springs where $\alpha \approx 0$).

**Step 3 — Apply torsion equation to find angular twist:**
The torsion equation gives the rate of twist per unit length:
$$\frac{T}{J} = \frac{G \theta}{L_{wire}}$$
where $J = \dfrac{\pi d^4}{32}$ is the polar moment of inertia of the circular wire cross-section, and $\theta$ is the angle of twist (radians) over the wire length $L_{wire}$.

Solving for total angle of twist $\phi$ over the entire wire length $L = \pi D N$:
$$\phi = \frac{T \cdot L}{G \cdot J} = \frac{(WD/2) \cdot (\pi D N)}{G \cdot (\pi d^4 / 32)}$$

$$\phi = \frac{WD/2 \cdot \pi D N \cdot 32}{G \pi d^4} = \frac{16 W D^2 N}{G d^4}$$

**Step 4 — Convert angular twist to axial deflection:**
When the wire twists by an angle $\phi$ (in radians) over the full spring length, the free end of the spring moves axially by a distance $\delta$. The geometry of the helix gives:
$$\delta = R \cdot \phi = \frac{D}{2} \cdot \phi$$

Substituting the expression for $\phi$:
$$\delta = \frac{D}{2} \cdot \frac{16 W D^2 N}{G d^4} = \frac{8 W D^3 N}{G d^4}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for stiffness:**
By definition $k = W/\delta$:
$$k = \frac{W}{\delta} = \frac{W \cdot G d^4}{8 W D^3 N} = \frac{G d^4}{8 D^3 N}$$

$$\boxed{\,k_{close} = \frac{G d^4}{8 D^3 N} \quad ; \quad \delta_{close} = \frac{8 W D^3 N}{G d^4}\,}$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Deflection & Stiffness of an Open-Coiled Helical Spring

**Given:** Same as above, plus a finite helix angle $\alpha$.

**Step 1 — Resolve the axial force $W$ along and perpendicular to the wire:**
$$W_\parallel = W \sin\alpha \quad (\text{creates direct tension in wire})$$
$$W_\perp = W \cos\alpha \quad (\text{creates torque about wire axis})$$

**Step 2 — Compute the torque on the wire cross-section:**
$$T = W_\perp \cdot R = \frac{W D \cos\alpha}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the wire length (accounting for helix pitch):**
One full helix turn has length $\pi D / \cos\alpha$, so for $N$ turns:
$$L_{wire} = \frac{\pi D N}{\cos\alpha}$$

**Step 4 — Apply torsion equation:**
$$\phi = \frac{T \cdot L_{wire}}{G \cdot J} = \frac{(WD\cos\alpha/2) \cdot (\pi D N / \cos\alpha)}{G \cdot (\pi d^4 / 32)}$$

$$\phi = \frac{WD\cos\alpha \cdot \pi D N \cdot 32}{2 \cos\alpha \cdot G \pi d^4} = \frac{16 W D^2 N}{G d^4}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to axial deflection:**
$$\delta_{open} = R \cdot \phi = \frac{D}{2} \cdot \frac{16 W D^2 N}{G d^4} = \frac{8 W D^3 N \cos\alpha}{G d^4}$$

**Step 6 — Stiffness expression:**
$$k_{open} = \frac{W}{\delta_{open}} = \frac{G d^4}{8 D^3 N \cos\alpha}$$

$$\boxed{\,k_{open} = \frac{G d^4}{8 D^3 N \cos\alpha} \quad ; \quad \delta_{open} = \frac{8 W D^3 N \cos\alpha}{G d^4}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key observation**: Since $0 < \cos\alpha < 1$ for $\alpha \in (0, 90^{\circ})$, we have $k_{open} > k_{close}$. **Open-coiled springs are stiffer** because a portion of the axial load directly stretches/compresses the wire (high axial stiffness of a bar), whereas in a close-coiled spring the load primarily twists the wire (much lower torsional rigidity).

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Equivalent Stiffness in Series Arrangement

**Given:** Two springs with individual stiffnesses $k_1$ and $k_2$ connected end-to-end, loaded by axial force $W$.

**Step 1 — Equilibrium gives same force in both springs:**
By Newton's third law and the series connection geometry, the same axial force $W$ acts through both springs:
$$W_1 = W_2 = W$$

**Step 2 — Compute individual deflections using Hooke's law:**
$$\delta_1 = \frac{W}{k_1} \quad , \quad \delta_2 = \frac{W}{k_2}$$

**Step 3 — Total deflection is the algebraic sum (in series, deflections add):**
$$\delta_{total} = \delta_1 + \delta_2 = \frac{W}{k_1} + \frac{W}{k_2} = W \left( \frac{1}{k_1} + \frac{1}{k_2} \right)$$

**Step 4 — By definition of equivalent stiffness:**
$$k_{eq} = \frac{W}{\delta_{total}} = \frac{W}{W(1/k_1 + 1/k_2)} = \frac{1}{1/k_1 + 1/k_2}$$

$$\boxed{\,k_{eq, \, series} = \frac{k_1 k_2}{k_1 + k_2}\,}$$

For $n$ springs in series: $\dfrac{1}{k_{eq}} = \displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{1}{k_i}$

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Equivalent Stiffness in Parallel Arrangement

**Given:** Two springs with stiffnesses $k_1$ and $k_2$ mounted side-by-side, sharing the same deflection.

**Step 1 — Compatibility gives same deflection in both springs:**
Since both springs are mounted between the same two rigid plates, both compress by the same amount:
$$\delta_1 = \delta_2 = \delta$$

**Step 2 — Compute individual forces using Hooke's law:**
$$W_1 = k_1 \delta \quad , \quad W_2 = k_2 \delta$$

**Step 3 — Total load is the algebraic sum (forces add):**
$$W_{total} = W_1 + W_2 = (k_1 + k_2) \delta$$

**Step 4 — By definition of equivalent stiffness:**
$$k_{eq} = \frac{W_{total}}{\delta} = \frac{(k_1 + k_2) \delta}{\delta} = k_1 + k_2$$

$$\boxed{\,k_{eq, \, parallel} = k_1 + k_2\,}$$

For $n$ springs in parallel: $k_{eq} = \displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} k_i$

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Examples

#### Example A — Close-Coiled Spring
A close-coiled spring has $G = 79.3 \times 10^3 \text{ N/mm}^2$, $d = 5 \text{ mm}$, $D = 50 \text{ mm}$, $N = 10$. Find stiffness under load $W = 100$ N.

**Step 1 — Substitute into formula:**
$$k = \frac{G d^4}{8 D^3 N} = \frac{79.3 \times 10^3 \times (5)^4}{8 \times (50)^3 \times 10}$$

$$k = \frac{79300 \times 625}{8 \times 125000 \times 10} = \frac{49562500}{10000000} = 4.956 \text{ N/mm}$$

**Step 2 — Deflection:**
$$\delta = \frac{W}{k} = \frac{100}{4.956} \approx 20.18 \text{ mm}$$

#### Example B — Open-Coiled Spring with $\alpha = 15^{\circ}$
Same dimensions, but helix angle $= 15^{\circ}$.

**Step 1 — Compute $\cos(15^{\circ}) = 0.9659$**

**Step 2 — Stiffness:**
$$k_{open} = \frac{4.956}{0.9659} \approx 5.131 \text{ N/mm}$$

**Step 3 — Deflection:**
$$\delta_{open} = \frac{100}{5.131} \approx 19.49 \text{ mm}$$

**Observation:** The open-coiled spring deflects less (19.49 mm vs 20.18 mm) for the same load, confirming higher stiffness.

#### Example C — Series Arrangement
Springs with $k_1 = 4 \text{ N/mm}$, $k_2 = 6 \text{ N/mm}$ in series. Load $W = 60$ N.

**Step 1 — Equivalent stiffness:**
$$k_{eq} = \frac{k_1 k_2}{k_1 + k_2} = \frac{4 \times 6}{4 + 6} = \frac{24}{10} = 2.4 \text{ N/mm}$$

**Step 2 — Total deflection:**
$$\delta_{total} = \frac{W}{k_{eq}} = \frac{60}{2.4} = 25 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Individual deflections (since $W_1 = W_2 = 60$ N):**
$$\delta_1 = \frac{60}{4} = 15 \text{ mm} \quad ; \quad \delta_2 = \frac{60}{6} = 10 \text{ mm}$$

**Check:** $\delta_1 + \delta_2 = 25$ mm = $\delta_{total}$ ✓

#### Example D — Parallel Arrangement
Same springs in parallel. Load $W = 60$ N.

**Step 1 — Equivalent stiffness:**
$$k_{eq} = k_1 + k_2 = 4 + 6 = 10 \text{ N/mm}$$

**Step 2 — Common deflection:**
$$\delta = \frac{W}{k_{eq}} = \frac{60}{10} = 6 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Individual loads:**
$$W_1 = k_1 \delta = 4 \times 6 = 24 \text{ N} \quad ; \quad W_2 = k_2 \delta = 6 \times 6 = 36 \text{ N}$$

**Check:** $W_1 + W_2 = 60$ N ✓

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Spring Classification Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[SPRING TYPES UNDER STUDY] --> B1[Helical Springs]
    A1 --> B2[Non-Helical Springs]
    B1 --> C1[Close Coiled]
    B1 --> C2[Open Coiled]
    C1 --> D1[Helix Angle less than 10 deg]
    C1 --> D2[Only Torsional Shear Stress]
    C1 --> D3[Lower Stiffness k close]
    C2 --> E1[Helix Angle 10 to 30 deg]
    C2 --> E2[Torsional plus Direct Shear]
    C2 --> E3[Higher Stiffness k open]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Arrangement of Springs

```mermaid
graph LR
    F1[SPRING ARRANGEMENTS] --> G1[Series Arrangement]
    F1 --> G2[Parallel Arrangement]
    G1 --> H1[Same Force W through all springs]
    G1 --> H2[Deflections add up]
    G1 --> H3[Equivalent stiffness k eq]
    G2 --> I1[Same deflection delta across all springs]
    G2 --> I2[Forces add up]
    G2 --> I3[Equivalent stiffness k eq sum]
    H3 --> J1[k eq equals k1 k2 divided by k1 plus k2]
    I3 --> J2[k eq equals k1 plus k2]
```

```mermaid
    N1 --> O2[Test 4 Parallel Arrangement]
    N2 --> O1
    N2 --> O2
    O1 --> P1[Plot Load vs Deflection Curve]
    O2 --> P1

```

```mermaid
graph LR
    R1[Stiffness Hierarchy Observed] --> S1[Highest: Open Coiled in Parallel]
    R1 --> S2[Mid High: Close Coiled in Parallel]
    R1 --> S3[Mid Low: Open Coiled in Series]
    R1 --> S4[Lowest: Close Coiled in Series]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question A.1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define spring stiffness. Explain how the stiffness of a close-coiled helical spring differs from that of an open-coiled helical spring.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Spring stiffness $k$ is defined as the axial load required to produce a unit axial deflection in a spring, expressed as $k = W/\delta$ with SI unit N/m.

For a close-coiled helical spring:
$$k_{close} = \frac{G d^4}{8 D^3 N}$$

For an open-coiled helical spring with helix angle $\alpha$:
$$k_{open} = \frac{G d^4}{8 D^3 N \cos\alpha}$$

Since $\cos\alpha < 1$ for $\alpha > 0$, the open-coiled spring has **higher stiffness** than the close-coiled spring. This is because, in an open-coiled spring, part of the axial load acts directly along the wire (direct tension), which adds to the torsional resistance. In a close-coiled spring, the load acts almost entirely as torque on the wire, producing only torsional deformation, which is much more flexible. **(3 marks)**

#### Question A.2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between series and parallel arrangements of springs. State the formula for equivalent stiffness in each case (for two springs).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Property | Series Arrangement | Parallel Arrangement |
|---|---|---|
| Force | Same $W$ through both springs | Different $W_1$, $W_2$ shared |
| Deflection | $\delta_{total} = \delta_1 + \delta_2$ | $\delta_{total} = \delta_1 = \delta_2$ |
| Equivalent Stiffness | $k_{eq} = \dfrac{k_1 k_2}{k_1 + k_2}$ | $k_{eq} = k_1 + k_2$ |

In series, springs share the load but deflections add up — softer overall behaviour. In parallel, springs share the deflection but forces add up — stiffer overall behaviour. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each) with Internal Choice

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the deflection and stiffness of a close-coiled helical spring subjected to an axial load $W$. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions:**
1. The helix angle is negligibly small (close-coiled condition), so the wire is assumed to lie in a plane perpendicular to the spring axis at every section.
2. The shear stress due to direct shear is neglected compared to the torsional shear stress.
3. The effect of curvature of the wire on stress (Wahl's factor) is initially neglected.
4. The wire material is homogeneous, isotropic, and obeys Hooke's law.

**Derivation:**

Consider a close-coiled helical spring with mean coil diameter $D$, wire diameter $d$, and $N$ active coils. Apply an axial tensile load $W$.

**Step 1:** The force $W$ acting at the mean radius $R = D/2$ creates a torque on the wire cross-section:
$$T = W \cdot R = \frac{WD}{2}$$

**Step 2:** The total length of wire for $N$ turns:
$$L = \pi D N$$

**Step 3:** Using the torsion equation $\dfrac{T}{J} = \dfrac{G\theta}{L}$ with $J = \dfrac{\pi d^4}{32}$:
$$\phi = \frac{T \cdot L}{G \cdot J} = \frac{(WD/2)(\pi D N)}{G(\pi d^4/32)} = \frac{16 W D^2 N}{G d^4}$$

**Step 4:** The axial deflection $\delta$ is related to angular twist by $\delta = R\phi$:
$$\delta = \frac{D}{2} \cdot \frac{16 W D^2 N}{G d^4} = \frac{8 W D^3 N}{G d^4}$$

**Step 5:** Stiffness:
$$k = \frac{W}{\delta} = \frac{G d^4}{8 D^3 N}$$

**(b)** Two close-coiled helical springs, A and B, are connected in series. Spring A has a stiffness of $k_A = 10 \text{ N/mm}$ and Spring B has $k_B = 15 \text{ N/mm}$. An axial load of $W = 150$ N is applied. Find: **(i)** equivalent stiffness, **(ii)** deflection of each spring, and **(iii)** load shared by each spring. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $k_A = 10$ N/mm, $k_B = 15$ N/mm, $W = 150$ N.

**(i) Equivalent stiffness in series:**
$$k_{eq} = \frac{k_A \cdot k_B}{k_A + k_B} = \frac{10 \times 15}{10 + 15} = \frac{150}{25} = 6 \text{ N/mm}$$

**(ii) Total deflection:**
$$\delta_{total} = \frac{W}{k_{eq}} = \frac{150}{6} = 25 \text{ mm}$$

Since springs are in series, the same force $W = 150$ N passes through both springs:
$$\delta_A = \frac{W}{k_A} = \frac{150}{10} = 15 \text{ mm}$$
$$\delta_B = \frac{W}{k_B} = \frac{150}{15} = 10 \text{ mm}$$

**Check:** $\delta_A + \delta_B = 15 + 10 = 25$ mm = $\delta_{total}$ ✓

**(iii) Load shared:** In series, $W_A = W_B = W = 150$ N (same force passes through both springs).

**Final Answers:** $k_{eq} = 6$ N/mm, $\delta_A = 15$ mm, $\delta_B = 10$ mm, $W_A = W_B = 150$ N. **(7 marks)**

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] — INTERNAL CHOICE

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the stress distribution in the wire cross-section of a close-coiled helical spring. Derive the maximum shear stress formula, including the Wahl's correction factor. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

When an axial load $W$ is applied to a close-coiled spring, the wire cross-section experiences two types of stresses:

**Stress components:**
1. **Torsional shear stress** $\tau_t$ due to torque $T = WD/2$:
$$\tau_t = \frac{16T}{\pi d^3} = \frac{16 \cdot (WD/2)}{\pi d^3} = \frac{8WD}{\pi d^3}$$

2. **Direct shear stress** $\tau_s$ due to axial load $W$:
$$\tau_s = \frac{W}{A} = \frac{W}{\pi d^2/4} = \frac{4W}{\pi d^2}$$

The torsional shear stress is maximum at the outer fibre of the wire (point on the wire cross-section farthest from the wire axis), while the direct shear stress is uniform across the section.

**Maximum shear stress (without correction):**
$$\tau_{max} = \tau_t + \tau_s = \frac{8WD}{\pi d^3} + \frac{4W}{\pi d^2} = \frac{8WD}{\pi d^3}\left(1 + \frac{d}{2D}\right) = \frac{8WD}{\pi d^3}\left(\frac{2C + 1}{2C}\right)$$
where $C = D/d$ is the spring index.

**Wahl's correction factor:**
Due to curvature of the wire, additional stress concentration occurs at the inner fibre. Wahl's factor accounts for this:
$$K_w = \frac{4C - 1}{4C - 4} + \frac{0.615}{C}$$

**Maximum shear stress with Wahl's correction:**
$$\tau_{max, \, corrected} = K_w \cdot \tau_t = \frac{K_w \cdot 8WD}{\pi d^3}$$

**(b)** Two close-coiled helical springs with stiffness $k_1 = 200 \text{ N/mm}$ and $k_2 = 300 \text{ N/mm}$ are connected in parallel. A total axial load of $W = 1500$ N is applied. Find: **(i)** equivalent stiffness, **(ii)** deflection, and **(iii)** load shared by each spring. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $k_1 = 200$ N/mm, $k_2 = 300$ N/mm, $W = 1500$ N, parallel arrangement.

**(i) Equivalent stiffness in parallel:**
$$k_{eq} = k_1 + k_2 = 200 + 300 = 500 \text{ N/mm}$$

**(ii) Common deflection:**
$$\delta = \frac{W}{k_{eq}} = \frac{1500}{500} = 3 \text{ mm}$$

**(iii) Load shared by each spring:**
$$W_1 = k_1 \cdot \delta = 200 \times 3 = 600 \text{ N}$$
$$W_2 = k_2 \cdot \delta = 300 \times 3 = 900 \text{ N}$$

**Check:** $W_1 + W_2 = 600 + 900 = 1500$ N = $W$ ✓

**Final Answers:** $k_{eq} = 500$ N/mm, $\delta = 3$ mm, $W_1 = 600$ N, $W_2 = 900$ N. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Marks Are Lost:**
> 1. **Confusing series/parallel formulas** — most common error. Remember: series = softer (inverse sum), parallel = stiffer (direct sum).
> 2. **Unit conversion** — students often mix cm and mm. Always convert to a single unit (preferably mm) before substituting.
> 3. **Forgetting Wahl's factor** in stress calculations. $K_w \geq 1$ and increases as $C \to 4$.
> 4. **Using $\alpha$ in degrees instead of radians** in trigonometric calculations. Always check.
> 5. **Forgetting active coils vs total coils** — inactive (end) coils do not contribute to deflection; use only active $N$ in $k$ formula.
> 6. **In series arrangement**, students wrongly assume each spring carries $W/2$; the correct statement is $W_1 = W_2 = W$ (same load).
> 7. **In parallel arrangement**, students wrongly assume each spring deflects by $W/(2k)$; the correct statement is $\delta_1 = \delta_2 = \delta_{total}$ (same deflection).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Spring stiffness** is the slope of the Load–Deflection graph; higher slope = stiffer spring.
- **Close-coiled spring** ($k_{close} = Gd^4/(8D^3N)$): helix angle $\alpha < 10^{\circ}$, flexible, only torsional shear stress.
- **Open-coiled spring** ($k_{open} = Gd^4/(8D^3N\cos\alpha)$): helix angle $\alpha > 10^{\circ}$, stiffer, both torsional and direct shear stress.
- **Series arrangement**: $k_{eq} = k_1 k_2 / (k_1 + k_2)$; same force, deflections add.
- **Parallel arrangement**: $k_{eq} = k_1 + k_2$; same deflection, forces add.
- **Stiffness ranking** (highest to lowest): Open-coiled Parallel > Close-coiled Parallel > Open-coiled Series > Close-coiled Series.
- **Wire diameter** $d$ has the **highest influence** on $k$ (fourth power dependence).
- **Mean coil diameter** $D$ has a **cubic inverse** effect on $k$.
- **Spring index** $C = D/d$ should be in the range $4 \leq C \leq 12$ for practical springs.
- **Wahl's factor** $K_w = (4C-1)/(4C-4) + 0.615/C$ corrects for wire curvature stress concentration.
- **Stiffness is inversely proportional to the number of active coils** $N$.
- **Experimental verification**: The slope of the load–deflection graph plotted from measured data should match the theoretical $k$ within $\pm 5\%$ tolerance.
- **Units**: Always use $G$ in N/mm² and dimensions in mm to get $k$ in N/mm directly.
- **Direct shear contribution** is usually small (about $d/2D$ times the torsional shear) and is often neglected for close-coiled springs.
- **Active vs inactive coils**: The two end coils are usually considered inactive; total coil count minus 2 (for plain ends) gives active $N$.
- **Experimental observation**: Open-coiled springs show a **steeper load–deflection curve**; parallel arrangement shows a **steeper combined curve**; series shows a **flatter combined curve** than individual springs.
- **KTU 2024 emphasis**: Numerical problems from this topic frequently appear in Part B with 7-mark sub-parts combining derivation + numerical application.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Perform a Bending Test on a Wooden Beam to Assess Its Load-Carrying Capacity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMUL407 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Perform a Bending Test on a Wooden Beam to Assess Its Load-Carrying Capacity ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) The **Bending Test on a Wooden Beam** is a standard **Strength of Materials Laboratory (Solids Lab)** experiment conducted to determine the **Flexural Strength (Modulus of Rupture)**, **Young's Modulus of Elasticity (E)** in bending, and the **Load-Deflection behavior** of a timber specimen under transverse loading. The test is performed on a **simply supported beam** subjected to a gradually increasing **central point load** (or two-point loading for symmetric pure bending) until failure occurs. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Note (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Although the parent course is *Fluid Mechanics & Hydraulic Machines Lab*, the structural testing of beams is frequently co-listed under **PES/MES/PMS Labs (Strength of Materials Lab)** in the 2024 scheme. The theoretical treatment follows **IS 3629-1986** (Specification for Structural Timber) and **IS 1708-1986** (Methods of Testing Small Clear Specimens of Timber). > [!NOTE] > **Key Engineering Properties Determined:** > - **Modulus of Elasticity (E)** in bending → Stiffness indicator > - **Modulus of Rupture (MOR)** → Ultimate flexural strength > - **Fiber Stress at Proportional Limit (FSPL)** → Elastic limit behavior > - **Load-Deflection Curve** → Ductility/Brittleness assessment --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are standing in the middle of a wooden plank that bridges a small ditch. As you walk to its center, the plank **bends downward** in the middle. The amount it bends depends on three things: 1. **How heavy you are** (the **Load**, *P*) 2. **How long the plank is** between the ditch banks (the **Span**, *L*) 3. **How thick the plank is** (the **Cross-section**, *b × d*) Now imagine doing this in a controlled laboratory, where we slowly add weights and carefully measure how much the wood dips (deflects) at every step. That is exactly what a bending test is — a controlled simulation of "someone standing on a wooden plank." > [!TIP] > **Plain English Summary:** The test answers two questions — *"How much can this wooden beam carry before it snaps?"* (Strength) and *"How much will it sag under a given load before breaking?"* (Stiffness). --- ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Parameter | Standard Value / Unit | |---|---| | Standard span-to-depth ratio | **L/d = 14 to 16** (IS 1708-1986) | | Typical specimen cross-section | **50 mm × 50 mm** to **100 mm × 100 mm** | | Standard span length | **300 mm, 600 mm, 700 mm** | | Acceleration due to gravity | **g = 9.81 m/s²** | | Density of typical wood (Teak) | **600–750 kg/m³** | | Modulus of Elasticity (Teak) | **~ 9–13 GPa** | --- ### 1.4 Aim of the Experiment > [!IMPORTANT] > **Stated Aim:** To conduct a **three-point bending test** (or **four-point bending test**) on a wooden beam specimen and to evaluate its **flexural strength (Modulus of Rupture)** and **flexural rigidity (Young's Modulus)** from the experimental load-deflection data. ### 1.5 Apparatus Required - **Universal Testing Machine (UTM)** of capacity 400 kN / 1000 kN — *or* a dedicated **Beam Testing Apparatus** - Wooden beam specimen (seasoned, defect-free timber — usually *Teak, Mango, or Pine*) - **Two knife-edge supports** (roller-type to allow free axial movement) - **Loading nose (central knife edge)** with provision for applying load at mid-span - **Dial Gauge** of least count **0.01 mm** (or **LVDT** of least count **0.001 mm**) - **Vernier Caliper** (least count **0.02 mm**) for cross-section measurement - **Measuring tape / scale** for span length - Weights / hydraulic loading system - Pencil, graph sheet, and stopwatch ### 1.6 Specimen Preparation (As per IS 1708) - The specimen must be **straight-grained**, free from knots, splits, or decay. - Cross-section dimensions: **b × d = 50 × 50 mm** (preferred) or as per standard. - Length of specimen: **L + 150 mm** to allow overhang of 75 mm on each side. - Specimen conditioned at **27°C ± 2°C** and **65% ± 5% relative humidity** until **constant mass** is attained. > [!CAUTION] > **KTU Lab Safety:** Always wear safety goggles during testing. Sudden fracture of wood can launch splinters at high velocity. Keep hands clear of the loading zone. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Pure Bending Theory When a simply supported beam is loaded transversely, it experiences: - **Bending Moment (M)** → Causes bending stresses - **Shear Force (V)** → Causes shear stresses (negligible for slender beams) The **Euler-Bernoulli Beam Theory** (applicable for **L/d > 20**) gives: $$\sigma = \frac{M \cdot y}{I}$$ Where: - $\sigma$ = Bending stress at distance $y$ from neutral axis - $M$ = Bending moment at the section - $y$ = Distance from neutral axis - $I$ = Second moment of area ($I = \frac{bd^3}{12}$ for rectangular section) --- ### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of the Test **Step 1 — Specimen Setup:** The wooden beam is placed on **two roller supports** separated by an **effective span L**. A **central loading nose** is positioned at mid-span. **Step 2 — Initial Readings:** - Measure **b** (width) and **d** (depth) at **three locations** (near supports and mid-span) and take the average. - Set the **dial gauge** to zero at the mid-span (unloaded condition). **Step 3 — Incremental Loading:** - Apply load in **equal increments** (typically **50 N, 100 N, or 200 N** depending on capacity). - Wait **30 seconds to 1 minute** for stabilization. - Record the **deflection** corresponding to each load step. **Step 4 — Continuous Loading Until Failure:** - Continue loading until the specimen **fractures** or shows visible damage. - Note the **failure load (P_max)**. **Step 5 — Plotting:** - Draw a **Load (P) vs Deflection (δ)** graph. - Determine the **slope** of the linear elastic portion. **Step 6 — Calculations:** - Compute **Modulus of Elasticity (E)** from the linear region. - Compute **Modulus of Rupture (MOR)** from the failure load. --- ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table contains **every formula** you need for the KTU lab examination on this topic. Memorize the boxed entries — they appear in **almost every past paper**. | Sl. No. | Parameter | Formula | Description | |:---:|:---|:---|:---| | 1 | Max. Bending Moment (Central Point Load) | $M = \dfrac{P \cdot L}{4}$ | $P$ = load at mid-span, $L$ = effective span | | 2 | Max. Bending Moment (Two-Point Load, 4-Point) | $M = \dfrac{P \cdot a}{2}$ | $a$ = shear span (distance from support to nearest load point) | | 3 | Section Modulus (Rectangular) | $Z = \dfrac{b \cdot d^2}{6}$ | $b$ = width, $d$ = depth | | 4 | Second Moment of Area | $I = \dfrac{b \cdot d^3}{12}$ | For rectangular cross-section | | 5 | Bending Stress (Flexural) | $\sigma = \dfrac{M}{Z} = \dfrac{3PL}{2bd^2}$ | Maximum fiber stress | | 6 | Modulus of Rupture (MOR) | $MOR = \dfrac{3 P_{max} \cdot L}{2 \cdot b \cdot d^2}$ | At failure load $P_{max}$ | | 7 | Max. Deflection (Central Load) | $\delta_{max} = \dfrac{P \cdot L^3}{48 \cdot E \cdot I}$ | Theoretical elastic deflection | | 8 | Young's Modulus (E) — Central Load | $E = \dfrac{P \cdot L^3}{4 \cdot \delta \cdot b \cdot d^3}$ | Derived from $\delta = \dfrac{PL^3}{4Ebd^3}$ | | 9 | Young's Modulus (E) — From Slope | $E = \dfrac{m \cdot L^3}{4 \cdot b \cdot d^3}$ | Where $m = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\Delta \delta}$ (slope of P-δ graph) | | 10 | Fiber Stress at Proportional Limit | $FSPL = \dfrac{3 P_{pl} \cdot L}{2 \cdot b \cdot d^2}$ | $P_{pl}$ = load at proportional limit | | 11 | Span-to-Depth Ratio (Standard) | $\dfrac{L}{d} = 14 \text{ to } 16$ | As per IS 1708-1986 | | 12 | Curvature Relation | $\dfrac{1}{R} = \dfrac{M}{EI}$ | Radius of curvature of bent beam | > [!NOTE] > **The 'Why' Behind The Formulas:** > - **M = PL/4** because for a simply supported beam with central point load, the reactions are P/2 each, and the maximum moment occurs directly under the load at mid-span. > - **I = bd³/12** comes from integrating $y^2 \, dA$ over the rectangular cross-section. > - **δ = PL³/(4Ebd³)** comes from double integration of the Euler-Bernoulli equation $EI \cdot \dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2} = M(x)$. --- ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Civil Engineering:** Design of roof beams, floor joists, formwork timbers, scaffolding planks, and railway sleepers. - **Mechanical Engineering:** Design of wooden tool handles, wooden gears, and wooden structural components. - **Aerospace & Marine:** Plywood / laminated wood components in aircraft interiors and boat hulls. - **Architecture:** Restoration of heritage wooden structures (temples, palaces) requires MOR and E testing of original timber. - **Furniture Industry:** Quality control of load-bearing furniture (chairs, tables, beds). > [!TIP] > **KTU Examiner's Note:** Always convert all dimensions to a **consistent unit system**. Use **mm** for length, **N** for load, and report **E in N/mm² (MPa)** and **MOR in N/mm² (MPa)**. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Maximum Bending Moment for Simply Supported Beam with Central Point Load **Given:** A simply supported wooden beam of effective span $L$ subjected to a concentrated load $P$ at mid-span. **Step 1 — Reactions at Supports:** By symmetry of loading and geometry: $$R_A = R_B = \frac{P}{2}$$ **Step 2 — Bending Moment Equation (Left Half, 0 ≤ x ≤ L/2):** $$M(x) = R_A \cdot x = \frac{P}{2} \cdot x$$ **Step 3 — Maximum Bending Moment:** Maximum moment occurs at mid-span ($x = L/2$): $$M_{max} = \frac{P}{2} \cdot \frac{L}{2} = \frac{P \cdot L}{4}$$ $$\boxed{M_{max} = \frac{P \cdot L}{4}}$$ --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Maximum Bending Stress (Flexural Stress Formula) **Step 1 — Pure Bending Equation:** $$\frac{M}{I} = \frac{\sigma}{y} = \frac{E}{R}$$ Therefore, at the extreme fiber ($y = d/2$): $$\sigma_{max} = \frac{M \cdot (d/2)}{I} = \frac{M \cdot d}{2I}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute $M = PL/4$ and $I = bd^3/12$:** $$\sigma_{max} = \frac{(PL/4) \cdot (d/2)}{bd^3/12} = \frac{PLd}{8} \cdot \frac{12}{bd^3}$$ $$\sigma_{max} = \frac{12 \cdot P \cdot L \cdot d}{8 \cdot b \cdot d^3} = \frac{12 \cdot P \cdot L}{8 \cdot b \cdot d^2}$$ $$\boxed{\sigma_{max} = \frac{3 \cdot P \cdot L}{2 \cdot b \cdot d^2}}$$ **Step 3 — Section Modulus Form:** Since $Z = \dfrac{bd^2}{6}$, we can also write: $$\sigma_{max} = \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{PL/4}{bd^2/6} = \frac{6PL}{4bd^2} = \frac{3PL}{2bd^2}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Concept Check:** At the **top fiber** of the beam, $\sigma$ is **compressive**; at the **bottom fiber**, $\sigma$ is **tensile**. For wood (which is weaker in tension perpendicular to grain), failure initiates at the **tension side**. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Maximum Deflection (Central Point Load) **Step 1 — Euler-Bernoulli Differential Equation:** $$EI \cdot \frac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = M(x) = \frac{P}{2} \cdot x \quad \text{for } 0 \leq x \leq L/2$$ **Step 2 — First Integration (Slope Equation):** $$EI \cdot \frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{P}{4} \cdot x^2 + C_1$$ By symmetry, slope at mid-span is zero: at $x = L/2$, $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = 0$: $$0 = \frac{P}{4} \cdot \frac{L^2}{4} + C_1 \Rightarrow C_1 = -\frac{PL^2}{16}$$ Therefore: $$EI \cdot \frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{Px^2}{4} - \frac{PL^2}{16}$$ **Step 3 — Second Integration (Deflection Equation):** $$EI \cdot y = \frac{Px^3}{12} - \frac{PL^2 \cdot x}{16} + C_2$$ Boundary condition: at $x = 0$, $y = 0$ → $C_2 = 0$. **Step 4 — Deflection at Mid-Span (x = L/2):** $$EI \cdot y_{max} = \frac{P(L/2)^3}{12} - \frac{PL^2(L/2)}{16}$$ $$EI \cdot y_{max} = \frac{PL^3}{96} - \frac{PL^3}{32} = \frac{PL^3}{96} - \frac{3PL^3}{96}$$ $$EI \cdot y_{max} = -\frac{2PL^3}{96} = -\frac{PL^3}{48}$$ The negative sign indicates downward deflection. Taking magnitude: $$\boxed{\delta_{max} = \frac{P \cdot L^3}{48 \cdot E \cdot I}}$$ **Step 5 — Substitute $I = \dfrac{bd^3}{12}$:** $$\delta_{max} = \frac{P \cdot L^3}{48 \cdot E \cdot (bd^3/12)} = \frac{12 \cdot P \cdot L^3}{48 \cdot E \cdot b \cdot d^3}$$ $$\boxed{\delta_{max} = \frac{P \cdot L^3}{4 \cdot E \cdot b \cdot d^3}}$$ --- ### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Modulus of Elasticity from Load-Deflection Data **From the central deflection formula:** $$\delta = \frac{P \cdot L^3}{4 \cdot E \cdot b \cdot d^3}$$ **Solving for E:** $$E = \frac{P \cdot L^3}{4 \cdot \delta \cdot b \cdot d^3}$$ **For the graphical method (using slope $m$ of the linear portion of P-δ curve):** Since $P = m \cdot \delta$ in the elastic region, substitute $P = m \cdot \delta$: $$E = \frac{(m \cdot \delta) \cdot L^3}{4 \cdot \delta \cdot b \cdot d^3} = \frac{m \cdot L^3}{4 \cdot b \cdot d^3}$$ $$\boxed{E = \frac{m \cdot L^3}{4 \cdot b \cdot d^3}}$$ --- ### 3.5 Numerical Example — Worked Out **Given Data:** - Specimen dimensions: $b = 50$ mm, $d = 50$ mm - Effective span: $L = 700$ mm - Failure load: $P_{max} = 3500$ N - Slope of P-δ graph (linear region): $m = 500$ N/mm **Step 1 — Modulus of Rupture:** $$MOR = \frac{3 \cdot P_{max} \cdot L}{2 \cdot b \cdot d^2} = \frac{3 \times 3500 \times 700}{2 \times 50 \times 50^2}$$ $$MOR = \frac{7,350,000}{250,000} = 29.4 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 29.4 \text{ MPa}$$ **Step 2 — Young's Modulus:** $$E = \frac{m \cdot L^3}{4 \cdot b \cdot d^3} = \frac{500 \times 700^3}{4 \times 50 \times 50^3}$$ $$E = \frac{500 \times 343,000,000}{4 \times 50 \times 125,000} = \frac{1.715 \times 10^{11}}{2.5 \times 10^7}$$ $$E = 6860 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 6.86 \text{ GPa}$$ --- ### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Loading Configurations | Parameter | 3-Point Bending (Central Load) | 4-Point Bending (Two-Point Load) | |---|---|---| | Maximum Moment | $M = \dfrac{PL}{4}$ | $M = \dfrac{P \cdot a}{2}$ | | Moment Distribution | Triangular (linear) | Rectangular (pure bending in middle third) | | Max. Deflection | $\delta = \dfrac{PL^3}{48EI}$ | $\delta = \dfrac{Pa(3L^2 - 4a^2)}{48EI}$ | | Failure Location | Random (typically at mid-span) | Always in pure bending zone | | Use Case | Quick screening tests | Accurate modulus determination | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Bending Test Setup ```mermaid flowchart TD startA["Experiment Initiation"] --> nodeB["Specimen Inspection
Dimensions Measurement
b d L Recorded"] nodeB --> nodeC["Specimen Placement
on Two Roller Supports
Effective Span L"] nodeC --> nodeD["Dial Gauge Installation
at Mid-Span
Initial Reading Zero"] nodeD --> nodeE["Apply Initial Seating Load
to Remove Slack
Reset Dial Gauge"] nodeE --> nodeF["Incremental Loading
Load Steps of 50N or 100N
30s Stabilization Period"] nodeF --> nodeG{"Deflection Reading
Recorded per Step"} nodeG --> nodeH["Plot Load vs Deflection Curve
Identify Linear Region
Calculate Slope m"] nodeF --> nodeI{"Failure Load
Reached?"} nodeI -->|"No"| nodeF nodeI -->|"Yes"| nodeJ["Record Ultimate Load Pmax
Note Failure Mode
Tension or Compression Side"] nodeH --> nodeK["Calculate MOR
MOR equals 3PmaxL over 2bd squared"] nodeH --> nodeL["Calculate Young Modulus E
E equals mL cubed over 4bd cubed"] nodeJ --> nodeM["Tabulate Results
Compare with IS 3629
Specify Wood Grade"] nodeK --> nodeM nodeL --> nodeM nodeM --> nodeEnd["Experiment Conclusion
Report Submission"] style startA fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000 style nodeE fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000 style nodeF fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000 style nodeH fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,color:#000 style nodeK fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000 style nodeL fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000 style nodeEnd fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` --- ### 4.2 Schematic Flow of Stress Distribution in the Beam ```mermaid flowchart LR loadA["Applied Load P
at Mid-Span"] --> distribB["Bending Moment

1 over R equals M over EI"] distribC --> distribD["Stress Distribution

at Top Fiber"] style loadA fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000 style distribB fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000 style distribC fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000 style distribD fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,color:#000 style fiberE fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#FFF style fiberF fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000 style fiberG fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#FFF style failI fill:#DC143C,stroke:#000,color:#FFF style failK fill:#DC143C,stroke:#000,color:#FFF ``` --- ### 4.3 Typical Load-Deflection Curve for a Wooden Beam ```mermaid flowchart LR curveA["Origin
Zero Load
