They serve as the schematic equivalent of a physical setup diagram. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **Define the term "Modulus of Rupture" (MOR) and write its formula for a rectangular wooden beam under three-point bending.** **Model Answer:** The **Modulus of Rupture (MOR)** is defined as the **maximum bending stress** sustained by the specimen at the moment of failure. It is **not** a true stress (since the linear stress distribution assumption breaks down near failure), but rather an **apparent stress** that represents the **flexural strength** of the material. **Formula for three-point bending (rectangular cross-section):** $$MOR = \frac{3 \cdot P_{max} \cdot L}{2 \cdot b \cdot d^2}$$ Where: - $P_{max}$ = Failure load (N) - $L$ = Effective span (mm) - $b$ = Width of beam (mm) - $d$ = Depth of beam (mm) **Unit:** N/mm² or MPa. --- #### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **Why is the span-to-depth ratio (L/d) maintained between 14 and 16 in the standard bending test of timber?** **Model Answer:** The span-to-depth ratio **L/d = 14 to 16** is maintained as per **IS 1708-1986** for the following reasons: 1. **To ensure bending-dominated failure (not shear failure):** If L/d is too small, shear stresses dominate and the beam fails in shear rather than flexure. 2. **To validate Euler-Bernoulli assumptions:** The classical beam theory assumes that **plane sections remain plane** and that **shear deformation is negligible**. This is valid only when L/d > 10 (preferably 14–16). 3. **To prevent lateral-torsional buckling:** Adequate L/d ratio prevents the beam from buckling sideways before flexural failure. 4. **To produce measurable deflections:** Higher L/d gives sufficient deflection for accurate dial gauge measurement. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) #### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the procedure to perform a **three-point bending test** on a wooden beam to determine its **Young's Modulus (E)**. **(b) [7 Marks]** A wooden beam of cross-section **60 mm × 60 mm** and span **800 mm** failed at a central load of **4200 N**. In the elastic region, the load-deflection data gave a slope of **600 N/mm**. Calculate: - (i) Modulus of Rupture - (ii) Young's Modulus of the wood --- **Model Answer to Question A(a):** **Apparatus Required:** - Universal Testing Machine (UTM) / Beam testing apparatus with loading capacity 100 kN - Wooden beam specimen (seasoned, defect-free) - Two roller-type knife-edge supports (span adjustable up to 1000 mm) - Central loading nose (with hemispherical seat for self-alignment) - Dial gauge (LC = 0.01 mm) with magnetic base - Vernier caliper (LC = 0.02 mm) **Experimental Procedure:** 1. **Specimen Preparation:** Measure the cross-section dimensions (b and d) at three locations along the length using a vernier caliper. Record the average values. 2. **Specimen Mounting:** Place the wooden beam on the two roller supports. Set the effective span **L** as **14 to 16 times** the depth (e.g., for d = 60 mm, L = 840 to 960 mm; here L = 800 mm as specified). 3. **Gauge Setup:** Mount the dial gauge directly beneath the beam at mid-span. Ensure the gauge plunger is vertical. Set the dial to **zero**. 4. **Pre-Loading:** Apply a small **seating load** (about 5–10% of expected failure load) to remove any slack. Reset the dial gauge to zero. 5. **Incremental Loading:** Apply load in equal increments of **100 N**. After each increment, wait **30 seconds** for stabilization and record the dial gauge reading. 6. **Continuous Loading:** Continue the process up to about **70% of expected failure load** (still in elastic range), then unload gradually. 7. **Final Loading:** Reload and continue until the specimen **fractures** with an audible crack. Record the **maximum load (P_max)**. 8. **Graph Plotting:** Plot **Load (P) on Y-axis** vs **Deflection (δ) on X-axis**. Identify the linear elastic region and determine its **slope (m)**. 9. **Calculations:** Apply formulas to compute MOR and E. **Sketch (ASCII Representation for KTU Answer Sheet):** ``` [Loading Nose] ↓ P _________↓_________ | ←—————L——————→ | |___________________| ↑ ↑ [Support A] [Support B] (Rollers) Dial Gauge at mid-span (below beam) ``` --- **Model Answer to Question A(b):** **Given Data:** - Width: $b = 60$ mm - Depth: $d = 60$ mm - Span: $L = 800$ mm - Failure load: $P_{max} = 4200$ N - Slope of P-δ graph: $m = 600$ N/mm **Step (i) — Modulus of Rupture:** $$MOR = \frac{3 \cdot P_{max} \cdot L}{2 \cdot b \cdot d^2}$$ $$MOR = \frac{3 \times 4200 \times 800}{2 \times 60 \times 60^2}$$ $$MOR = \frac{10,080,000}{432,000}$$ $$\boxed{MOR = 23.33 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 23.33 \text{ MPa}}$$ **Step (ii) — Young's Modulus:** $$E = \frac{m \cdot L^3}{4 \cdot b \cdot d^3}$$ $$E = \frac{600 \times 800^3}{4 \times 60 \times 60^3}$$ $$E = \frac{600 \times 512,000,000}{4 \times 60 \times 216,000}$$ $$E = \frac{3.072 \times 10^{11}}{5.184 \times 10^7}$$ $$\boxed{E = 5926.0 \text{ N/mm}^2 \approx 5.93 \text{ GPa}}$$ **Comparison with Standard Values:** The calculated E ≈ 5.93 GPa is in the range typical for **softwood / lower-grade hardwood**, indicating moderate stiffness. --- #### **Question B (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for the **maximum deflection** of a simply supported wooden beam carrying a **concentrated load P at the mid-span**, using **Euler-Bernoulli beam theory**. **(b) [7 Marks]** A wooden beam of dimensions **b = 50 mm, d = 75 mm** is tested over a span of **900 mm**. The central deflection recorded at a load of **1000 N** is **1.85 mm**. Calculate the **Young's Modulus** of the wood. Also, if the same beam fails at a load of **4500 N**, compute its **Modulus of Rupture**. --- **Model Answer to Question B(a):** Refer to **Section 3.3 — Derivation 3** above for the complete step-by-step derivation. Key steps: 1. Write differential equation: $EI \cdot \dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2} = \dfrac{Px}{2}$ 2. Integrate once: $EI \cdot \dfrac{dy}{dx} = \dfrac{Px^2}{4} + C_1$ 3. Apply symmetry: slope = 0 at x = L/2, giving $C_1 = -\dfrac{PL^2}{16}$ 4. Integrate again: $EI \cdot y = \dfrac{Px^3}{12} - \dfrac{PL^2 x}{16} + C_2$ 5. Apply boundary: y = 0 at x = 0, giving $C_2 = 0$ 6. Substitute x = L/2: $y_{max} = -\dfrac{PL^3}{48EI}$ 7. Take magnitude and substitute $I = \dfrac{bd^3}{12}$: $$\boxed{\delta_{max} = \frac{P \cdot L^3}{4 \cdot E \cdot b \cdot d^3}}$$ --- **Model Answer to Question B(b):** **Given Data:** - $b = 50$ mm, $d = 75$ mm, $L = 900$ mm - At $P = 1000$ N, deflection $\delta = 1.85$ mm - Failure load $P_{max} = 4500$ N **Step 1 — Young's Modulus (using single point data):** $$E = \frac{P \cdot L^3}{4 \cdot \delta \cdot b \cdot d^3}$$ $$E = \frac{1000 \times 900^3}{4 \times 1.85 \times 50 \times 75^3}$$ $$E = \frac{1000 \times 729,000,000}{4 \times 1.85 \times 50 \times 421,875}$$ $$E = \frac{7.29 \times 10^{11}}{1.56094 \times 10^8}$$ $$\boxed{E \approx 4670.6 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 4.67 \text{ GPa}}$$ **Step 2 — Modulus of Rupture:** $$MOR = \frac{3 \cdot P_{max} \cdot L}{2 \cdot b \cdot d^2}$$ $$MOR = \frac{3 \times 4500 \times 900}{2 \times 50 \times 75^2}$$ $$MOR = \frac{12,150,000}{562,500}$$ $$\boxed{MOR = 21.6 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 21.6 \text{ MPa}}$$ --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Lab Exams:** > > 1. **Unit Inconsistency (2-3 marks lost frequently):** Students mix units — using *cm* for dimensions and *N* for load without converting. **Always convert to mm and N first.** > > 2. **Forgetting to Square/Cube Correctly (4-5 marks):** The formula $\sigma = \dfrac{3PL}{2bd^2}$ uses $d^2$ (square), while $E = \dfrac{PL^3}{4Ebd^3}$ uses $d^3$ (cube). Confusing these leads to errors of **one order of magnitude**. > > 3. **Using B instead of D (3 marks):** Some students confuse **b (width)** and **d (depth)**. **Depth (d) is the dimension in the plane of bending**, i.e., the dimension along which the load causes deflection. > > 4. **Not Stating L/d Ratio (1-2 marks):** Examiners expect students to **verify** that L/d lies between 14 and 16. If not, the test is non-standard. > > 5. **Forgetting Span vs Total Length:** The **effective span (L)** is between the two supports, **not** the total length of the specimen. The specimen must overhang by at least 25 mm on each side. > > 6. **Ignoring Self-Weight:** For highly precise work, the **self-weight of the beam** must be added to the applied load. Examiners appreciate this mention even if not numerically applied. > > 7. **Plotting Error — Wrong Axis:** Load is always on the **Y-axis** and deflection on the **X-axis**. Slope $m$ is therefore in units of **N/mm**. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Bending Test on Wooden Beam** - ✅ **Aim:** Determine **Young's Modulus (E)** and **Modulus of Rupture (MOR)** of timber. - ✅ **Standard:** **IS 1708-1986** governs the testing method; **IS 3629-1986** governs structural timber specifications. - ✅ **Test Type:** **Three-point bending** (central point load) or **four-point bending** (pure bending region). - ✅ **Specimen:** Defect-free, straight-grained, conditioned at 27°C / 65% RH. - ✅ **L/d Ratio:** **14 to 16** — must be verified before calculations. - ✅ **Core Formula — Bending Stress:** $\sigma = \dfrac{3PL}{2bd^2}$ - ✅ **Core Formula — Modulus of Rupture:** $MOR = \dfrac{3 P_{max} L}{2 b d^2}$ - ✅ **Core Formula — Young's Modulus:** $E = \dfrac{m L^3}{4 b d^3}$ - ✅ **Core Formula — Section Modulus:** $Z = \dfrac{bd^2}{6}$ - ✅ **Failure Side:** Wood fails first on the **tension side** (perpendicular to grain). - ✅ **Loading Rate:** Slow, incremental (typically **1–2 mm/min** crosshead speed in UTM). - ✅ **Typical E Values:** Teak ≈ 9–13 GPa, Pine ≈ 8–12 GPa, Mango ≈ 6–9 GPa. - ✅ **Typical MOR Values:** Teak ≈ 50–95 MPa, Pine ≈ 40–80 MPa. - ✅ **Graph:** P-δ curve is **linear** in elastic region; **slope m** gives stiffness. - ✅ **Units to remember:** $E$ in **GPa**, $MOR$ in **MPa**, dimensions in **mm**, load in **N**. - ✅ **Distinguish MOR vs Working Stress:** MOR is the **ultimate** value; the **allowable working stress** is typically **MOR / Factor of Safety (≈ 3 to 6)**. --- > [!TIP] > **Final KTU Exam Tip (From Senior Toppers):** When asked to "describe the procedure," structure your answer into **Aim → Apparatus → Theory → Procedure → Tabulation → Graphs → Calculations → Result → Precautions**. Examiners allocate marks for each of these sections, and missing any one costs you 1–2 marks.

topics.title: Perform Stress Analysis Using Photo-Elasticity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUL407 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Perform Stress Analysis Using Photo-Elasticity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Photoelasticity** is a full-field, non-contact, experimental stress analysis technique that exploits the phenomenon of **temporary birefringence** (also called **artificial double refraction**) exhibited by certain transparent, isotropic materials when subjected to mechanical loading. When such a stressed specimen is placed in a **polariscope** and illuminated with polarized monochromatic light, it produces a pattern of bright and dark **isochromatic fringes** and **isoclinic lines** that can be interpreted to determine the **principal stresses** and the **maximum shear stress** at every point in the model.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Standard):**
> "Photoelasticity is an experimental method of stress analysis based on the property of birefringence exhibited by transparent materials under stress, where the difference in principal stresses at a point is proportional to the fringe order observed under a polariscope."

The governing physical principle is the **Stress-Optic Law (Maxwell's Stress-Optic Law)**, mathematically expressed as:

$$\sigma_1 - \sigma_2 = \frac{N \cdot f_\sigma}{t}$$

Where:
- $\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_2$ = principal stresses at a point
- $N$ = **fringe order** (integer or fractional number of isochromatic fringes)
- $f_\sigma$ = **material fringe value** (a calibration constant, in N/m·fringe)
- $t$ = **model thickness** (in meters)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you have a **clear plastic ruler** (made of **polycarbonate** or **epoxy**) and you bend it gently between your hands. The plastic itself looks uniform. But now, imagine you could look at it through a pair of special **"stress-vision sunglasses"** that revealed, in vivid rainbow colors, the *internal push and pull* happening inside the material.

- The **brighter bands** (called **fringes**) are like topographic contour lines on a map of a mountain. Each contour line represents the same "stress altitude" — in technical terms, the same **principal stress difference** $\sigma_1 - \sigma_2$.
- Where the contours are **densely packed**, it is like a steep cliff: the stress is changing rapidly across a small distance — this is a **stress concentration** zone (typical near holes, notches, or fillets).
- Where the contours are **widely spaced**, the stress is changing gently — a low-stress region.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in real engineering:**
> Before modern Finite Element Analysis (FEA), photoelasticity was the **gold standard** for visualizing stress concentrations in **aircraft wings, pressure vessels, turbine blades, gear teeth, and bridge components**. Even today, it is used as a **validation tool** for FEA results because it directly *measures* the actual stress field inside a real material, rather than computing an idealized mathematical model.

## 1.3 Why Certain Materials Behave This Way

Certain transparent polymers, when unstressed, are **optically isotropic** — light passes through with the **same velocity** regardless of polarization direction. When a stress $\sigma$ is applied, the molecular chains of the polymer deform, and the material becomes **optically anisotropic**: light polarized along the high-stress direction travels at a **different speed** than light polarized along the low-stress direction. This speed difference produces a **relative phase retardation** $\Delta$ between the two light components, which is what creates the visible fringe patterns.

> [!TIP]
> **Common photoelastic materials used in the KTU laboratory:**
> - **Araldite** (epoxy resin) — most common in KTU labs
> - **Polycarbonate** (PC)
> - **Polymethyl methacrylate** (PMMA, Plexiglas)
> - **CR-39** (allyl diglycol carbonate) — used for commercial stress analysis
> - **Glass** (3D photoelasticity only, not used in KTU)

The standard **model material in KTU labs** is **Araldite (epoxy)** with a typical **material fringe value** $f_\sigma \approx 12$ to $14$ N/m·fringe for $\lambda = 589$ nm sodium light.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physical Foundations: Polarized Light & Birefringence

### 2.1.1 Nature of Light
Light is a **transverse electromagnetic wave** consisting of mutually perpendicular oscillating electric ($\vec{E}$) and magnetic ($\vec{B}$) fields. The direction of $\vec{E}$ defines the **plane of polarization**.

### 2.1.2 Plane-Polarized Light
In **unpolarized light** (e.g., sunlight), the $\vec{E}$ vector oscillates randomly in all directions perpendicular to propagation. When this light passes through a **polarizer** (typically a **Polaroid** filter or a **Nicol prism**), only the component of $\vec{E}$ aligned with the **transmission axis** of the polarizer is allowed through, producing **plane-polarized light**.

### 2.1.3 Birefringence (Double Refraction)
A **birefringent material** has **two principal refractive indices**:
- $n_1$ (refractive index for light polarized along the $\sigma_1$ direction)
- $n_2$ (refractive index for light polarized along the $\sigma_2$ direction)

The phase difference introduced between these two components after traversing thickness $t$ is:

$$\Delta = \frac{2\pi t}{\lambda} (n_1 - n_2)$$

## 2.2 Maxwell's Stress-Optic Law — The Heart of Photoelasticity

When a birefringent polymer is stressed, the induced birefringence is **linearly proportional to the difference in principal stresses** (this is the empirically observed Maxwell stress-optic law):

$$n_1 - n_2 = C \cdot (\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)$$

Where $C$ is the **stress-optic coefficient** (in Brewsters, $1 \text{ Brewster} = 10^{-12} \text{ Pa}^{-1}$).

Substituting into the retardation equation:

$$\Delta = \frac{2\pi t}{\lambda} \cdot C \cdot (\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)$$

For **constructive interference** (bright fringe) in a circular polariscope, the retardation must equal an **integer multiple of the wavelength**:

$$\Delta = N \cdot \lambda$$

Therefore, the **fundamental working equation of photoelasticity** is:

$$\boxed{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2 = \frac{N \cdot f_\sigma}{t}}$$

where the **material fringe value** is defined as:

$$f_\sigma = \frac{\lambda}{C}$$

The material fringe value represents the **stress difference** that produces exactly **one full fringe** in a model of **unit thickness** (1 m).

## 2.3 Isochromatic Fringes vs Isoclinic Lines

| Feature | Isochromatic Fringes | Isoclinic Lines |
|---|---|---|
| **What they represent** | Loci of constant $(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)$ | Loci of constant principal stress direction |
| **Governing quantity** | Principal stress difference | Principal stress direction $\theta_p$ |
| **Fringe order N** | Integer (N = 0, 1, 2, ...) — can be fractional with compensator | Not applicable (N is a function of $\theta_p$) |
| **Wavelength dependence** | Yes (colored with white light) | No (always dark) |
| **Polariscope type** | Circular polariscope (preferred) | Plane polariscope |
| **Use in design** | Quantitative stress magnitudes | Determining stress trajectories |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Use:**
> - **Isochromatic fringes** are used to compute **numerical values** of stress at a point.
> - **Isoclinic lines** are used to determine the **orientation of principal stresses** and to construct **stress trajectories** that guide reinforcement in concrete structures.

## 2.4 Types of Polariscopes

### 2.4.1 Plane Polariscope (Linear Polariscope)
- **Components:** Light source → **Polarizer** → Specimen → **Analyzer** (crossed at 90°)
- **Output:** Both isochromatic fringes AND isoclinic lines appear simultaneously
- **Drawback:** Isoclinics **obscure** the isochromatics, making quantitative analysis difficult
- **Use:** Determination of **isoclinic angles** (principal stress direction)

### 2.4.2 Circular Polariscope
- **Components:** Light source → Polarizer → **Quarter Wave Plate (QWP₁)** → Specimen → **QWP₂** → Analyzer
- **Effect:** The two quarter-wave plates convert the linearly polarized light into **circularly polarized light** components, which **suppress the isoclinics** and leave only the **isochromatic fringes** visible
- **Use:** Quantitative stress analysis (used in all KTU lab experiments)

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma_1 - \sigma_2 = \dfrac{N \cdot f_\sigma}{t}$ | **Stress-optic law** (primary equation) | Pa |
| 2 | $N = \dfrac{(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2) \cdot t}{f_\sigma}$ | Fringe order in terms of stress | dimensionless |
| 3 | $f_\sigma = \dfrac{\lambda}{C}$ | Material fringe value definition | N/m·fringe |
| 4 | $\Delta = N \cdot \lambda$ | Phase retardation for N-th bright fringe | m |
| 5 | $\Delta = \dfrac{2\pi t}{\lambda} (n_1 - n_2)$ | Optical retardation | m |
| 6 | $n_1 - n_2 = C (\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)$ | Birefringence-stress relationship | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}{2} = \dfrac{N \cdot f_\sigma}{2t}$ | Maximum shear stress | Pa |
| 8 | $\sigma_{avg} = \dfrac{\sigma_1 + \sigma_2}{2}$ | Hydrostatic stress (NOT obtained from photoelasticity) | Pa |
| 9 | $\sigma_\theta = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2} \cos 2\theta + \tau_{xy} \sin 2\theta$ | 2D stress transformation | Pa |
| 10 | $\tan 2\theta_p = \dfrac{2 \tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}$ | Principal stress angle | rad |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Critical KTU Trap:**
> Photoelasticity gives the **principal stress DIFFERENCE** $(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)$, NOT the **sum** $(\sigma_1 + \sigma_2)$. You cannot directly obtain individual principal stresses from a single photoelastic test! The hydrostatic stress must be obtained from a **separate experiment** (e.g., strain gauges or by adding a known stress).

## 2.6 Calibration of Material Fringe Value $f_\sigma$

The material fringe value must be **experimentally determined** before any model can be analyzed. KTU labs use a **calibration specimen**, typically a **circular disk under diametral compression** (Brazilian disk test), which produces a **well-known analytical stress field**.

For a **circular disk** of diameter $D$ under diametral load $P$:

$$\sigma_1 - \sigma_2 = \frac{8PD}{\pi t D_c^2}$$

where $D_c$ is the distance from the load line to the point of interest (varies along the disk). The fringe order $N$ at the disk center is:

$$N_c = \frac{8P}{\pi D f_\sigma}$$

By measuring $N_c$ for a known load $P$ and known disk diameter $D$:

$$f_\sigma = \frac{8P}{\pi D N_c}$$

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Engineering Field | Application Example |
|---|---|
| **Aerospace** | Stress around rivet holes in aircraft fuselage panels |
| **Pressure Vessels** | Stress concentration at nozzle-to-shell junctions |
| **Civil Engineering** | Stress trajectories in dam structures and arch bridges |
| **Automotive** | Stress analysis of gear teeth and crankshaft fillets |
| **Biomedical** | Stress distribution in bone-prosthesis interfaces |
| **Civil/Geotechnical** | Stress around tunnel openings and underground cavities |
| **Quality Control** | Residual stress measurement in tempered glass and injection-molded plastics |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Stress-Optic Law (Full Maxwell Derivation)

### Step 1: Set up the optical situation
Consider a ray of **plane-polarized monochromatic light** of wavelength $\lambda$ propagating along the $z$-axis and passing through a stressed photoelastic specimen of thickness $t$.

The two principal stresses $\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_2$ act in the $x$ and $y$ directions respectively (we orient the axes along the principal stress directions).

### Step 2: Decompose the light into components
The incident light vector $\vec{E}$ is polarized at some arbitrary angle $\alpha$ to $\sigma_1$. It can be decomposed into two orthogonal components:

$$E_x = E_0 \cos \alpha \cdot \cos(\omega t - k_1 z)$$

$$E_y = E_0 \sin \alpha \cdot \cos(\omega t - k_2 z)$$

where $k_1$ and $k_2$ are the wave numbers in the two principal directions:

$$k_1 = \frac{2\pi n_1}{\lambda}, \quad k_2 = \frac{2\pi n_2}{\lambda}$$

### Step 3: Apply the birefringence hypothesis
By Maxwell's **stress-optic law hypothesis**, the birefringence is linearly proportional to the stress difference:

$$n_1 - n_2 = C(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)$$

where $C$ is the **stress-optic coefficient** (material constant).

### Step 4: Compute the phase retardation
The **optical path difference** (OPD) between the two components after traversing the thickness $t$ is:

$$\text{OPD} = t(n_1 - n_2) = C \cdot t \cdot (\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)$$

The corresponding **phase retardation** is:

$$\Delta = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} \cdot \text{OPD} = \frac{2\pi C t}{\lambda} (\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)$$

### Step 5: Apply the interference condition
For **constructive interference** (bright fringe) at the analyzer, the retardation must equal an integer number of wavelengths:

$$\Delta = N \lambda, \quad N = 0, 1, 2, 3, \ldots$$

### Step 6: Equate the two expressions for retardation
Setting the two expressions for $\Delta$ equal:

$$\frac{2\pi C t}{\lambda} (\sigma_1 - \sigma_2) = N \lambda$$

Rearranging:

$$(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2) = \frac{N \lambda^2}{2 \pi C t}$$

Define the **material fringe value** as:

$$f_\sigma = \frac{\lambda}{C}$$

Substituting $C = \lambda / f_\sigma$:

$$(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2) = \frac{N \lambda^2}{2\pi t \cdot (\lambda / f_\sigma)} = \frac{N \lambda f_\sigma}{2 \pi t}$$

Note: Some texts use a slightly different convention where $f_\sigma = \lambda / (2\pi C)$ to absorb the $2\pi$ factor. The KTU lab convention uses:

$$\boxed{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2 = \frac{N \cdot f_\sigma}{t}}$$

This is the **working equation** of photoelasticity. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example #1 — Circular Disk Calibration

**Problem (KTU Model):**
A circular photoelastic disk of diameter $D = 60$ mm and thickness $t = 6$ mm is loaded diametrically with a force $P = 800$ N. At the center of the disk, the isochromatic fringe order is found to be $N_c = 4$. Determine the material fringe value $f_\sigma$ of the disk material.

**Solution — Step by Step:**

**Step 1:** For a circular disk under diametral compression, the analytical expression for the principal stress difference at the **center** of the disk is:

$$\sigma_1 - \sigma_2 = \frac{8P}{\pi D t}$$

**Step 2:** Substituting the values (with $D = 0.060$ m, $t = 0.006$ m, $P = 800$ N):

$$\sigma_1 - \sigma_2 = \frac{8 \times 800}{\pi \times 0.060 \times 0.006}$$

Calculate numerator and denominator:

$$= \frac{6400}{1.13097 \times 10^{-3}} = 5.657 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa} = 5.657 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the stress-optic law at the disk center:

$$N_c \cdot f_\sigma = (\sigma_1 - \sigma_2) \cdot t$$

$$4 \cdot f_\sigma = 5.657 \times 10^{6} \times 0.006$$

$$4 \cdot f_\sigma = 33,942 \text{ N/m}$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $f_\sigma$:

$$f_\sigma = \frac{33,942}{4} = 8485.5 \text{ N/m·fringe} \approx 8.49 \text{ kN/m·fringe}$$

This value is typical for an epoxy photoelastic material under **sodium light** ($\lambda = 589$ nm). $\blacksquare$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example #2 — Fringe Order in a Beam

**Problem (KTU Model):**
A rectangular photoelastic beam (length $L = 200$ mm, width $b = 30$ mm, thickness $t = 6$ mm) is loaded in **four-point bending**, producing a region of **pure bending** in the central span. In this region, the bending stress is given by:

$$\sigma_x = \frac{M \cdot y}{I}, \quad \sigma_y = 0$$

where $M = 240$ N·m is the bending moment and $I = \frac{bt^3}{12}$ is the moment of inertia. If the material fringe value is $f_\sigma = 8.49$ kN/m·fringe, determine the **fringe order** at a point located $y = 1.5$ mm from the neutral axis.

**Solution — Step by Step:**

**Step 1:** Calculate the moment of inertia $I$:

$$I = \frac{b \cdot t^3}{12} = \frac{0.030 \times (0.006)^3}{12} = \frac{0.030 \times 2.16 \times 10^{-7}}{12} = 5.4 \times 10^{-10} \text{ m}^4$$

**Step 2:** Calculate the principal stress difference at $y = 1.5$ mm = $0.0015$ m:

Since $\sigma_x \neq 0$ and $\sigma_y = 0$:

$$\sigma_1 - \sigma_2 = \sigma_x - 0 = \frac{M \cdot y}{I}$$

Substituting:

$$\sigma_1 - \sigma_2 = \frac{240 \times 0.0015}{5.4 \times 10^{-10}}$$

$$= \frac{0.36}{5.4 \times 10^{-10}} = 6.667 \times 10^{8} \text{ Pa} = 666.7 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the stress-optic law to get the fringe order:

$$N = \frac{(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2) \cdot t}{f_\sigma}$$

$$N = \frac{666.7 \times 10^{6} \times 0.006}{8490}$$

$$N = \frac{4.0 \times 10^{6}}{8490} = 471.1 \text{ fringes}$$

This is a **very high fringe order** — beyond the practical resolution of a typical polariscope. In practice, the load would have to be much smaller, or a lower-$f_\sigma$ material used. $\blacksquare$

> [!TIP]
> **Practical KTU lab note:** In real lab experiments, fringe orders are kept between **0.5 and 8** by choosing the load appropriately. This ensures clear, countable fringes. Use the **Tardy compensation method** or a **Babinet-Soleil compensator** to measure fractional fringe orders when the load is small.

## 3.4 Half-Fringe Order Identification (Tardy's Method)

When a **circular polariscope** is used, the isochromatic fringes have a sharp, unambiguous fringe order. However, when the load is small or the stress gradient is gentle, you may see **half fringes** ($N = 0.5, 1.5, 2.5, \ldots$). These correspond to **dark** fringes in a **plane polariscope** and **bright** fringes in a **circular polariscope**. The integer order $N$ (counted at a point) tells you directly the **principal stress difference** at that point.

For the **$N$-th bright fringe in a circular polariscope**:

$$(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)_N = \frac{N \cdot f_\sigma}{t}$$

For the **$N$-th dark fringe** (always $N = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$):

$$(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)_N = \frac{N \cdot f_\sigma}{t} \quad \text{(same equation)}$$

> [!CAUTION]
> **Common student error:** Many students confuse the isoclinic angle (a direction in degrees) with the fringe order (a dimensionless number). They are **completely different physical quantities**:
> - Isoclinic angle $\theta_p$ → principal stress direction (radians or degrees)
> - Fringe order $N$ → principal stress difference (dimensionless integer)

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Circular Polariscope Setup

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the **optical path** of a **circular polariscope** used in KTU labs for stress analysis. The monochromatic light source (sodium lamp) emits yellow light ($\lambda = 589$ nm) which is filtered, polarized, and finally analyzed to reveal the isochromatic fringe pattern in the loaded photoelastic specimen.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Monochromatic Light Source Sodium Lamp lambda 589 nm] --> B[Collimating Lens]
    B --> C[Polarizer Linear Polaroid Filter]
    C --> D[Quarter Wave Plate QWP1 Fast Axis 45 deg]
    D --> E[Photoelastic Specimen Under Load]
    E --> F[Quarter Wave Plate QWP2 Fast Axis minus 45 deg]
    F --> G[Analyzer Linear Polarizer Crossed to P]
    G --> H[Imaging Lens]
    H --> I[Observation Screen or CCD Camera]
    I --> J[Digital Image Processing Software]

    K[Loading Frame and Load Cell] --> E

    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style C fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style D fill:#98FB98,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style E fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style F fill:#98FB98,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style G fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style J fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

**Component-wise Function Explanation:**

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **Light Source** | Provides monochromatic light; sodium lamp gives sharp yellow light at $\lambda = 589$ nm. Mercury vapor or LED sources are alternatives. |
| **Collimating Lens** | Converts divergent light into a **parallel (collimated) beam** so that all rays traverse the specimen at the same angle. |
| **Polarizer** | Converts unpolarized light into **plane-polarized** light. The transmission axis is set at 45° to the principal stress directions in the specimen. |
| **QWP1** | Converts plane-polarized light into **right-circularly polarized** light. The fast axis is set at **+45°** to the polarizer. |
| **Specimen** | The photoelastic model under load. Acts as a **wave plate** with retardation proportional to the stress difference. |
| **QWP2** | Converts the two orthogonal circular components back into **two orthogonal plane-polarized** components, eliminating the isoclinics. |
| **Analyzer** | Crossed at 90° to the polarizer. Allows only the in-phase component to pass, producing the **isochromatic fringe pattern**. |
| **Imaging Lens & Camera** | Records the fringe pattern for further analysis (digital photoelasticity). |

## 4.2 Experimental Procedure Flowchart (KTU Standard)

The following Mermaid diagram captures the **step-by-step experimental procedure** for performing photoelastic stress analysis as prescribed in the KTU 2024 lab manual.

```mermaid

    H --> I[Step 9: Identify Isochromatic Fringe Order N at Points of Interest]
    I --> J[Step 10: Calculate Principal Stress Difference sigma1 minus sigma2 equals N times f sigma divided by t]
    J --> K[Step 11: Compute Maximum Shear Stress tau max equals sigma1 minus sigma2 divided by 2]
    K --> L[Step 12: Tabulate Results, Plot Stress Contours, Validate with FEA]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Digital Photoelasticity)

Modern KTU labs increasingly use **digital image correlation** combined with **phase-shifting photoelasticity** to extract full-field data automatically. The following Mermaid block diagram shows this advanced architecture.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Photoelastic Specimen under Load] --> B[Circular Polariscope]
    B --> C[CCD or CMOS Camera]
    C --> D[Frame Grabber Digitizer]
    D --> E[Phase Shifting Module Four Step Algorithm]
    E --> F[Isoclinic Angle Map theta p]
    E --> G[Isochromatic Fringe Map N]
    F --> H[Stress Separation Algorithm Shear Difference Method]
    G --> H
    I[Boundary Conditions from Load Cell] --> H
    H --> J[Full Field sigma1 minus sigma2 Contour]
    H --> K[Full Field tau max Contour]
    J --> L[Visualization Software MATLAB Python Paraview]
    K --> L

    style A fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style E fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style H fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style L fill:#98FB98,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

**Why This Architecture Matters:**
The classical photoelastic experiment yields only the **fringe order N** at discrete points. The **shear-difference method** (a numerical integration of the equilibrium equations) is then used to **separate** the individual stresses $\sigma_x$, $\sigma_y$ and $\tau_{xy}$, and from them, the principal stresses $\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_2$. This is essential because, as emphasized in Section 2, photoelasticity alone gives only the **difference** $\sigma_1 - \sigma_2$.
