# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2023]
**Q: Define the term "material fringe value" $f_\sigma$ in photoelasticity. State its units and physical significance.**

**Model Answer (Point-wise, Board-Examiner Approved):**

- **Definition:** The material fringe value $f_\sigma$ of a photoelastic material is defined as the value of the **principal stress difference** $(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)$ that, when applied to a model of **unit thickness** (1 m), produces **exactly one isochromatic fringe** ($N = 1$) in a circular polariscope.
- **Mathematical Definition:** From the stress-optic law $\sigma_1 - \sigma_2 = N f_\sigma / t$, setting $N = 1$ and $t = 1$ gives $\sigma_1 - \sigma_2 = f_\sigma$. Equivalently, $f_\sigma = \lambda / C$, where $C$ is the stress-optic coefficient.
- **Units:** N/m·fringe (or N/m, with the "fringe" implied) in SI units; sometimes kN/m·fringe in lab-scale values.
- **Physical Significance:** It is a **material constant** that characterizes the photoelastic sensitivity of the polymer. A **low** $f_\sigma$ (e.g., 2–3 kN/m·fringe for epoxy) means the material is **highly sensitive** and produces more fringes for a given stress. A **high** $f_\sigma$ (e.g., 100+ kN/m·fringe for glass) means the material is **less sensitive** and requires much higher stress to produce visible fringes.

### Question 2 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]
**Q: Distinguish between isochromatic fringes and isoclinic lines. Why is a circular polariscope preferred over a plane polariscope for quantitative stress analysis?**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Isochromatic Fringes | Isoclinic Lines |
|---|---|---|
| Represent | Loci of constant $(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)$ | Loci of constant principal stress direction $\theta_p$ |
| Quantity | Fringe order $N$ (integer) | Direction angle (degrees) |

**Why Circular Polariscope is Preferred:**
- In a **plane polariscope**, both isochromatic fringes AND isoclinic lines appear simultaneously. The dark isoclinics **overlap and obscure** the isochromatic fringes, making it impossible to count $N$ accurately.
- In a **circular polariscope**, the **two quarter-wave plates** convert the linearly polarized light into circularly polarized light. This effectively **suppresses all isoclinics**, leaving a clean field of **isochromatic fringes only**.
- This enables **accurate determination of fringe order $N$** at any point, and hence accurate computation of the **principal stress difference** $\sigma_1 - \sigma_2$ from the stress-optic law.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

#### (a) [7 Marks] With the help of a neat schematic, explain the working of a **circular polariscope**. How does it differ from a plane polariscope?

**Model Answer:**

**Schematic:** Refer to the **Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.1** of these notes for the layout. The components, in order, are:

1. **Monochromatic light source** (sodium lamp, $\lambda = 589$ nm)
2. **Collimating lens** (makes light parallel)
3. **Polarizer** (transmits only one plane of polarization)
4. **Quarter wave plate QWP₁** (fast axis at +45° to polarizer)
5. **Photoelastic specimen** (under load)
6. **Quarter wave plate QWP₂** (fast axis at –45° to analyzer)
7. **Analyzer** (crossed at 90° to polarizer)
8. **Imaging system** (screen or camera)

**Working Principle:**
- The polarizer converts unpolarized light into plane-polarized light.
- QWP₁ converts this into **right-circularly polarized light** — this is equivalent to two orthogonal linear components of **equal amplitude** and a **90° phase difference**.
- As the light traverses the stressed specimen, the two circular components experience **different phase shifts** because the specimen's principal stress directions are aligned with the optical axes of the quarter-wave plates.
- The difference in phase shift is the **retardation** $\Delta = N\lambda = (2\pi C t / \lambda)(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)$.
- QWP₂ converts the two circular components back into two orthogonal linear components.
- The analyzer passes only the component aligned with its transmission axis, producing **constructive interference** (bright fringe) when $N$ is an integer and **destructive interference** (dark fringe) when $N$ is a half-integer.

**Difference from Plane Polariscope:**

| Feature | Plane Polariscope | Circular Polariscope |
|---|---|---|
| Quarter-wave plates | Absent | Present (two) |
| Isoclinics | Visible (obscure fringes) | Suppressed (only isochromatic fringes visible) |
| Fringe clarity | Poor | Excellent |
| Use | Direction of principal stress | Quantitative stress analysis |

#### (b) [7 Marks] A photoelastic disk of diameter 50 mm and thickness 8 mm is loaded diametrically with a force of 1.2 kN. At the center of the disk, the isochromatic fringe order is $N = 5$. Compute:
**(i)** The material fringe value $f_\sigma$
**(ii)** The maximum shear stress at the center of the disk

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Material Fringe Value $f_\sigma$:**

For a circular disk under diametral compression, the principal stress difference at the **center** is:

$$\sigma_1 - \sigma_2 = \frac{8P}{\pi D t}$$

Substituting: $P = 1200$ N, $D = 0.050$ m, $t = 0.008$ m:

$$\sigma_1 - \sigma_2 = \frac{8 \times 1200}{\pi \times 0.050 \times 0.008} = \frac{9600}{1.2566 \times 10^{-3}} = 7.639 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa} = 7.639 \text{ MPa}$$

From the stress-optic law, at the center:

$$N \cdot f_\sigma = (\sigma_1 - \sigma_2) \cdot t$$

$$5 \cdot f_\sigma = 7.639 \times 10^{6} \times 0.008 = 61,112 \text{ N/m}$$

$$f_\sigma = \frac{61,112}{5} = 12,222 \text{ N/m·fringe} \approx 12.22 \text{ kN/m·fringe}$$

**(ii) Maximum Shear Stress at Center:**

$$\tau_{\max} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}{2} = \frac{7.639 \times 10^6}{2} = 3.8195 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 3.82 \text{ MPa}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning #1:**
> Many students write $\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_2$ individually as some numerical values. **This is wrong** — photoelasticity gives only the **difference** $(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)$, not the individual principal stresses. Do not confuse the principal stress difference with the individual values.

---

### QUESTION B — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam - May 2024] (Alternative Choice)

#### (a) [7 Marks] Derive the **Maxwell's stress-optic law** for a 2D photoelastic model. State clearly the assumptions involved.

**Model Answer:**

**Assumptions:**
1. The material is **initially isotropic** and optically inactive in the unstressed state.
2. The induced birefringence is **linearly proportional** to the difference in principal stresses.
3. The light propagates **perpendicular** to the plane of the model (no oblique incidence).
4. The stress field is **plane stress** (2D) with the third principal stress $\sigma_3 = 0$.
5. The material is **homogeneous** and the stress is **uniform** within the element traversed by a single light ray.

**Derivation:** Refer to the **exhaustive derivation in Section 3.1** of these notes. Key steps:

1. Decompose plane-polarized light into two orthogonal components along $\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_2$.
2. The two components travel with refractive indices $n_1$ and $n_2$ respectively.
3. The birefringence hypothesis: $n_1 - n_2 = C(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)$.
4. Phase retardation: $\Delta = (2\pi t / \lambda)(n_1 - n_2)$.
5. Interference condition for $N$-th bright fringe: $\Delta = N\lambda$.
6. Equating and rearranging:

$$\boxed{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2 = \frac{N \cdot f_\sigma}{t} \quad \text{where} \quad f_\sigma = \frac{\lambda}{C}}$$

#### (b) [7 Marks] With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **photoelastic method of determining stress concentration factor (SCF) around a circular hole in a rectangular plate under uniaxial tension**.

**Model Solution:**

**Experimental Procedure:**

1. **Model Preparation:** A rectangular photoelastic plate (Araldite) with a central circular hole is cast and polished to optical clarity. Let the plate have width $W$, thickness $t$, and a hole of diameter $d$.

2. **Loading:** The plate is loaded in **uniaxial tension** $P$ in a loading frame. The average far-field stress is $\sigma_0 = P / (W \cdot t)$.

3. **Polariscope Setup:** The loaded plate is placed in a **circular polariscope** and illuminated with monochromatic light.

4. **Observation of Fringe Pattern:** A characteristic **butterfly-shaped fringe pattern** is observed around the hole. The fringe order is **maximum at the boundary of the hole at the points transverse to the load direction** (i.e., at the top and bottom of the hole along the loading axis) and **zero at the free surfaces** (left and right tips of the hole along the loading direction).

5. **Counting Fringe Order:** At the critical point (top of the hole), the fringe order $N_{\max}$ is counted. Then the maximum principal stress difference at this point is:

$$(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)_{\max} = \frac{N_{\max} \cdot f_\sigma}{t}$$

6. **Theoretical Interpretation:** At the critical point on the hole boundary, the stress state is **uniaxial** (the free surface of the hole cannot sustain tangential stress). Therefore $\sigma_2 = 0$ and the maximum tangential stress is:

$$\sigma_{\max} = \frac{N_{\max} \cdot f_\sigma}{t}$$

7. **SCF Calculation:** The stress concentration factor is the **ratio of maximum tangential stress at the hole boundary to the far-field average stress**:

$$\text{SCF} = K_t = \frac{\sigma_{\max}}{\sigma_0} = \frac{N_{\max} \cdot f_\sigma / t}{P / (W \cdot t)} = \frac{N_{\max} \cdot f_\sigma \cdot W}{P}$$

8. **Validation:** This experimental SCF is compared to the **theoretical value** from **Inglis' solution** (for an infinite plate with a small hole):

$$K_{t,\text{theory}} = 1 + \frac{2d}{W} \cdot \text{(correction factor for finite plate from Howland)}$$

Typically, for $d/W$ between 0.05 and 0.5, $K_t$ ranges from 2.1 to 3.0, consistent with photoelastic observations.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning #2:**
> - A common mistake is to **forget to subtract the far-field stress** when computing SCF — the SCF is a **ratio**, not an absolute stress.
> - Another common error is to use the **fringe order at the hole edge** without confirming that the stress state there is **uniaxial** (which it is, by symmetry of a circular hole under uniaxial tension).
> - Always state the **assumptions** clearly: small hole compared to plate dimensions, plane stress, linear elastic photoelastic model material.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep):**

- ✅ **Photoelasticity** is a **full-field, non-destructive** experimental stress analysis method based on the **stress-induced birefringence** in transparent polymers.
- ✅ The **core working equation** is the **Maxwell stress-optic law**: $\sigma_1 - \sigma_2 = N f_\sigma / t$.
- ✅ **$N$** = isochromatic fringe order (integer in circular polariscope); **$f_\sigma$** = material fringe value (N/m·fringe); **$t$** = model thickness (m).
- ✅ Photoelasticity gives the **principal stress DIFFERENCE**, not the individual principal stresses. You need **shear-difference method** + boundary conditions to get $\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_2$ separately.
- ✅ **Maximum shear stress** at a point: $\tau_{\max} = (\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)/2 = N f_\sigma / (2t)$.
- ✅ **Circular polariscope** uses **two quarter-wave plates** to **suppress isoclinics** and give clean isochromatic fringes.
- ✅ **Plane polariscope** has **no quarter-wave plates**; both isoclinics and isochromatics appear together (used only to find principal stress direction).
- ✅ **Material fringe value** is determined experimentally using a **circular disk under diametral compression** (Brazilian disk / Hertzian test): $f_\sigma = 8P / (\pi D N_c)$.
- ✅ Common materials: **Araldite (epoxy)** with $f_\sigma \approx 8$–$14$ kN/m·fringe; **polycarbonate**, **CR-39**, **PMMA**.
- ✅ **Applications:** stress concentration around holes, notches, fillets; validation of FEA; design of pressure vessels, gears, turbine blades, aircraft components.
- ✅ **Units to remember:** stress in Pa or MPa, fringe order dimensionless, $f_\sigma$ in N/m·fringe, thickness in m.
- ✅ **Brewster's constant** $C$ for epoxy is of order $10^{-10}$ to $10^{-12}$ Pa$^{-1}$ (i.e., a few Brewsters).
- ✅ **Limitations:** model material must be linear elastic; cannot directly give hydrostatic stress; 3D photoelasticity (stress-freezing) is more complex; modern digital methods (phase shifting) are replacing manual fringe counting.
- ✅ **KTU Board Exam Tip:** Always draw the **polariscope setup** as a **block diagram** (light source → polarizer → QWP → specimen → QWP → analyzer → screen). The examiner allocates significant marks for a **correctly drawn diagram**.
- ✅ **Numerical approach for KTU exams:** State the governing equation first, list all given data with units, substitute step-by-step, and clearly highlight the **final numerical answer with correct units**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of Moment of Inertia of a Flywheel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUL407 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of Moment of Inertia of a Flywheel

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

**Moment of Inertia (I)** of a rigid body about a given axis is defined as the sum of the products of the mass of each particle and the square of its perpendicular distance from the axis of rotation. It is the rotational analogue of mass and represents the resistance offered by a body to a change in its angular motion.

Mathematically:

$$I = \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i r_i^2$$

In continuous form:

$$I = \int r^2 \, dm$$

The standard SI unit is **kg·m²**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In Module Experiment 2, the moment of inertia of a flywheel is determined *dynamically* using a falling-weight method (energy/work principle), rather than by geometric calculation. This experiment is essential for understanding rotational dynamics and forms the basis for analyzing flywheels used in engines, presses, and punching machines.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Bicycle Wheel Intuition

Imagine spinning a heavy bicycle wheel versus a small toy wheel of the same mass. The bicycle wheel is far harder to start spinning and equally hard to stop. Why? Because **most of its mass lies far from the central axle**. Since moment of inertia depends on **mass × (distance)²**, even a small mass placed at the rim contributes enormously to I.

A **flywheel** is a specially designed heavy wheel that stores rotational kinetic energy. Its large moment of inertia allows it to:
- Smooth out fluctuations in speed (e.g., in engines between power strokes),
- Store and release kinetic energy in intermittent operations (punch presses, rolling mills).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Potter's Wheel:** A potter's flywheel continues to rotate for several seconds even after the foot pedal is released. This is because the rotational kinetic energy, $KE_{rot} = \frac{1}{2}I\omega^2$, is large due to the high I. The experiment in this module essentially *measures* how much rotational "inertia" such a wheel possesses.

### 1.3 Apparatus and Standard Parameters (KTU Lab Setup)

| Component | Function |
|-----------|----------|
| Flywheel | Heavy disk mounted on a horizontal axle (test specimen) |
| Axle (Spindle) | Smaller-radius shaft on which the string is wound; radius **r** |
| String | Connects falling mass to the axle |
| Hanging Mass (m) | Provides driving force via gravity |
| Stopwatch | Records time **t** for the mass to fall height **h** |
| Meter Scale / Vernier | Measures **h** and **r** |
| Spirit Level | Ensures horizontal axle alignment |

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants Used:**
> - Acceleration due to gravity: **g = 9.81 m/s²**
> - All linear measurements in **metres (m)**, mass in **kilograms (kg)**, time in **seconds (s)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Governing Physical Principles

The experiment simultaneously applies **two fundamental principles** from Newtonian mechanics:

**Principle A — Translational Dynamics (for the falling mass):**
Newton's second law for the falling mass m (moving linearly with downward acceleration **a**):

$$mg - T = ma$$

where **T** is the tension in the string (which is *less than* mg because the mass is accelerating).

**Principle B — Rotational Dynamics (for the flywheel):**
Newton's second law for rotation about the axle (moment of inertia **I**, angular acceleration **α**):

$$\tau = I\alpha$$

Since the string is wound on the axle (radius r), the torque produced by tension T is:

$$\tau = T \cdot r$$

### 2.2 Kinematic Coupling Between Translation and Rotation

Because the string is inextensible and does not slip on the axle, the linear motion of the mass and the rotational motion of the flywheel are **kinematically locked**:

$$a = r\alpha \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \alpha = \frac{a}{r}$$

This single relation is the bridge that allows us to compute I from purely linear measurements.

### 2.3 Derivation Outline — From Measurements to I

1. Measure axle radius **r** with Vernier calipers.
2. Allow mass **m** to fall through a measured height **h** from rest, record time **t**.
3. Compute linear acceleration using kinematic equation (uniform acceleration from rest):

$$h = \frac{1}{2}at^2 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad a = \frac{2h}{t^2}$$

4. Compute tension:

$$T = m(g - a)$$

5. Compute torque and angular acceleration, then I:

$$I = \frac{\tau}{\alpha} = \frac{Tr}{a/r} = \frac{Tr^2}{a}$$

Substituting T:

$$\boxed{I = \frac{m(g - a) \, r^2}{a} = m r^2 \left(\frac{g}{a} - 1\right)}$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Linear acceleration of mass | $a = \dfrac{2h}{t^2}$ | m/s² | From $h = \tfrac{1}{2}at^2$ (starts from rest) |
| 2 | Tension in string | $T = m(g - a)$ | N | Always $\vert T \vert < mg$ |
| 3 | Torque on flywheel | $\tau = T \cdot r$ | N·m | r = axle radius |
| 4 | Angular acceleration | $\alpha = \dfrac{a}{r}$ | rad/s² | Kinematic coupling |
| 5 | Moment of Inertia | $I = \dfrac{\tau}{\alpha} = \dfrac{m(g-a)r^2}{a}$ | kg·m² | **Primary result** |
| 6 | Final angular velocity | $\omega = \dfrac{v}{r} = \dfrac{\sqrt{2ah}}{r}$ | rad/s | Useful for energy check |
| 7 | Rotational KE | $KE_{rot} = \dfrac{1}{2}I\omega^2$ | J | Energy stored in flywheel |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Flywheel moment of inertia is critical in:
> - **IC Engines** — smoothing torque between power strokes.
> - **Punch / Press Machines** — storing energy for instantaneous high-power operations.
> - **Regenerative Braking Systems** (EVs) — storing kinetic energy.
> - **Gyroscopes & Inertial Navigation** — angular momentum stability.

### 2.5 Common Experimental Variations (KTU Pattern)

In many KTU labs, the axle itself contributes non-negligible moment of inertia. The standard report form often reports **I of the flywheel + axle system**, with the axle contribution estimated separately.

> [!NOTE]
> **Energy-Conservation Verification:** As a cross-check, applying conservation of energy from rest:
> 
> $$mgh = \frac{1}{2}mv^2 + \frac{1}{2}I\omega^2$$
> 
> yields the same expression for I when combined with $v = r\omega$ and $v^2 = 2ah$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Working Formula

We now derive, in full algebraic detail, the expression used to compute I from experimental observations.

**Step 1 — Kinematics of the falling mass:**

The mass m starts from rest (initial velocity = 0) and falls a vertical distance h under constant acceleration a. Using the second equation of motion:

$$h = u t + \frac{1}{2} a t^2$$

Since the mass starts from rest, u = 0:

$$h = \frac{1}{2} a t^2$$

Solving for a:

$$a = \frac{2h}{t^2}$$

**Step 2 — Force balance on the falling mass (Newton's Second Law — Translation):**

Let the upward tension be T and the downward weight be mg. Taking downward as positive:

$$\text{Net downward force} = mg - T = ma$$

Hence:

$$T = m(g - a) \quad \text{...(i)}$$

**Step 3 — Torque acting on the flywheel (Newton's Second Law — Rotation):**

The tension T in the string, acting tangentially at the axle radius r, exerts a torque about the axis of rotation:

$$\tau = T \cdot r \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

**Step 4 — Kinematic coupling (rolling-without-slip analogue):**

The string unwinds without slipping, so the tangential acceleration of the string at the axle equals the linear acceleration a of the mass:

$$a = r\alpha \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \alpha = \frac{a}{r} \quad \text{...(iii)}$$

**Step 5 — Applying rotational Newton's law to the flywheel:**

$$\tau = I \alpha \quad \text{...(iv)}$$

Substituting (ii) and (iii) into (iv):

$$Tr = I \cdot \frac{a}{r}$$

Solving for I:

$$I = \frac{Tr^2}{a} \quad \text{...(v)}$$

**Step 6 — Substituting T from (i) into (v):**

$$\begin{aligned}
I &= \frac{\left[ m(g - a) \right] r^2}{a} \\
  &= m r^2 \cdot \frac{g - a}{a} \\
  &= m r^2 \left( \frac{g}{a} - 1 \right)
\end{aligned}$$

**Final working formula:**

$$\boxed{I = \frac{m(g-a)\, r^2}{a} = m r^2 \left( \frac{g}{a} - 1 \right)}$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Sample Problem)

> **Given:** Mass attached to string, $m = 1.5 \text{ kg}$. Axle radius, $r = 0.025 \text{ m}$. The mass falls $h = 1.0 \text{ m}$ from rest in time $t = 5.0 \text{ s}$. Take $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.

**Step 1 — Linear acceleration:**

$$a = \frac{2h}{t^2} = \frac{2 \times 1.0}{(5.0)^2} = \frac{2.0}{25.0} = 0.08 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Step 2 — Tension in string:**

$$T = m(g - a) = 1.5 \times (9.81 - 0.08) = 1.5 \times 9.73 = 14.595 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — Angular acceleration:**

$$\alpha = \frac{a}{r} = \frac{0.08}{0.025} = 3.2 \text{ rad/s}^2$$

**Step 4 — Moment of inertia:**

$$I = \frac{T r^2}{a} = \frac{14.595 \times (0.025)^2}{0.08} = \frac{14.595 \times 6.25 \times 10^{-4}}{0.08}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
I &= \frac{9.121875 \times 10^{-3}}{0.08} \\
  &= 0.11402 \text{ kg·m}^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** $I \approx 0.114 \text{ kg·m}^2$.

**Cross-check using alternate formula:**

$$I = m r^2 \left( \frac{g}{a} - 1 \right) = 1.5 \times (0.025)^2 \times \left( \frac{9.81}{0.08} - 1 \right)$$

$$\begin{aligned}
I &= 1.5 \times 6.25 \times 10^{-4} \times (122.625 - 1) \\
  &= 9.375 \times 10^{-4} \times 121.625 \\
  &= 0.11402 \text{ kg·m}^2
\end{aligned}$$

Both methods agree perfectly. ✓

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Sources of Error and Their Effect

| Error Source | Effect on Computed I | Mitigation |
|--------------|----------------------|------------|
| String slipping on axle | Underestimated I | Ensure tight winding, rough axle surface |
| Friction at bearings | Overestimated I (apparent g reduced) | Lubricate bearings; use graphical extrapolation |
| Misreading time t (reaction-time error) | Large scatter in I | Repeat 5–6 trials, take mean |
| Mass not starting from rest | a ≠ 2h/t² | Use a holding pin released sharply |
| Inaccurate axle radius r | I ∝ r² amplified error | Measure r with Vernier at multiple points |
| String not vertical during fall | Component of mg lost | Guide the falling mass along a vertical line |

### 3.4 Graphical Method (Advanced — KTU Lab Viva Favourite)

If readings are taken for multiple hanging masses $m_1, m_2, \ldots$ and corresponding times $t_1, t_2, \ldots$, plot $m$ vs $a$ (or $a$ vs $m$).

The torque equation gives:

$$mg \cdot r - T_{friction} \cdot r = I\alpha \cdot r / r$$

For an idealised frictionless system: $m g r = I \alpha + T_{friction} r$. With $a = r\alpha$:

$$mg = \frac{I}{r^2} a + T_{friction}$$

A graph of $m$ vs $a$ yields slope $\dfrac{I}{gr^2}$, hence $I = \text{slope} \times g r^2$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of the Experimental Setup

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Flywheel Disk] --> A1[Horizontal Axle of Radius r]
    A1 --> A2[String Wound on Axle]
    A2 --> A3[Falling Mass m]
    A3 --> A4[Vertical Drop of Height h]
    A4 --> A5[Stopwatch Records Time t]
    B0[Mass m Provides Driving Force mg] --> B1[Net Force mg minus T equals ma]
    B1 --> B2[Tension T Computed from T equals m of g minus a]
    B2 --> B3[Torque tau equals T times r]
    C0[Linear a Couples to Angular alpha] --> C1[a equals r times alpha]
    C1 --> C2[Apply tau equals I times alpha]
    C2 --> C3[Output Moment of Inertia I equals m times r squared times g by a minus 1]
    A5 --> D1[Compute a equals 2h by t squared]
    D1 --> B1
    D1 --> C0
    A1 --> B3
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Procedural Flow — Step-by-Step Lab Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1 Measure Axle Radius r with Vernier] --> S2[Step 2 Measure Mass m]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Wind String on Axle and Mark Starting Height]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 Release Mass from Rest]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 Record Fall Time t for Distance h]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6 Compute a equals 2h by t squared]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7 Compute T equals m of g minus a]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8 Compute I equals Tr squared by a]
    S8 --> S9[Step 9 Repeat for Multiple Trials and Take Mean]
    S9 --> S10[Step 10 Tabulate and Report I in kg m squared]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Free-Body Concept Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TranslatingBody[Falling Mass m]
        M1[Weight mg acting downward]
        M2[Tension T acting upward]
        M3[Net force mg minus T equals ma downward]
    end
    subgraph RotatingBody[Flywheel plus Axle]
        F1[Tension T creates Torque T times r about axis]
        F2[Angular acceleration alpha equals a by r]
        F3[Moment of Inertia I is unknown to be found]
    end
    M3 -- Kinematic Coupling a equals r alpha --> F2
    M2 -- Same string transmits force --> F1
    F1 -- Newton's Law tau equals I alpha --> F3
```

### 4.4 Observation Table Template (For Lab Record)

| Trial No. | Mass m (kg) | Height h (m) | Time t (s) | a = 2h/t² (m/s²) | T = m(g − a) (N) | I = Tr²/a (kg·m²) | Mean I (kg·m²) |
|-----------|-------------|--------------|------------|------------------|------------------|--------------------|----------------|
| 1 | | | | | | | |
| 2 | | | | | | | |
| 3 | | | | | | | |
| 4 | | | | | | | |
| 5 | | | | | | | |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern]**
**Define Moment of Inertia of a rigid body. State its SI unit.**

**Model Answer:**
The Moment of Inertia (I) of a rigid body about a given axis is the sum of the products of the mass of each constituent particle and the square of its perpendicular distance from the axis of rotation. Mathematically, $I = \sum m_i r_i^2$. It represents the body's resistance to change in its rotational motion and is the rotational analogue of mass.

**SI Unit:** kg·m²

**Other Units:** g·cm², slug·ft² (in FPS system)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (often asked):** Moment of Inertia is *not* an "inertia" in the F = ma sense — it is a *geometric* property depending on both mass distribution and choice of axis. The same body can have different I about different axes.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Module 2 Short Note Pattern]**
**State and explain the formula used to determine the moment of inertia of a flywheel dynamically using a falling mass.**

**Model Answer:**
When a mass m attached to a string wound on the flywheel axle of radius r falls through a height h in time t from rest, the linear acceleration is:

$$a = \frac{2h}{t^2}$$

The tension in the string is $T = m(g - a)$, which produces torque $\tau = T r$ on the flywheel. With angular acceleration $\alpha = a/r$, applying $\tau = I \alpha$ yields:

$$I = \frac{m(g - a)\, r^2}{a} = m r^2 \left( \frac{g}{a} - 1 \right)$$

This is the working formula for the dynamic determination of moment of inertia.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — Full-Marks 14-Mark Question**

**[KTU University Exam — End Semester Pattern]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the moment of inertia of a flywheel about its axis of rotation, using the falling-mass method. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** In an experiment to determine the moment of inertia of a flywheel, the following observations were recorded:

- Hanging mass, $m = 2.0 \text{ kg}$
- Radius of the axle, $r = 2.5 \text{ cm} = 0.025 \text{ m}$
- Height of fall, $h = 1.2 \text{ m}$
- Time of fall, $t = 4.0 \text{ s}$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

Calculate the moment of inertia of the flywheel. Also compute the final angular velocity of the flywheel. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q-A:**

**(a) Derivation (7 Marks):**

See the **complete step-by-step derivation in Section 3.1** above. Key points the examiner expects:

1. Free-body diagram of falling mass and flywheel.
2. Translating equation: $mg - T = ma$ → $T = m(g - a)$ — **(1 mark)**
3. Rotating equation: $\tau = I\alpha$, with $\tau = Tr$ — **(1 mark)**
4. Kinematic coupling: $a = r\alpha$ — **(1 mark)**
5. Kinematic equation for fall: $a = 2h/t^2$ — **(1 mark)**
6. Final substitution yielding $I = m(g - a)r^2 / a$ — **(2 marks)**
7. Stating assumptions: inextensible string, no slip, no friction, mass starts from rest, string vertical. — **(1 mark)**

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Acceleration:**

$$a = \frac{2h}{t^2} = \frac{2 \times 1.2}{(4.0)^2} = \frac{2.4}{16} = 0.15 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Step 2 — Tension:**

$$T = m(g - a) = 2.0 \times (9.81 - 0.15) = 2.0 \times 9.66 = 19.32 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — Torque:**

$$\tau = T \cdot r = 19.32 \times 0.025 = 0.483 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 4 — Angular acceleration:**

$$\alpha = \frac{a}{r} = \frac{0.15}{0.025} = 6.0 \text{ rad/s}^2$$

**Step 5 — Moment of inertia:**

$$I = \frac{\tau}{\alpha} = \frac{0.483}{6.0} = 0.0805 \text{ kg·m}^2$$

**Alternatively using direct formula:**

$$I = \frac{m(g - a) r^2}{a} = \frac{2.0 \times 9.66 \times (0.025)^2}{0.15}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
I &= \frac{2.0 \times 9.66 \times 6.25 \times 10^{-4}}{0.15} \\
  &= \frac{12.075 \times 10^{-3}}{0.15} \\
  &= 0.0805 \text{ kg·m}^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Final angular velocity:**

The mass attains velocity $v = at = 0.15 \times 4.0 = 0.60 \text{ m/s}$. Hence:

$$\omega = \frac{v}{r} = \frac{0.60}{0.025} = 24.0 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Final Answers:**

$$\boxed{I = 0.0805 \text{ kg·m}^2, \quad \omega = 24.0 \text{ rad/s}}$$

---

#### **Question B — Alternative 14-Mark Question (Internal Choice)**

**(a)** Explain the principle and procedure of the experiment to determine the moment of inertia of a flywheel about its axis of rotation using a falling weight. List the precautions to be taken. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** During an experiment, a flywheel of axle diameter $2.5 \text{ cm}$ has a $1.0 \text{ kg}$ mass attached to a string wound around its axle. The mass falls through $0.9 \text{ m}$ from rest in $3.0 \text{ s}$. Determine:
  (i) The moment of inertia of the flywheel.
  (ii) The rotational kinetic energy of the flywheel at the instant the mass reaches the bottom.
  (Take $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$) **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q-B:**

**(a) Principle, Procedure & Precautions (7 Marks):**

**Principle (2 marks):** When a mass m hanging from a string wound on the axle of radius r falls under gravity, the linear motion of the mass is converted into rotational motion of the flywheel. The angular acceleration $\alpha$ of the flywheel is related to the linear acceleration $a$ of the mass by $\alpha = a/r$. Applying Newton's laws of motion to both translation (mass) and rotation (flywheel), we obtain the moment of inertia as:

$$I = \frac{m(g - a) r^2}{a}$$

**Procedure (3 marks):**
1. Measure the axle diameter with Vernier calipers at several points and find mean radius r.
2. Note the mass m attached to the string.
3. Wind the string on the axle and mark a fixed height h above the floor.
4. Release the mass from rest and use a stopwatch to record time t for the fall.
5. Repeat the experiment 5–6 times for accuracy; compute mean t.
6. Compute a, T, and finally I.

**Precautions (2 marks):**
- Flywheel axle must be horizontal (use spirit level).
- String must not slip on the axle.
- String must be vertical throughout the fall.
- Mass must be released from rest without any initial push.
- Use Vernier for accurate measurement of axle radius (because I ∝ r²).
- Take multiple readings and report mean I.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

Given: $m = 1.0 \text{ kg}$, $r = 2.5/2 = 1.25 \text{ cm} = 0.0125 \text{ m}$, $h = 0.9 \text{ m}$, $t = 3.0 \text{ s}$.

**Step 1 — Linear acceleration:**

$$a = \frac{2h}{t^2} = \frac{2 \times 0.9}{(3.0)^2} = \frac{1.8}{9.0} = 0.20 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Step 2 — Moment of inertia:**

$$I = \frac{m(g - a) r^2}{a} = \frac{1.0 \times (9.81 - 0.20) \times (0.0125)^2}{0.20}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
I &= \frac{1.0 \times 9.61 \times 1.5625 \times 10^{-4}}{0.20} \\
  &= \frac{15.0156 \times 10^{-4}}{0.20} \\
  &= 7.5078 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg·m}^2
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{I \approx 7.51 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg·m}^2}$$

**Step 3 — Final velocity of mass:**

$$v = at = 0.20 \times 3.0 = 0.60 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 4 — Final angular velocity:**

$$\omega = \frac{v}{r} = \frac{0.60}{0.0125} = 48.0 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 5 — Rotational KE:**

$$KE_{rot} = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 7.5078 \times 10^{-3} \times (48.0)^2$$

$$\begin{aligned}
KE_{rot} &= \frac{1}{2} \times 7.5078 \times 10^{-3} \times 2304 \\
         &= 0.5 \times 17.298 \\
         &= 8.649 \text{ J}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{KE_{rot} \approx 8.65 \text{ J}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> - **Forgetting the kinematic coupling** $a = r\alpha$: This is the most common deduction. Without it, the moment-of-inertia equation cannot be closed.
> - **Confusing radius r (axle) with radius R (flywheel)**: I refers to inertia about the flywheel's axis, but the *driving torque* uses the smaller axle radius where the string is wound.
> - **Mixing units**: Using r in cm while everything else is in SI units. Always convert r to **metres** before substituting.
> - **Using $g = 10 \text{ m/s}^2$ blindly**: Unless the question explicitly says so, use **g = 9.81 m/s²** in KTU exams.
> - **Stopping at the formula**: KTU values full substitution steps; showing $a$, then $T$, then $\alpha$ then I step-by-step fetches full marks.
> - **Ignoring the **"$<$**" in $T < mg$**: Stating $T = mg$ is wrong; tension is *less* because the mass is accelerating.
> - **Not stating assumptions** in derivation: KTU's outcome-based evaluation deducts 1 mark for missing assumptions list.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** Moment of inertia $I = \sum m_i r_i^2$; SI unit **kg·m²**.
- **Dynamic method** uses a falling mass, not geometry — measure *h*, *t*, *m*, and *r*.
- **Core working formula:** $\displaystyle I = \frac{m(g - a)\, r^2}{a}$, where $a = \dfrac{2h}{t^2}$.
- **Two Newton laws used simultaneously:** translation (mass) and rotation (flywheel).
- **Kinematic bridge equation:** $a = r\alpha$ — never omit this in derivations.
- **Tension in string:** $T = m(g - a)$, *always less than mg*.
- **Final angular velocity:** $\omega = v/r = at/r$.
- **Rotational KE:** $\frac{1}{2}I\omega^2$ — useful for energy cross-checks.
- **I ∝ r²:** Even small error in measuring axle radius r produces large error in I — measure r with Vernier.
- **Multiple readings essential:** Time measurement carries human reaction-time error (~0.2 s); take ≥ 5 readings and average.
- **Assumptions to state:** inextensible string, no slip, frictionless bearings, vertical fall, mass starting from rest.
- **Graphical method:** plot $m$ vs $a$; slope = $I/(gr^2)$.
- **Engineering applications:** IC engines (smoothing torque), punch presses (energy storage), regenerative braking (EVs), gyroscopes.
- **Common units check:** If r is in cm, convert to m; if m is in grams, convert to kg — *before* substitution.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of Modulus of Elasticity of a Mild Steel Specimen Using Strain Gauges
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUL407 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of Modulus of Elasticity of a Mild Steel Specimen Using Strain Gauges

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Modulus of Elasticity (Young's Modulus, E)** is defined as the ratio of longitudinal stress (σ) to longitudinal strain (ε) within the elastic limit of a material, as expressed by **Hooke's Law**:

$$E = \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon} = \frac{F/A}{\Delta L/L_0}$$

For a **Mild Steel (MS) Specimen**, the standard expected value of Young's Modulus lies in the range of **1.9 × 10⁵ N/mm² to 2.1 × 10⁵ N/mm² (≈ 200 GPa)**, as per IS 2062 standards.

A **Strain Gauge** is a passive resistive transducer whose electrical resistance changes proportionally with applied mechanical strain. The fundamental governing relation is the **Gauge Factor (GF)**:

$$GF = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon}$$

For standard foil-type strain gauges, **GF ≈ 2.0 ± 1%** (manufacturer specified), making it a highly precise sensor for microstrain measurement.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This experiment integrates *Strength of Materials* theory (Hooke's Law, Stress-Strain Relationship) with *Measurement & Instrumentation* concepts (Wheatstone Bridge, Gauge Factor). Students must demonstrate both theoretical clarity and practical lab competence in the End Semester Evaluation (ESE) viva and record submission.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine stretching a **rubber band** with your fingers. You can *see* it elongate. Now, imagine stretching a **steel rod** by the same amount visually — you cannot see it elongate because steel is roughly **10,000 times stiffer** than rubber. However, the steel rod *is* deforming, just at the **microscopic level (microstrain, µε = 10⁻⁶)**.

A **strain gauge** is essentially a tiny **"electrical ruler"** that detects this imperceptible deformation. It works on a beautifully simple principle:

- The gauge contains a thin metallic foil arranged in a **zig-zag (serpentine) pattern**.
- When the underlying specimen stretches, the foil stretches too.
- The foil's **length increases** and **cross-sectional area decreases** (since volume is conserved).
- Since electrical resistance $R = \rho L / A$, an increase in $L$ and a decrease in $A$ both **increase R**.
- This tiny change in resistance is measured using a **Wheatstone Bridge** and converted back into strain.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Summary:** The strain gauge is a translator — it converts the *silent language of mechanical deformation* into the *electrical language of resistance change*, which we can measure with high precision.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value for Mild Steel |
|---|---|
| Young's Modulus (E) | **2.0 × 10⁵ N/mm²** (200 GPa) |
| Poisson's Ratio (ν) | **0.30** |
| Density (ρ) | **7850 kg/m³** |
| Yield Stress (σ_y) | **250 N/mm²** (for Fe 410 grade) |
| Ultimate Tensile Stress | **410 N/mm²** |
| Gauge Factor (typical foil gauge) | **2.0 ± 1%** |
| Gauge Resistance (R) | **120 Ω, 350 Ω, or 1000 Ω** (standard) |
| Operating Temperature Range | **−50 °C to +150 °C** (typical) |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Step-by-Step Logic

The experiment is built on **three interconnected pillars of physics**:

**Pillar 1: Hooke's Law (Mechanics)**
- Within the elastic limit, stress is linearly proportional to strain.
- $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$
- Rearranging: $E = \sigma / \varepsilon$
- **Why?** Because at the atomic level, interatomic bonds behave like tiny springs obeying linear force-displacement relations.

**Pillar 2: Piezoresistive Effect (Electrical)**
- Discovered by Lord Kelvin (1856); refined by Edward Simmons (1938, bonded wire gauge).
- A conductor's resistance changes when subjected to mechanical strain.
- The fractional change in resistance $\Delta R / R$ is proportional to the strain $\varepsilon$.
- **How?** $R = \rho L / A$; differentiating logarithmically: $\Delta R / R = \Delta \rho / \rho + \Delta L / L - \Delta A / A$.
- For metallic gauges, the dominant term is geometric ($\Delta L / L$ and $\Delta A / A$), with a small piezoresistive contribution ($\Delta \rho / \rho$).

**Pillar 3: Wheatstone Bridge Circuit (Measurement)**
- Used to convert the tiny resistance change (often milliohms) into a measurable voltage.
- A balanced bridge produces zero output voltage; an imbalance indicates strain.
- Output voltage: $V_o = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R_1 R_3 - R_2 R_4}{(R_1 + R_2)(R_3 + R_4)}$
- For a **quarter-bridge** (one active gauge, three fixed precision resistors): $V_o \approx \frac{V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon}{4}$ (for small strains).

### 2.2 The Real-World Engineering Utility

This experiment's underlying principle powers:

- **Structural Health Monitoring (SHM)** of bridges, dams, and skyscrapers
- **Load cells** in weighing scales and industrial presses
- **Aerospace stress analysis** on aircraft wings and fuselage
- **Pressure transducers** in hydraulic and pneumatic systems
- **Torque measurement** on rotating shafts (using slip rings or telemetry)
- **Medical devices** such as digital blood pressure monitors and force platforms

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL KTU EXAM NOTE:** Always state units clearly and convert consistently. Marks are deducted for unit mismatches (e.g., mixing N/mm² with MPa incorrectly, or N with kN).

| S.No | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma = P / A$ | Engineering stress | N/mm² (MPa) |
| 2 | $A = b \times t$ | Area of rectangular cross-section | mm² |
| 3 | $A = \pi d^2 / 4$ | Area of circular cross-section | mm² |
| 4 | $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L_0$ | Engineering strain | Dimensionless (or µε) |
| 5 | $E = \sigma / \varepsilon$ | Young's Modulus | N/mm² or GPa |
| 6 | $GF = (\Delta R / R) / \varepsilon$ | Gauge Factor | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $\Delta R / R = GF \cdot \varepsilon$ | Fractional resistance change | Dimensionless |
| 8 | $V_o = \frac{V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon}{4}$ | Quarter-bridge output voltage | Volts (V) |
| 9 | $\Delta L = \varepsilon \cdot L_0$ | Elongation | mm |
| 10 | $P_{max} = \sigma_y \times A$ | Maximum permissible load (within elastic limit) | N |

**Key Note on Cross-Modal Conversion:**
- $1 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 1 \text{ MPa} = 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 10^{-3} \text{ GPa}$
- $1 \text{ kN} = 1000 \text{ N}$
- $1 \text{ microstrain } (\mu\varepsilon) = 10^{-6} \text{ strain}$

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: From Resistance Change to Strain

We will rigorously derive the **gauge factor expression** from first principles, as this is a **favourite KTU 14-mark question**.

**Starting Point — Resistance of a Conductor:**

$$R = \rho \frac{L}{A}$$

Where:
- $R$ = Electrical resistance (Ω)
- $\rho$ = Resistivity of the material (Ω·m)
- $L$ = Length of the conductor (m)
- $A$ = Cross-sectional area (m²)

**Step 1 — Logarithmic Differentiation (to find fractional change)**

Take the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$\ln R = \ln \rho + \ln L - \ln A$$

Differentiating both sides with respect to strain $\varepsilon$:

$$\frac{1}{R} \frac{dR}{d\varepsilon} = \frac{1}{\rho} \frac{d\rho}{d\varepsilon} + \frac{1}{L} \frac{dL}{d\varepsilon} - \frac{1}{A} \frac{dA}{d\varepsilon}$$

Expressed in differential form (for small changes):

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} + \frac{\Delta L}{L} - \frac{\Delta A}{A}$$

**Step 2 — Relate Length Change to Strain**

By definition of longitudinal strain:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L}$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{\Delta L}{L} = \varepsilon$$

**Step 3 — Relate Area Change to Strain (via Poisson's Ratio)**

For a cylindrical conductor of diameter $d$, area $A = \pi d^2 / 4$.
Differentiating logarithmically: $\Delta A / A = 2 \cdot (\Delta d / d)$.
Lateral strain = $-\nu \cdot \varepsilon$ (negative sign because diameter contracts when length extends).
Therefore:

$$\frac{\Delta d}{d} = -\nu \cdot \varepsilon \implies \frac{\Delta A}{A} = -2\nu \varepsilon$$

**Step 4 — Piezoresistive Term**

For metallic strain gauges, the resistivity change is empirically related to strain through a constant $\pi_L$ (longitudinal piezoresistive coefficient). However, for most engineering approximations, the geometric terms dominate, and we write:

$$\frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} = \pi_L \cdot E \cdot \varepsilon$$

**Step 5 — Combine All Terms**

Substituting back:

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \pi_L \cdot E \cdot \varepsilon + \varepsilon - (-2\nu \varepsilon)$$

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \varepsilon \left( \pi_L E + 1 + 2\nu \right)$$

**Step 6 — Define the Gauge Factor**

By definition:

$$GF = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon}$$

Therefore, the final compact form is:

$$\boxed{GF = 1 + 2\nu + \pi_L E}$$

For most metallic gauges, $\pi_L E$ is very small compared to $(1 + 2\nu)$, giving $GF \approx 1 + 2\nu$.
For steel with $\nu = 0.3$: $GF \approx 1 + 0.6 = 1.6$.
For constantan (alloy with negligible piezoresistive effect): $GF \approx 2.0$ (the industry standard).

### 3.2 Worked Numerical — Typical KTU Exam Problem

> **Problem:** A mild steel specimen of rectangular cross-section (25 mm × 6 mm) and gauge length 200 mm is subjected to a tensile load of 30 kN. A strain gauge of gauge factor 2.1 and resistance 120 Ω is bonded to it. Find: (a) the stress, (b) the strain, (c) the change in gauge resistance, and (d) the Young's Modulus if the strain reading is 380 µε.

**Given Data:**
- Width, $b = 25$ mm
- Thickness, $t = 6$ mm
- Gauge length, $L_0 = 200$ mm
- Load, $P = 30$ kN = 30,000 N
- Gauge factor, $GF = 2.1$
- Gauge resistance, $R = 120$ Ω
- Measured strain, $\varepsilon = 380 \times 10^{-6}$

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Stress**

$$\sigma = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{P}{b \times t} = \frac{30000}{25 \times 6} = \frac{30000}{150}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma = 200 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 200 \text{ MPa}}$$

**Part (b) — Theoretical Strain (assuming E = 2 × 10⁵ MPa)**

$$\varepsilon_{theoretical} = \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{200}{2 \times 10^5} = 1 \times 10^{-3} = 1000 \text{ } \mu\varepsilon$$

**Part (c) — Change in Gauge Resistance**

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = GF \times \varepsilon = 2.1 \times 380 \times 10^{-6} = 7.98 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\Delta R = 7.98 \times 10^{-4} \times 120 = 0.09576 \text{ } \Omega$$

$$\boxed{\Delta R \approx 0.0958 \text{ } \Omega = 95.76 \text{ m}\Omega}$$

**Part (d) — Young's Modulus from Measured Strain**

$$E = \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_{measured}} = \frac{200 \text{ N/mm}^2}{380 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$E = \frac{200}{3.8 \times 10^{-4}} = 5.263 \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

$$\boxed{E \approx 2.11 \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}^2 \text{ (i.e., 211 GPa)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** The experimental value (211 GPa) is within 5.5% of the standard mild steel value (200 GPa). This deviation is **acceptable** in KTU lab evaluations and is attributed to gauge misalignment, temperature effects, and material inhomogeneity.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Experimental Setup

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[LoadFrame UTM] --> B[MSpecimen Mild Steel Specimen]
    B --> C[SGA Strain Gauge Active]
    B --> SGD[SGComp Strain Gauge Dummy for Temp Compensation]
    C --> E[WB Wheatstone Bridge Quarter Bridge]
    SGD --> E
    E --> F[SI Strain Indicator Digital Display]
    F --> G[DL Data Logger]
    G --> H[PC PC for Graph Plotting Stress vs Strain]
    P[PS Power Supply 5V DC Excitation] --> E
    L[LVDT Linear Variable Differential Transformer for Cross-Check] --> F
    style A fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F77B4
    style B fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#FF7F0E
    style C fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2CA02C
    style SGD fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#9467BD
    style E fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#D62728
    style F fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#17A2B8
    style G fill:#FFF8E1,stroke:#BCBD22
    style H fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#E377C2
    style P fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#5DA5DA
    style L fill:#F1F8E9,stroke:#8EBA42
```

### 4.2 Functional Flow Description

| Block | Function | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|
| LoadFrame UTM | Applies controlled tensile load | Ensures monotonic, uniaxial loading |
| MSpecimen | Test sample (Fe 410 grade) | Standardized geometry for repeatable results |
| SGA | Active gauge on specimen | Converts strain → resistance change |
| SGComp | Dummy gauge on unstamped piece | Cancels thermal strain (passive temperature compensation) |
| WB | Wheatstone bridge circuit | Converts $\Delta R$ to voltage signal |
| PS | Excitation voltage source | Provides $V_{ex} = 5$ V or 10 V DC |
| SI | Strain indicator | Displays strain in $\mu\varepsilon$ |
| LVDT | Optional cross-validation sensor | Mechanical backup measurement |
| DL + PC | Data acquisition | Generates stress-strain plot for E determination |

### 4.3 Wheatstone Bridge Schematic (Quarter-Bridge Configuration)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VEX[Vex DC Source] --> N1
    N1 --> R1[Fixed R Precision Resistor]
    N1 --> R2[Fixed R Precision Resistor]
    R1 --> N2
    R2 --> N3
    N2 --> R3[Fixed R Precision Resistor]
    N3 --> RG[Active Strain Gauge RG]
    R3 --> N4
    RG --> N4
    N2 --> VoutA
    N3 --> VoutB
    VoutA --> VOM[Voltmeter Micro-volt]
    VoutB --> VOM
    style VEX fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#D62728
    style RG fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2CA02C
    style VOM fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#17A2B8
    style R1 fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#FF7F0E
    style R2 fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#FF7F0E
    style R3 fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#FF7F0E
```

**Bridge Balance Condition:**

$$R_1 \cdot RG = R_2 \cdot R_3$$

When the specimen is strained, $RG$ changes by $\Delta R$, unbalancing the bridge and producing output voltage $V_o$ proportional to strain.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define Gauge Factor of a strain gauge. What is its typical value for a metallic foil gauge?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Gauge Factor (GF) is defined as the **ratio of fractional change in resistance ($\Delta R / R$) to the mechanical strain ($\varepsilon$)** producing it:

$$GF = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon}$$

It is a **dimensionless quantity**. For a standard metallic foil gauge (typically made of **constantan or Karma alloy**), the typical value is **GF ≈ 2.0 ± 1%**. For semiconductor gauges, GF can be as high as **100–200**, but they are more temperature-sensitive.

*(Mark distribution: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for typical value and material.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Why is a dummy strain gauge used in a Wheatstone bridge circuit?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **dummy (compensating) gauge** is mounted on an **unstrained piece of the same material** and placed in an adjacent arm of the Wheatstone bridge. Its purpose is to provide **passive temperature compensation**. When ambient temperature changes, both the active and dummy gauges experience **identical thermal strain** ($\varepsilon_T$). Since they are in **adjacent arms of the bridge**, the thermal-induced $\Delta R$ cancels out, leaving only the **mechanical strain** in the output voltage. This eliminates false readings due to temperature drift and significantly improves measurement accuracy.

*(Mark distribution: 1 mark for purpose, 1 mark for thermal compensation logic, 1 mark for bridge cancellation explanation.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Writing $E$ in mixed units (e.g., N/mm² for stress and m/m for strain). Always convert strain to a pure ratio first, OR keep it in µε and use $\sigma$ in same consistent unit basis.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Forgetting to convert kN to N when computing stress.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Confusing the *active* and *dummy* gauge roles. Always state explicitly that dummy is on **unstrained material**.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Skipping the gauge factor formula derivation — KTU examiners allocate **4–5 marks** for the derivation alone.
> - **Common Mistake #5:** Not mentioning the **linear elastic limit** assumption when applying Hooke's Law.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **gauge factor** of a strain gauge in terms of Poisson's ratio and piezoresistive coefficient. A strain gauge with $R = 350$ Ω and $GF = 2.05$ is bonded to a steel specimen. If the gauge resistance changes by 0.7 Ω, calculate the strain and the corresponding stress. Take $E = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm². **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **Wheatstone bridge circuit** used for strain measurement. Explain how a quarter-bridge, half-bridge, and full-bridge configuration differ in sensitivity and temperature compensation. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of Gauge Factor (5 marks) + Numerical (2 marks):**

Starting with the resistance equation: $R = \rho L / A$.

Differentiating logarithmically:

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} + \frac{\Delta L}{L} - \frac{\Delta A}{A}$$

Substituting:
- $\Delta L / L = \varepsilon$
- $\Delta A / A = -2\nu \varepsilon$
- $\Delta \rho / \rho = \pi_L E \varepsilon$

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \pi_L E \varepsilon + \varepsilon + 2\nu \varepsilon = \varepsilon (\pi_L E + 1 + 2\nu)$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{GF = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon} = 1 + 2\nu + \pi_L E}$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given: $R = 350$ Ω, $GF = 2.05$, $\Delta R = 0.7$ Ω, $E = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm².

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta R / R}{GF} = \frac{0.7 / 350}{2.05} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-3}}{2.05} = 9.756 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon \approx 9.76 \times 10^{-4} = 975.6 \text{ } \mu\varepsilon}$$

Stress:

$$\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon = 2 \times 10^5 \times 9.756 \times 10^{-4} = 195.12 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

$$\boxed{\sigma \approx 195.12 \text{ MPa}}$$

---

**(b) Wheatstone Bridge Description (7 Marks):**

The Wheatstone bridge consists of **four resistive arms** ($R_1, R_2, R_3, R_G$) arranged in a diamond configuration, excited by a DC voltage source $V_{ex}$. The output voltage $V_o$ is measured across the midpoints.

General output equation:

$$V_o = V_{ex} \left( \frac{R_G}{R_G + R_1} - \frac{R_3}{R_3 + R_2} \right)$$

**Comparison of Bridge Configurations:**

| Configuration | Active Gauges | Sensitivity Factor | Temperature Compensation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Quarter-Bridge | 1 | $V_o = V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon / 4$ | Requires dummy gauge |
| Half-Bridge | 2 (opposite arms) | $V_o = V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon / 2$ | Built-in (2 active gauges share temperature) |
| Full-Bridge | 4 | $V_o = V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon$ | Excellent (all gauges compensate) |

**Key Points for Full Marks:**
- Sensitivity is **proportional to the number of active gauges** (1:2:4 ratio).
- Half-bridge with **two gauges on opposite faces** also **doubles sensitivity** and **cancels bending strain**.
- Full-bridge with **four gauges** gives **maximum sensitivity and noise rejection**, ideal for load cells.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **experimental procedure** to determine Young's Modulus of a mild steel specimen using strain gauges. Include a neat sketch of the setup and tabulate a typical observation table. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** During a tensile test on a mild steel specimen of cross-section 20 mm × 5 mm and gauge length 100 mm, the following observations were recorded:

| Load (kN) | 0 | 4 | 8 | 12 | 16 | 20 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Strain (µε) | 0 | 200 | 405 | 610 | 815 | 1010 |

Plot the **stress–strain curve**, determine the **Young's Modulus**, and comment on the linearity. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Experimental Procedure (7 Marks):**

1. **Specimen Preparation:** Cut a mild steel flat bar of standard dimensions (e.g., 200 mm gauge length, rectangular cross-section). Polish the surface where the gauge will be bonded using emery paper (grades 220 → 400 → 600). Clean with acetone.
2. **Gauge Bonding:** Apply a thin layer of cyanoacrylate adhesive (e.g., M-Bond 200) on the cleaned surface. Mount the strain gauge carefully along the longitudinal axis (axially) using tweezers. Apply firm pressure for 60 seconds. Cure for 5–10 minutes.
3. **Wiring:** Solder lead wires to the gauge terminals. Connect the gauge to the **Wheatstone bridge** in a **quarter-bridge configuration**. Connect the dummy gauge on an unstrained piece to the adjacent arm.
4. **Mounting:** Clamp the specimen in the **Universal Testing Machine (UTM)** grips, ensuring axial alignment.
5. **Initial Reading:** With zero load, balance the bridge (set strain indicator to zero).
6. **Loading:** Apply incremental tensile loads (e.g., 2 kN steps) up to about 50% of the yield load. Record the strain reading at each step.
7. **Unloading:** Gradually release the load and verify that strain returns to zero (confirms elastic behaviour).
8. **Calculations:** Compute stress $\sigma = P/A$ and plot stress vs strain. The slope gives **E**.

**Typical Observation Table:**

| S.No | Load P (kN) | Strain $\varepsilon$ ($\mu\varepsilon$) | Stress $\sigma$ (N/mm²) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| 2 | 4 | $\varepsilon_1$ | 40 |
| 3 | 8 | $\varepsilon_2$ | 80 |
| ... | ... | ... | ... |

---

**(b) Numerical — Stress-Strain and E Determination (7 Marks):**

**Cross-sectional Area:**

$$A = 20 \times 5 = 100 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Stress Calculations:**

$$\sigma_1 = \frac{4000}{100} = 40 \text{ N/mm}^2; \quad \sigma_2 = 80; \quad \sigma_3 = 120; \quad \sigma_4 = 160; \quad \sigma_5 = 200 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**Strain Conversions:**

$\varepsilon_1 = 200 \times 10^{-6}$, etc.

**Method 1 — Average E from each pair:**

$$E_1 = \frac{40}{200 \times 10^{-6}} = 2.0 \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

$$E_2 = \frac{80}{405 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.975 \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

$$E_3 = \frac{120}{610 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.967 \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

$$E_4 = \frac{160}{815 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.963 \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

$$E_5 = \frac{200}{1010 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.980 \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**Mean Young's Modulus:**

$$E_{mean} = \frac{2.0 + 1.975 + 1.967 + 1.963 + 1.980}{5} \times 10^5$$

$$E_{mean} = \frac{9.885}{5} \times 10^5 = 1.977 \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

$$\boxed{E_{mean} \approx 1.98 \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 198 \text{ GPa}}$$

**Method 2 — Least Squares Fit (more accurate):**

Using slope of best-fit line through origin (since zero load = zero strain):

$$E = \frac{\sum \sigma \varepsilon}{\sum \varepsilon^2} \cdot \frac{1}{1} = \frac{\sum \sigma \varepsilon}{\sum \varepsilon^2}$$

(Computations omitted for brevity, but result is similar: $E \approx 1.98 \times 10^5$ N/mm²).

**Comment on Linearity:**
The stress-strain curve is **linear** within the elastic region (R² ≈ 0.9998), confirming Hooke's Law validity for mild steel. The slight deviation in the last reading (1010 µε at 200 MPa) is approaching the **proportional limit** (~210 MPa for Fe 410 grade).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ **Young's Modulus** is the slope of the **stress–strain curve** within the elastic limit: $E = \sigma / \varepsilon$.
- ✅ **Strain Gauge** converts mechanical deformation into resistance change via the **piezoresistive + geometric effect**.
- ✅ **Gauge Factor** $GF = 1 + 2\nu + \pi_L E \approx 2.0$ for metallic foil gauges.
- ✅ **Quarter-bridge** uses **1 active gauge**; needs a **dummy gauge** for temperature compensation.
- ✅ **Half-bridge** doubles sensitivity and provides built-in temperature compensation.
- ✅ **Full-bridge** quadruples sensitivity — used in **precision load cells**.
- ✅ **Standard mild steel E** = **2.0 × 10⁵ N/mm² = 200 GPa**.
- ✅ **Stress formula:** $\sigma = P / A$; for rectangular bar: $A = b \times t$.
- ✅ **Strain must be unitless** when used in $E = \sigma / \varepsilon$, or use $\mu\varepsilon$ consistently.
- ✅ **Apply loads within the elastic limit** (typically < 50% of yield load) to avoid permanent deformation.
- ✅ **Always zero/balance the bridge** before applying load to remove initial offset.
- ✅ **Align the strain gauge** exactly along the **axis of loading** to measure only longitudinal strain.
- ✅ **Plot a graph** of $\sigma$ (Y-axis) vs $\varepsilon$ (X-axis); slope = E.
- ✅ **KTU Viva Question:** *"Why use constantan alloy for strain gauges?"* — Answer: Low temperature coefficient of resistance and high gauge factor stability.
- ✅ **KTU Viva Question:** *"What happens if the gauge is mounted transversely?"* — Answer: It measures lateral strain, related to longitudinal via Poisson's ratio.

---

**End of Module Experiment 2 Notes — PCMUL407**
*Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme assessment guidelines and NEP 2020 outcome-based education framework.*

topics.title: Determination of Coefficient of Friction (Steel on Steel, Steel on Wood, Steel on Asbestos) Using Inclined Plane and Sliding Friction Apparatus
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUL407 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of Coefficient of Friction (Steel on Steel, Steel on Wood, Steel on Asbestos) Using Inclined Plane and Sliding Friction Apparatus

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Coefficient of Friction (μ)** is a dimensionless scalar quantity that quantifies the ratio of the frictional force resisting motion between two contacting surfaces to the normal force pressing them together. In the context of this experiment, the **Static Coefficient of Friction (μs)** is determined experimentally by observing the angle at which a block resting on an inclined plane begins to slide. This critical angle is termed the **Angle of Repose (θ)** or **Angle of Friction**.

For the three test pairs in this experiment:
- **Steel on Steel** — typical μs ≈ **0.15 – 0.20**
- **Steel on Wood** — typical μs ≈ **0.20 – 0.40**
- **Steel on Asbestos** — typical μs ≈ **0.30 – 0.40**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The experiment is conducted using the **Inclined Plane and Sliding Friction Apparatus**, a standard lab instrument under Module 2 of PCMUL407. It is mapped to the Course Outcome dealing with the **application of friction principles in hydraulic machine components** (e.g., bearing friction, packing friction in pumps).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Staircase Trick"

Imagine pushing a heavy wooden box across a smooth marble floor versus a rough carpet. The marble floor offers much less resistance — the box slides easily. Now tilt that same marble floor slowly, like a ramp. As the tilt increases, gravity pulls the box more strongly downhill. At a certain tilt angle, the box will begin to slide on its own — that tilt angle is the **Angle of Repose**.

- A **higher angle** → rougher surface → **higher μ**.
- A **lower angle** → smoother surface → **lower μ**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Takeaway:** The tangent of the angle at which an object just begins to slide on an inclined plane directly gives the static coefficient of friction. No spring balance or force measurement is required — only geometry!

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Force Analysis on the Inclined Plane

When a block of weight **W = mg** rests on an inclined plane inclined at angle **θ** to the horizontal, the following forces act:

**Step 1 — Resolve the weight:**
The weight vector **W** is resolved into two rectangular components relative to the inclined surface:
- Component **perpendicular** to the inclined surface (pressing into the surface): **N = W cos θ**
- Component **parallel** to the inclined surface (trying to slide the block downhill): **F_tangential = W sin θ**

**Step 2 — Identify the resisting force:**
The frictional force **F** opposes the impending motion and acts **up the plane**. By Coulomb's law of dry friction:
$$F = \mu_s \cdot N = \mu_s \cdot W \cos\theta$$

**Step 3 — Apply the equilibrium condition at the threshold of sliding:**
Just as the block is on the verge of sliding (impending motion), the system is in **limiting equilibrium**. The tangential pull of gravity exactly balances the maximum static friction:
$$W \sin\theta = \mu_s \cdot W \cos\theta$$

**Step 4 — Simplify to obtain the working formula:**
The weight **W** cancels out from both sides, yielding the elegant relationship:
$$\mu_s = \tan\theta$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**
The coefficient of static friction is **numerically equal to the tangent of the angle of repose**. This is independent of the mass of the block and the gravitational acceleration.

## 2.2 Why This Works for All Three Material Pairs

| Material Pair | Surface Interaction | Typical Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| Steel on Steel | Metal-on-metal, very smooth at microscale | Lowest μ, slides at a small tilt |
| Steel on Wood | Hard-on-soft, wood fibers deform | Medium μ, slides at moderate tilt |
| Steel on Asbestos | Fibrous rough texture | Higher μ, slides at larger tilt |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** This principle is used in designing **brake linings** (automotive engineering), **chute slopes** (bulk material handling), **belt conveyors** (mining/manufacturing), and **friction dampers** (civil/mechanical structures).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\mu_s = \tan\theta$ | Coefficient of static friction from angle of repose | dimensionless |
| 2 | $W = m \cdot g$ | Weight of the test block (taken on Earth) | N |
| 3 | $N = W \cos\theta$ | Normal reaction on inclined surface | N |
| 4 | $F_t = W \sin\theta$ | Tangential (driving) component along plane | N |
| 5 | $F_{friction} = \mu_s \cdot N$ | Coulomb's law of dry friction | N |
| 6 | $\theta_{repose} = \tan^{-1}(\mu_s)$ | Angle of repose expressed from μ | degrees or radians |
| 7 | $\mu_k \leq \mu_s$ | Kinetic friction coefficient is always ≤ static (verified in part B of experiment) | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strict Substitution Rule:** In all markdown tables throughout these notes, the vertical bar symbol has been replaced with `\vert` or `\mid` wherever a modulus or absolute-value notation appears, to preserve table integrity.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — From Newton's Laws to μ = tan θ

**Given:**
A rigid block of mass **m** rests on a flat inclined surface. The surface is slowly tilted until the block is at the threshold of sliding. The inclination angle at this instant is **θ**.

**Free Body Diagram Forces:**
1. Weight **W** = mg, acting vertically downward through the center of gravity.
2. Normal reaction **N**, acting perpendicular to the inclined surface (away from surface).
3. Static friction force **F**, acting parallel to the inclined surface (up the slope, opposing impending motion).

**Derivation (with every step explicit):**

**Step A — Resolve weight into components along and perpendicular to the plane:**

The angle between the vertical weight vector and the normal to the plane is **θ** (because the plane itself makes angle θ with the horizontal, so its normal makes angle θ with the vertical).

Therefore:

Component of W perpendicular to plane (into surface):
$$W_\perp = W \cos\theta$$

Component of W parallel to plane (along downhill direction):
$$W_\parallel = W \sin\theta$$

**Step B — Apply perpendicular equilibrium (no acceleration normal to plane):**

The block is not lifting off the surface nor penetrating it, so:
$$\sum F_\perp = 0 \implies N - W \cos\theta = 0$$

Therefore:
$$N = W \cos\theta$$

**Step C — Apply parallel equilibrium at the verge of sliding (impending motion):**

At the critical angle **θ**, the block is just about to move, so it is still in static equilibrium. The net force along the plane is zero, with friction acting up the plane to balance the tangential weight component:
$$\sum F_\parallel = 0 \implies F - W \sin\theta = 0$$

Therefore:
$$F = W \sin\theta$$

**Step D — Apply Coulomb's Law of Limiting Friction:**

At impending motion, the friction force attains its maximum value, called **limiting friction**:
$$F_{max} = \mu_s \cdot N$$

Substituting from Steps B and C:
$$W \sin\theta = \mu_s \cdot (W \cos\theta)$$

**Step E — Simplify:**

Divide both sides by **W cos θ** (assuming cos θ ≠ 0, i.e., the plane is not vertical):
$$\frac{W \sin\theta}{W \cos\theta} = \mu_s$$

$$\mu_s = \frac{\sin\theta}{\cos\theta} = \tan\theta$$

$$\boxed{\mu_s = \tan\theta}$$

This is the **master equation** for the experiment.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Variation of Forces with Angle θ

The table below summarizes how the forces and the friction coefficient evolve as the incline angle is gradually increased. All forces are normalized with respect to **W** for clarity.

| Incline Angle θ | Normal Reaction N/W | Driving Force (W sin θ)/W | Friction Required F/W | Sliding Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0° (horizontal) | 1.000 | 0.000 | 0.000 | At rest, no friction needed |
| 15° | 0.966 | 0.259 | 0.259 | At rest, friction supplies exactly 0.259 W |
| 30° | 0.866 | 0.500 | 0.500 | At rest, friction supplies 0.500 W |
| 45° | 0.707 | 0.707 | 0.707 | Critical — limiting friction |
| 60° | 0.500 | 0.866 | 0.866 | Sliding — friction can only supply 0.500 W |
| 90° (vertical) | 0.000 | 1.000 | 1.000 | Block falls freely |

> [!NOTE]
> At θ = 45°, μ = tan 45° = 1.0. This means for a material with μs = 1.0, the block just begins to slide at 45°. **Steel on asbestos** has μs less than 1, so it slides well before this angle.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** In a friction experiment using an inclined plane, a steel block on a wooden surface begins to slide when the plane is tilted to **23°**. Find the coefficient of static friction.

**Solution:**

Given: θ = 23°

Applying the working formula:
$$\mu_s = \tan(23°)$$

$$\mu_s = 0.4245$$

$$\boxed{\mu_s \approx 0.42}$$

**Verification by force method:** Let the block mass m = 2 kg, so W = 2 × 9.81 = 19.62 N.

$$N = W \cos 23° = 19.62 \times 0.9205 = 18.06 \text{ N}$$

$$F_{driving} = W \sin 23° = 19.62 \times 0.3907 = 7.66 \text{ N}$$

$$\mu_s = \frac{F}{N} = \frac{7.66}{18.06} = 0.4241 \approx 0.42 \checkmark$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Inclined Plane Apparatus Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Inclined Plane Apparatus Base] --> B[Adjustable Tilting Surface]
    B --> C[Graduated Arc Protractor 0 to 90 deg]
    B --> D[Sliding Friction Block with Test Surface]
    D --> D1[Face A: Steel Plate]
    D --> D2[Face B: Wood Plate]
    D --> D3[Face C: Asbestos Lining]
    A --> E[Graduated Scale for Vertical Height]
    A --> F[Locking Screw for Fixed Angle]
    F --> G[Stable Reading Position]

    H[Operator Tilts Plane Slowly] --> B
    I[Block Begins to Slide] --> J[Record Angle of Repose Theta]
    J --> K[Compute mu = tan Theta]
    K --> L[Repeat for All Three Material Pairs]
    L --> M[Tabulate Results and Compare with Standard Values]

    style A fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style B fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style D fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style K fill:#d62728,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style M fill:#9467bd,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Free Body Force Resolution on the Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    blockA[Block on Plane] -->|Weight W = mg| w1[Vertical Downward]
    w1 --> w2[Resolved into Components]
    w2 --> w3[W cos Theta - Normal Component]
    w2 --> w4[W sin Theta - Tangential Component]
    w4 --> w5[Driving Force Down the Plane]
    blockA -->|Normal N| n1[N = W cos Theta Upward]
    blockA -->|Friction F| f1[F = mu N Up the Plane]
    w5 -->|Impending Motion Condition| cond[W sin Theta = mu W cos Theta]
    f1 -->|Apply Coulomb Law| cond
    cond --> result[mu = tan Theta]

    style blockA fill:#8c564b,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style cond fill:#e377c2,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style result fill:#bcbd22,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Apparatus Description (Block-Level Functional Map)

The **Inclined Plane and Sliding Friction Apparatus** consists of the following subsystems:

| Subsystem | Function |
|---|---|
| **Base Plate** | Provides rigid support and houses the pivot/hinge for tilting |
| **Tilting Platform** | The inclined surface where the block is placed; angle is varied manually |
| **Protractor Arc** | Graduated from 0° to 90°; gives direct reading of angle θ |
| **Sliding Block** | A rectangular block with detachable faces for material change |
| **Test Faces** | Three interchangeable surfaces — steel, wood, asbestos |
| **Locking Mechanism** | Screw clamp to fix the platform at the desired angle for steady reading |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the term "Angle of Repose" and state its relationship with the coefficient of static friction.**

**Model Answer:**
The **Angle of Repose (θ)** is defined as the minimum angle of inclination of a plane with the horizontal at which a block placed on it just begins to slide down due to its own weight.

At this angle, the limiting static friction equals the tangential component of weight:
$$W \sin\theta = \mu_s W \cos\theta \implies \mu_s = \tan\theta$$

Hence, the coefficient of static friction is numerically equal to the **tangent of the angle of repose**.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Why is the coefficient of friction a dimensionless quantity?**

**Model Answer:**
The coefficient of friction **μ** is defined as the ratio of two forces:
$$\mu_s = \frac{F_{friction}}{N}$$

Since both the numerator and denominator have the same units (Newtons, N), the units cancel out:
$$[\mu_s] = \frac{N}{N} = \text{dimensionless}$$

Hence, μ is a pure number and has no units.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe with a neat sketch the experimental procedure to determine the coefficient of friction between steel and wood using an inclined plane apparatus. List the observations and tabulation format.

**(b) [7 Marks]** In the experiment, the steel block just begins to slide when the inclined plane is set to 28°. If the block mass is 1.5 kg, calculate (i) the coefficient of static friction, (ii) the normal reaction force, and (iii) the limiting friction force. Take g = 9.81 m/s².

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Apparatus Required:**
Inclined plane apparatus, sliding friction block with detachable steel face and wooden base, protractor arc, scale, spirit level.

**Procedure:**
1. Clean the contact surfaces of the steel block face and the wooden plane with a dry cloth.
2. Place the wooden surface on the inclined plane; mount the steel-faced block on top.
3. Using a spirit level, ensure the plane is initially horizontal (θ = 0°).
4. Slowly tilt the plane by adjusting the screw at the rear, increasing the angle gradually.
5. Observe the block carefully. Note the angle at which it **just begins to slide** without any external push.
6. Read this angle directly from the protractor arc — this is the angle of repose θ.
7. Repeat the experiment **five times** and take the **mean value** of θ for accuracy.
8. Calculate μ = tan θ (mean).

**Observation Table:**

| Trial No. | Angle of Repose θ (degrees) | tan θ = μs |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 27.5 | 0.5206 |
| 2 | 28.0 | 0.5317 |
| 3 | 28.5 | 0.5426 |
| 4 | 27.8 | 0.5282 |
| 5 | 28.2 | 0.5362 |
| **Mean** | **28.0** | **0.5319** |

**Model Solution for (b):**

Given: θ = 28°, m = 1.5 kg, g = 9.81 m/s²

**(i) Coefficient of static friction:**
$$\mu_s = \tan 28° = 0.5317$$
$$\boxed{\mu_s \approx 0.53}$$

**(ii) Normal reaction force:**
$$W = mg = 1.5 \times 9.81 = 14.715 \text{ N}$$
$$N = W \cos\theta = 14.715 \times \cos 28° = 14.715 \times 0.8829 = 12.99 \text{ N}$$
$$\boxed{N \approx 12.99 \text{ N}}$$

**(iii) Limiting friction force:**
$$F_{limiting} = \mu_s \cdot N = 0.5317 \times 12.99 = 6.91 \text{ N}$$
$$\boxed{F_{limiting} \approx 6.91 \text{ N}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare the coefficients of friction for the three material pairs — Steel on Steel, Steel on Wood, and Steel on Asbestos. State the physical reasons for the differences observed.

**(b) [7 Marks]** In a comparative friction test, the angle of repose for Steel on Steel is 11° and for Steel on Asbestos is 20°. Find (i) the ratio of their coefficients of friction, and (ii) the angle at which a block would rest in equilibrium on a plane inclined at 35° for the steel-on-asbestos pair, if a horizontal restraining force P is applied. Comment on the result.

**Model Solution for (a):**

| Material Pair | Approximate μs | Physical Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Steel on Steel | 0.15 – 0.20 | Metal surfaces are hard and can be polished smooth; microscopic asperities are smaller; some adhesion due to metallic bonding |
| Steel on Wood | 0.25 – 0.40 | Wood has fibrous texture; fibers deform and interlock with steel asperities; moderately high contact area |
| Steel on Asbestos | 0.30 – 0.50 | Asbestos has a rough, fibrous, layered structure; very high interlocking with metal asperities; highest friction |

**Reasons for differences:**
1. **Surface roughness** — Asbestos is roughest, hence highest μ.
2. **Hardness of materials** — Harder surfaces deform less and have smaller real contact area.
3. **Molecular adhesion** — Metallic pairs have some adhesion, but it is small compared to interlocking.
4. **Surface contamination** — Clean surfaces show higher μ than oily/greasy ones.

**Model Solution for (b):**

Given:
- θ_steel-steel = 11° → μ_ss = tan 11° = 0.1944
- θ_steel-asbestos = 20° → μ_sa = tan 20° = 0.3640

**(i) Ratio of coefficients of friction:**
