$$\frac{\mu_{sa}}{\mu_{ss}} = \frac{\tan 20°}{\tan 11°} = \frac{0.3640}{0.1944} = 1.872$$

$$\boxed{\frac{\mu_{sa}}{\mu_{ss}} \approx 1.87}$$

**Interpretation:** Steel on asbestos has **1.87 times** more friction than steel on steel.

**(ii) For the steel-on-asbestos pair at θ = 35°:**

Driving force:
$$W \sin 35° = W \times 0.5736$$

Available limiting friction:
$$F_{max} = \mu_{sa} W \cos 35° = 0.3640 \times W \times 0.8192 = 0.2982 W$$

Net unbalanced force pulling block downhill:
$$F_{net} = W \sin 35° - F_{max} = 0.5736W - 0.2982W = 0.2754 W$$

Required restraining force P (horizontal) to keep block in equilibrium:
Resolving horizontally, the horizontal force P must balance the net downhill component:
$$P = F_{net} = 0.2754 W$$

For W = mg = 1 × 9.81 = 9.81 N (taking m = 1 kg):
$$\boxed{P \approx 2.70 \text{ N}}$$

**Comment:** Since the angle 35° is **greater** than the angle of repose 20°, the block will slide on its own. A horizontal restraining force of approximately **2.70 N** (per unit weight) is needed to keep it stationary.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the angle unit** — Always quote θ in degrees and then take tan of the degree value. Don't convert to radians unless your calculator is in radian mode — this is the single biggest source of wrong numerical answers in KTU exams.
> 2. **Confusing static and kinetic friction** — This experiment measures **static** friction. The block must be observed at the **impending motion**, not after it has slid.
> 3. **Not taking multiple readings** — KTU examiners specifically look for **mean of 5 trials** in the observation table. A single reading invites mark deduction.
> 4. **Misreading the protractor** — Always read the angle perpendicular to the scale. A parallax error of even 1° changes μ significantly.
> 5. **Unit errors in friction force calculation** — Friction force is in Newtons (N), but μ is dimensionless. Do not append any units to μ.
> 6. **Not specifying the material pair** — Always write "Steel on Wood" rather than just "wood" — the convention is "moving surface on stationary surface" or vice versa as defined by the apparatus manual.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- [x] **Angle of Repose (θ):** Minimum inclination at which the block just begins to slide under its own weight.
- [x] **Master Equation:** μs = tan θ (independent of mass).
- [x] **Normal Reaction:** N = W cos θ.
- [x] **Tangential Component:** F_t = W sin θ (driving force).
- [x] **Coulomb's Law:** F = μ N.
- [x] **Limiting Equilibrium:** W sin θ = μ W cos θ.
- [x] **Three Test Pairs:** Steel–Steel (low μ), Steel–Wood (medium μ), Steel–Asbestos (high μ).
- [x] **Procedure:** Tilt slowly → note angle of sliding → take mean of 5 trials → compute μ.
- [x] **μ is dimensionless** (ratio of two forces).
- [x] **Apparatus Components:** Base, tilting platform, protractor arc (0°–90°), sliding block with detachable faces, locking screw, scale.
- [x] **Applications:** Brake design, conveyor belts, chute design, friction dampers, bearing analysis, hydraulic machine packings.
- [x] **Limiting Friction Force:** F_max = μs × W cos θ (at the verge of sliding).
- [x] **Precautions:** Clean surfaces, take mean readings, avoid parallax, ensure plane starts horizontal, use spirit level.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Memory Aid:** *"Tilt till it slips, take the angle, press tan — that's your μ."* This three-step rhyme captures the entire essence of the experiment for viva voce preparation.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Non-Destructive Tests: Ultrasonic Flaw Detection, Magnetic Crack Detection, and Dye Penetration Tests
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUL407 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Non-Destructive Tests: Ultrasonic Flaw Detection, Magnetic Crack Detection, and Dye Penetration Tests

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Non-Destructive Testing (NDT) — The Master Concept

**Non-Destructive Testing (NDT)** is a branch of engineering science that deals with the inspection, testing, and evaluation of materials, components, and assemblies for discontinuities, defects, or variations in their internal and surface structure **without impairing their future usefulness or serviceability**. The part being inspected remains serviceable after the test — this is the cardinal rule that distinguishes NDT from destructive testing (like tensile, impact, or hardness tests you may have performed in earlier labs).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):** *Non-Destructive Testing is the process of evaluating the properties of a material, component, or system without causing damage to it. The three methods emphasized in this module are Ultrasonic Testing (UT), Magnetic Particle Testing (MPT), and Liquid Penetrant Testing (LPT).*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Medical Check-Up"

Imagine a doctor examining a patient. The doctor uses:
- **Ultrasound scans** → to "see" cracks *inside* the bone → that's our **Ultrasonic Flaw Detection**.
- **MRI / Magnetic resonance** → to detect hairline fractures using magnetism → that's our **Magnetic Crack Detection**.
- **Dye injection** (angiography) → to highlight surface wounds/blockages using a colored liquid → that's our **Dye Penetration Test**.

Just like a doctor never cuts a patient open for routine diagnosis, an NDT engineer never cuts a component to find flaws. The component continues to serve its purpose in service (pipes, pressure vessels, turbine blades, welds, etc.).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is NDT critical in Fluid Mechanics and Hydraulic Machines?**
> Hydraulic pipelines, pump impellers, pressure vessel shells, and turbine runners operate under **high cyclic stress, cavitation, and corrosive environments**. A small crack can grow into a catastrophic failure causing loss of life and property. NDT is the *insurance policy* of mechanical systems.

## 1.3 The Three Methods at a Glance

| Method | Working Principle | Detects | Material Limitation | Surface/Sub-surface |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Ultrasonic Testing (UT)** | High-frequency sound waves (1–25 MHz) | Internal flaws, voids, delaminations, thickness | Most metals; some plastics | Both |
| **Magnetic Particle Testing (MPT)** | Magnetic flux leakage at defect sites | Surface & near-surface cracks | **Ferromagnetic only** (iron, steel, nickel, cobalt) | Surface & ~6 mm depth |
| **Liquid Penetrant Testing (LPT/DPT)** | Capillary action of dye into surface openings | Surface-breaking cracks, porosity, laps | **Non-porous only** (metals, ceramics, glass) | Surface only |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick for Exams:** *"US-Mag-Dye"* — **U**ltrasound looks **U**nder the surface; **Mag**netic field **Mag**nifies **M**aterial **F**laws; **Dye** **D**raws itself into **D**efects.

## 1.4 Standards Governing NDT

Per KTU's outcome-based education (OBE) framework, students are expected to be aware of these codes:
- **ASTM E 114** — Ultrasonic contact testing
- **ASTM E 709** — Magnetic particle testing
- **ASTM E 165** — Liquid penetrant testing
- **ASME Section V** — Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code (NDE chapter)
- **IS 3658** — Indian Standard for NDT (relevant to Indian industry)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Ultrasonic Flaw Detection (UT)

### 2.1.1 Working Principle — The "Bouncing Bat" Logic

A piezoelectric transducer generates high-frequency sound waves (typically **1 MHz to 25 MHz**, well above human hearing). These waves travel through the material. When they encounter a **boundary** (back wall or defect), they reflect back. The time taken for the echo to return, combined with the known sound velocity, gives the **depth and size** of the defect.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Three Operating Modes of UT:**
> 1. **Pulse-Echo Mode** — Single probe sends and receives. Most common.
> 2. **Through-Transmission Mode** — Two probes on opposite sides. The defect casts a "shadow."
> 3. **Pitch-Catch Mode** — Two probes at an angle. Used for special geometries.

### 2.1.2 The "Why" Behind Ultrasonic Generation — Piezoelectric Effect

Jacques and Pierre Curie discovered in 1880 that certain crystals (e.g., **Quartz, Lead Zirconate Titanate (PZT)**) generate an electric charge when mechanically stressed (and vice versa). A high-voltage pulse applied to the crystal causes it to vibrate at its **resonant frequency**, producing ultrasound. The same crystal in reverse acts as the receiver.

### 2.1.3 Sound Velocity in Solids — Why It Matters

The longitudinal wave velocity in a thin, infinite solid bar is given by:

$$v_L = \sqrt{\frac{E}{\rho}}$$

For an **infinite solid** (which approximates a thick component), the velocity is:

$$v_L = \sqrt{\frac{E(1-\nu)}{\rho(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)}}$$

where:
- $E$ = Young's Modulus (Pa or N/m²)
- $\rho$ = Density (kg/m³)
- $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio (dimensionless, typically 0.28–0.33 for metals)

The **shear (transverse) wave velocity** is:

$$v_S = \sqrt{\frac{G}{\rho}} = \sqrt{\frac{E}{2\rho(1+\nu)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** Steel has $E \approx 200$ GPa and $\rho \approx 7850$ kg/m³. Substituting gives $v_L \approx 5920$ m/s and $v_S \approx 3255$ m/s. These are the numbers you must remember — they appear in **every** UT problem on board exams.

### 2.1.4 Attenuation and dB Scale

Sound waves lose energy as they travel. The **loss in amplitude (in decibels)** is:

$$\text{Attenuation (dB)} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{A_{\text{initial}}}{A_{\text{final}}}\right)$$

Attenuation occurs due to:
- **Beam spreading** (geometric)
- **Absorption** (conversion to heat)
- **Scattering** (at grain boundaries)

### 2.1.5 Snell's Law for Oblique Probes

When the sound wave hits a metal surface at an angle, it refracts. The **first critical angle** beyond which only shear waves exist is:

$$\sin\theta_c = \frac{v_1}{v_2}$$

where $v_1$ is the velocity in the wedge (typically Perspex) and $v_2$ is the longitudinal velocity in the test piece.

## 2.2 Magnetic Particle Testing (MPT)

### 2.2.1 The Physics — "Hidden Field Lines"

When a ferromagnetic material is magnetized, magnetic flux lines travel **inside** the material. At a **crack or discontinuity**, the flux lines cannot pass through the air gap (air has very low permeability). They **bulge out** of the surface, creating a **leakage field**. Iron particles sprinkled on the surface get attracted to this leakage field and pile up at the defect, making it visible.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The River Analogy:** Imagine a river flowing smoothly. Now place a **boulder** (the crack) in the path. Water (flux) flows around the boulder, creating a disturbance on the surface — that's exactly the leakage field.

### 2.2.2 The Magnetization Methods

| Method | Application |
|---|---|
| **Longitudinal magnetization** | Detects **transverse** defects (current flows along the part) |
| **Circular magnetization** | Detects **longitudinal** defects (current passes through the part) |
| **Yoke method (Permanent magnet / Electromagnet)** | Portable, field use, detects defects perpendicular to magnetic field |

**Best Practice:** Always magnetize in **two perpendicular directions** to detect all defect orientations.

### 2.2.3 Key Magnetic Quantities

- **Magnetic Field Strength (H):** $H = \dfrac{NI}{l}$ (A/m)
- **Magnetic Flux Density (B):** $B = \mu H = \mu_0 \mu_r H$ (Tesla)
- **Relative Permeability ($\mu_r$):** Steel ≈ 100–5000; Air ≈ 1
- **Permeability of free space:** $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ **H/m**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does MPT only work on ferromagnetic materials?** The leakage field requires a HUGE difference in permeability between the parent material (~1000) and the defect (air, ~1). For non-magnetic materials (aluminum, copper, austenitic stainless steel), this difference does not exist, so no leakage field is generated.

### 2.2.4 Demagnetization — A Mandatory Step

After testing, the component **must be demagnetized** to prevent:
- Attraction of iron filings in service
- Difficulty in subsequent machining
- Arc blow during welding

Demagnetization is achieved by passing a decaying AC current or placing in a decaying AC field.

## 2.3 Dye Penetration Testing (DPT/LPT)

### 2.3.1 The Physics — Capillary Action

A liquid penetrant (often dyed red or fluorescent) is applied to the cleaned surface. Due to **capillary action**, the penetrant is **drawn into surface-breaking cracks** by surface tension. After excess penetrant is removed, a developer (white chalky powder) is applied. The developer acts as a **blotter**, drawing the trapped penetrant back out, spreading it, and revealing the defect as a clearly visible indication.

### 2.3.2 The Six Mandatory Steps

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Exam Favorite — "PREP-DEP" Mnemonic:**
> 1. **P**re-clean the surface
> 2. **R**emove contaminants (degreaser, solvent)
> 3. **E**xamine for surface condition
> 4. **P**enetrate (apply penetrant, dwell time 5–60 min)
> 5. **D**evelop (apply developer, dwell time 10–30 min)
> 6. **P**ost-clean and interpret

### 2.3.3 Jurin's Law — The Capillary Equation

The height to which a liquid rises in a capillary tube (or the depth to which it penetrates a narrow crack) is:

$$h = \frac{2\gamma \cos\theta}{\rho g r}$$

where:
- $\gamma$ = Surface tension of the penetrant (N/m)
- $\theta$ = Contact angle between penetrant and the surface (degrees)
- $\rho$ = Density of penetrant (kg/m³)
- $g$ = Acceleration due to gravity = **9.81 m/s²**
- $r$ = Effective radius of the crack opening (m)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Designer Logic:** A good penetrant has **low contact angle** ($\theta \to 0°$, so $\cos\theta \to 1$), **low viscosity**, and **high surface tension**. This is why penetrants contain surfactants.

### 2.3.4 Types of Penetrants and Developers

| Type | Color | Best For | Sensitivity |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Visible Dye Penetrant** | Red | Field inspection, good lighting | Low–Medium |
| **Fluorescent Penetrant** | Glows under UV (365 nm) | Critical parts, dark conditions | High |
| **Water-Washable** | — | Easy removal | Medium |
| **Post-Emulsifiable** | — | Highest sensitivity | Highest |

| Developer Type | Form | Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Dry powder** | Powder | Smooth surfaces |
| **Wet (solvent-based)** | Suspension | Field use |
| **Wet (water-based)** | Suspension | Workshop use |

### 2.3.5 Indication Interpretation

| Indication Appearance | Probable Defect |
|---|---|
| Linear, sharp | Crack |
| Round, smooth | Porosity |
| Cluster of dots | Pitting / corrosion |
| Continuous line along weld | Lack of fusion / undercut |

## 2.4 Master Formula Sheet (High-Yield for KTU 2024)

> [!NOTE]
> Use $\vert$ notation for absolute values — vertical pipes are forbidden inside the markdown table per KTU formatting rules.

| # | Topic | Formula | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Longitudinal wave velocity (thin rod) | $v_L = \sqrt{E/\rho}$ | $E$ in Pa, $\rho$ in kg/m³, $v$ in m/s |
| 2 | Longitudinal wave velocity (bulk) | $v_L = \sqrt{E(1-\nu) \;/ \; \rho(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)}$ | $\nu$ dimensionless |
| 3 | Shear wave velocity | $v_S = \sqrt{E \;/\; 2\rho(1+\nu)}$ | Same as above |
| 4 | Acoustic Impedance | $Z = \rho v$ | $Z$ in Rayl (kg/m²·s) |
| 5 | Reflection coefficient (pressure) | $R = (Z_2 - Z_1)^2 / (Z_2 + Z_1)^2$ | $Z_1, Z_2$ acoustic impedances |
| 6 | Snell's Law | $\sin\theta_1 / v_1 = \sin\theta_2 / v_2$ | Angles in radians |
| 7 | Attenuation in dB | $\alpha = 20\log_{10}(A_0 / A_x)$ | $A$ amplitude |
| 8 | Magnetic field strength (solenoid) | $H = N I / l$ | $N$ turns, $I$ in A, $l$ in m |
| 9 | Magnetic flux density | $B = \mu_0 \mu_r H$ | $B$ in Tesla |
| 10 | Capillary rise (Jurin's law) | $h = 2\gamma\cos\theta / \rho g r$ | $\gamma$ in N/m, $r$ in m |
| 11 | Sensitivity formula (UT, ASTM) | $\text{Sensitivity} = (B_2 - B_1)/B_{\text{back wall}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 12 | Time of flight in UT | $t = 2d / v$ | $d$ depth, $v$ velocity |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Tip — Always quote ONE industry example:**

- **UT:** Periodic in-service inspection of **aircraft wings**, **railway axles**, **boiler tubes**, and **reactor pressure vessels** in nuclear plants.
- **MPT:** Inspection of **welded joints** in shipbuilding, **forgings**, and **castings** in the heavy engineering industry.
- **DPT:** Inspection of **turbine blades**, **valve castings**, **aluminum die-castings**, and **non-magnetic stainless steel welds** (where MPT cannot be used).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Longitudinal Wave Velocity in an Infinite Solid

We start from **Newton's Second Law** applied to a small element of a solid bar of cross-sectional area $A$ and density $\rho$.

### Step 1: Set up the equation of motion

Consider a thin element of length $\delta x$. The stress on the right face is $\sigma + \frac{\partial \sigma}{\partial x} \delta x$ and on the left face is $\sigma$. The net force on the element is:

$$F_{\text{net}} = A \left[ \left(\sigma + \frac{\partial \sigma}{\partial x}\delta x \right) - \sigma \right] = A \frac{\partial \sigma}{\partial x} \delta x$$

### Step 2: Apply Newton's Second Law

The mass of the element is $m = \rho A \delta x$. Acceleration is $\dfrac{\partial^2 u}{\partial t^2}$ where $u$ is displacement.

$$F_{\text{net}} = m \cdot a = \rho A \delta x \cdot \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial t^2}$$

### Step 3: Equate and simplify

$$A \frac{\partial \sigma}{\partial x}\delta x = \rho A \delta x \cdot \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial t^2}$$

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial \sigma}{\partial x} = \rho \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial t^2}}$$

### Step 4: Apply Hooke's Law

From 1D elasticity, $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon = E \cdot \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x}$ for a thin rod.

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(E \frac{\partial u}{\partial x}\right) = \rho \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial t^2}$$

For a uniform material, $E$ is constant:

$$E \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2} = \rho \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial t^2}$$

### Step 5: Rearrange into the standard wave equation form

$$\frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial t^2} = \frac{E}{\rho} \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2}$$

Comparing with the standard 1D wave equation $\dfrac{\partial^2 u}{\partial t^2} = v^2 \dfrac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2}$, we identify:

$$\boxed{v_L = \sqrt{\frac{E}{\rho}}}$$

This is the **thin-rod longitudinal velocity** (valid when the lateral dimensions are much smaller than the wavelength).

### Step 6: Extension to a 3D infinite medium

For a 3D solid, the lateral contraction is constrained. Using generalized Hooke's Law, the relevant modulus is the **constrained modulus** $M$:

$$M = \frac{E(1-\nu)}{(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{v_L = \sqrt{\frac{E(1-\nu)}{\rho(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)}}}$$

**Numerical verification for mild steel:**
- $E = 200 \times 10^9$ Pa
- $\nu = 0.30$
- $\rho = 7850$ kg/m³

$$v_L = \sqrt{\frac{200 \times 10^9 \times (1-0.30)}{7850 \times (1+0.30)(1-0.60)}}$$

$$v_L = \sqrt{\frac{200 \times 10^9 \times 0.70}{7850 \times 1.30 \times 0.40}}$$

$$v_L = \sqrt{\frac{1.40 \times 10^{11}}{4082}} = \sqrt{3.43 \times 10^7}$$

$$\boxed{v_L \approx 5857 \text{ m/s} \approx 5920 \text{ m/s (literature value)}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Time of Flight in Pulse-Echo Ultrasonic Testing

### Step 1: Geometry of the test

The probe is placed on the top surface. A sound pulse travels **down**, hits the **back wall** (or defect), and **reflects back up**. Total distance traveled is **2d** (down and up).

### Step 2: Apply distance-velocity-time relation

$$\text{Total distance} = v \times t_{\text{total}}$$

$$2d = v \cdot t_{\text{total}}$$

### Step 3: Solve for depth

$$\boxed{d = \frac{v \cdot t_{\text{total}}}{2}}$$

**Worked Example (Very Important for KTU):**

> A steel plate of thickness 50 mm is inspected using a 4 MHz probe. The back-wall echo is observed at $t = 17.0$ μs. A defect echo appears at $t = 6.0$ μs. Find:
> (a) The longitudinal wave velocity in steel.
> (b) The depth of the defect.

**Solution:**

**(a) Velocity from back wall:**

$$v = \frac{2d}{t_{\text{BW}}} = \frac{2 \times 50 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}}{17.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s}}$$

$$v = \frac{0.1}{17.0 \times 10^{-6}} = 5882.4 \text{ m/s} \approx 5920 \text{ m/s}$$

This is consistent with the standard value for steel.

**(b) Defect depth:**

$$d_{\text{defect}} = \frac{v \cdot t_{\text{defect}}}{2} = \frac{5882.4 \times 6.0 \times 10^{-6}}{2}$$

$$d_{\text{defect}} = \frac{0.03529}{2} = 0.01765 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{d_{\text{defect}} \approx 17.65 \text{ mm from the top surface}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Acoustic Impedance and Reflection at an Interface

### Step 1: Define acoustic impedance

The acoustic impedance of a medium is the product of density and wave velocity:

$$Z = \rho \cdot v \quad [\text{units: Rayl} = \text{kg/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}]$$

| Material | $\rho$ (kg/m³) | $v_L$ (m/s) | $Z$ (MRayl) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Steel | 7850 | 5920 | 46.5 |
| Aluminum | 2700 | 6320 | 17.1 |
| Water | 1000 | 1480 | 1.48 |
| Air | 1.2 | 343 | 0.000411 |
| Perspex | 1180 | 2730 | 3.22 |

### Step 2: Boundary conditions at the interface

At the boundary between two media, both **pressure** and **particle velocity** must be continuous. Using the wave equations and applying these conditions yields the **reflection coefficient** (in terms of pressure amplitude):

$$R = \left(\frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}\right)^2$$

**Worked Example:**

> Ultrasound travels from **Perspex** (wedge) into **steel** (test piece). Calculate the percentage of energy reflected at the interface.

**Solution:**

$$R = \left(\frac{46.5 - 3.22}{46.5 + 3.22}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{43.28}{49.72}\right)^2$$

$$R = (0.8705)^2 = 0.7578$$

$$\boxed{R \approx 75.78\% \text{ of energy is reflected}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Implication:** This is why a **couplant** (oil, gel, or water) is MANDATORY between the probe and the test piece. Without it, you have a Perspex–Air–Steel interface. The Perspex–Air reflection is nearly 100% (because $Z_{\text{air}} \approx 0$), and NO energy enters the steel. The couplant excludes the air gap.

$$R_{\text{with air gap}} = \left(\frac{46.5 - 0.000411}{46.5 + 0.000411}\right)^2 \approx 1.0 = 100\%$$

This is why couplant is non-negotiable.

## 3.4 Derivation: Capillary Penetration Depth in a Surface Crack

### Step 1: Pressure balance in a capillary tube (modeling the crack)

The pressure difference across a curved liquid meniscus (Laplace pressure) is given by the **Young-Laplace equation**:

$$\Delta P = \frac{2\gamma}{r}$$

For a crack that is wider than a perfect cylinder, we model it as a slit. The Laplace pressure for a slit (rectangular cross-section) is:

$$\Delta P = \frac{2\gamma \cos\theta}{w}$$

where $w$ is the **width of the slit opening** and $\theta$ is the contact angle.

### Step 2: Equate to hydrostatic pressure of the liquid column

The liquid is drawn up the slit to a height $h$ where the hydrostatic pressure balances the Laplace pressure:

$$\Delta P = \rho g h$$

### Step 3: Solve for $h$

$$\rho g h = \frac{2\gamma \cos\theta}{w}$$

$$\boxed{h = \frac{2\gamma \cos\theta}{\rho g w}}$$

**Numerical Example:**

> A penetrant has surface tension $\gamma = 0.025$ N/m, density $\rho = 870$ kg/m³, and contact angle $\theta = 0°$ on a steel surface. Find the depth of penetration into a crack of width $w = 10$ μm.

**Solution:**

$$h = \frac{2 \times 0.025 \times \cos(0°)}{870 \times 9.81 \times 10 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$h = \frac{0.05}{0.08535} = 0.586 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{h \approx 586 \text{ mm}}$$

This explains why even a **micron-wide crack** can be filled by capillary action over time — hence the **dwell time** in DPT is critical.

## 3.5 Derivation: Magnetic Field of a Current-Carrying Conductor (Circular Magnetization)

### Step 1: Ampere's Circuital Law

For a long, straight conductor carrying current $I$, applying Ampere's law on a circular path of radius $r$ concentric with the wire:

$$\oint \vec{H} \cdot d\vec{l} = I_{\text{enclosed}}$$

$$H \cdot (2\pi r) = I$$

### Step 2: Solve for H

$$\boxed{H = \frac{I}{2\pi r}}$$

### Step 3: Compute B in the surrounding steel

For a steel component placed around the conductor, $B = \mu_0 \mu_r H$:

$$B = \mu_0 \mu_r \cdot \frac{I}{2\pi r}$$

**Example:** If $I = 1000$ A, $r = 25$ mm, and steel $\mu_r = 500$:

$$B = (4\pi \times 10^{-7}) \times 500 \times \frac{1000}{2\pi \times 0.025}$$

$$B = 2 \times 10^{-4} \times \frac{1000}{0.157} = 2 \times 10^{-4} \times 6366$$

$$\boxed{B \approx 1.27 \text{ Tesla}}$$

This is a strong field, sufficient for MPT (typically $B \geq 0.8$ T is required).

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is the **single most important summary** for the KTU viva. Examiners routinely ask: *"Compare the three methods."*

| Parameter | Ultrasonic (UT) | Magnetic (MPT) | Dye Penetrant (DPT) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Principle** | Acoustic waves | Magnetic flux leakage | Capillary action |
| **Detects** | Internal & surface | Surface & near-surface | Surface only |
| **Depth of detection** | Up to several meters | ~6 mm | Surface-breaking only |
| **Material** | All (conductive/non-conductive) | Ferromagnetic only | Non-porous only |
| **Surface prep** | Smooth, couplant needed | Clean, paint-free | Thorough cleaning critical |
| **Sensitivity to small defects** | Excellent (mm-level) | Good (μm-level) | Excellent (μm-level) |
| **Operator skill** | High | Medium | Low–Medium |
| **Cost of equipment** | High (₹2–15 lakh) | Low (₹10–50k) | Very low (₹2–10k) |
| **Speed** | Fast | Fast | Slow (long dwell times) |
| **Records obtained** | Permanent (A-scan) | Visual (must photograph) | Visual (must photograph) |
| **Hazard** | None (crystal vibrates) | Arc flash risk (high current) | Chemical exposure (solvents) |
| **Standards** | ASTM E114, E317 | ASTM E709, E1444 | ASTM E165, E1417 |
| **Best for** | Welds, thick sections | Welds, castings, forgings | Castings, weld surfaces, all metals |

## 3.7 KTU Numerical Pattern — Practice Calibration

A typical KTU 2024 numerical might state:

> *"In a UT test, the back wall echo is at 30 μs. A defect echo is at 9 μs. If the velocity of sound in steel is 5900 m/s, find the defect depth."*

**Model solution (always show all steps):**

$$d_{\text{defect}} = \frac{v \cdot t_{\text{defect}}}{2} = \frac{5900 \times 9 \times 10^{-6}}{2}$$

$$= \frac{0.0531}{2} = 0.02655 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{d_{\text{defect}} = 26.55 \text{ mm}}$$

**Bonus cross-check (using back wall):**

$$d_{\text{BW}} = \frac{5900 \times 30 \times 10^{-6}}{2} = 88.5 \text{ mm}$$

So the defect is at $26.55/88.5 = 30\%$ of the plate thickness from the top.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Process Flow — NDT Method Selection

This diagram shows the **decision tree** an inspector uses to select the correct NDT method based on material and defect type.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Component to be Inspected"] --> B{"Material Type?"}
    B -- "Ferromagnetic (Steel, Iron)" --> C{"Defect Location?"}
    B -- "Non-Magnetic (Al, SS, Cu, Plastic)" --> D{"Defect Location?"}
    B -- "Porous (Wood, Concrete)" --> Z["Reject - NDT not applicable"]
    
    C -- "Surface / Near-Surface" --> E["Use MPT - Magnetic Particle Test"]
    C -- "Internal / Subsurface" --> F["Use UT - Ultrasonic Test"]
    
    D -- "Surface-breaking only" --> G["Use DPT - Dye Penetrant Test"]
    D -- "Internal" --> H["Use UT - Ultrasonic Test"]
    D -- "Through-thickness" --> I["Use Radiography - Not in this module"]
    
    E --> J["Apply Iron Particles, Magnetize, Inspect"]
    F --> K["Apply Couplant, Scan with Probe, Read A-Scan"]
    G --> L["Clean, Apply Penetrant, Develop, Inspect"]
```

## 4.2 Ultrasonic Testing — Pulse-Echo Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Pulser / Receiver Unit"] -- "High voltage pulse" --> B["Piezoelectric Transducer"]
    B -- "Ultrasonic waves via couplant" --> C["Test Specimen"]
    C -- "Reflects from defect" --> B
    B -- "Electrical echo signal" --> A
    A -- "Processed signal" --> D["Display - A-Scan on CRO"]
    D -- "Operator interpretation" --> E["Defect Located and Sized"]
```

## 4.3 UT A-Scan Display — Visual Pattern

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A_Scan
    direction LR
    X["Initial Pulse"] --> Y["Defect Echo at depth d1"] --> Z["Back Wall Echo at depth d2"]
    end
```

**Reading the A-Scan:**
- The **first peak** (leftmost) is the **initial pulse** — the moment the probe fires.
- The **middle peak** is the **defect echo** — its horizontal position $\propto$ depth.
- The **last peak** is the **back wall echo** — gives total thickness.
- The **amplitude** of the defect echo $\propto$ defect size.

## 4.4 Magnetic Particle Testing — Equipment Setup

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["AC / DC Magnetizer Unit"] -- "High current via cables" --> B["Heavy Copper Cables / Heads"]
    B -- "Clamp on specimen" --> C["Specimen - Ferromagnetic"]
    C -- "Magnetized" --> D["Flux lines run through specimen"]
    D -- "Crack causes leakage" --> E["Iron particles sprayed"]
    E -- "Particles cluster at defect" --> F["Visible Indication under UV or light"]
```

## 4.5 Dye Penetrant Testing — Six-Step Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Step 1: Pre-Clean Surface - Remove oil, dirt, paint"] --> S2["Step 2: Apply Penetrant - Spray, brush, or dip"]
    S2 --> S3["Step 3: Dwell Time - Allow 5 to 60 min for capillary action"]
    S3 --> S4["Step 4: Remove Excess Penetrant - Wipe or water rinse"]
    S4 --> S5["Step 5: Apply Developer - Powder pulls penetrant back out"]
    S5 --> S6["Step 6: Inspect and Interpret - Look for indications"]
    S6 --> S7["Step 7: Post-Clean - Remove developer, clean part"]
```

## 4.6 Magnetic Field Around a Crack — Leakage Field Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Crack in Steel - Air Gap"] -- "Flux lines bulge out" --> B["Leakage Field above surface"]
    B -- "Attracts iron particles" --> C["Iron particle accumulation"]
    C -- "Forms visible indication" --> D["Inspector sees defect"]
```

## 4.7 Couplant Function in UT — The "Acoustic Bridge"

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Probe - High Z, Perspex or PZT"] -- "Z1" --> B["Couplant - Low Z, Oil or Gel"]
    B -- "Z2" --> C["Steel Specimen - Very High Z"]
    A -.- D["Without couplant: Air gap - Reflection ~100 percent"]
    B -.- E["With couplant: Continuous impedance path - Energy enters steel"]
```

## 4.8 Comparative Equipment Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph UT
    U1["Pulser/Receiver"] --> U2["Probe"] --> U3["Couplant Layer"] --> U4["Specimen"]
    end
    subgraph MPT
    M1["Magnetizer Current Source"] --> M2["Cables and Yoke"] --> M3["Specimen with crack"] --> M4["Iron particles on surface"]
    end
    subgraph DPT
    P1["Penetrant Can"] --> P2["Cleaned Specimen"] --> P3["Developer Powder"] --> P4["Indication visible"]
    end
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> These are direct definition/conceptual questions, typical of KTU module tests and viva voce.

### Q1. Define Non-Destructive Testing. List any four commonly used NDT methods. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**

**Model Answer (3 marks — write in 3 to 4 lines each):**

**Definition:** Non-Destructive Testing (NDT) is a group of analysis techniques used in industry to evaluate the properties of a material, component, or system without causing damage to it. The part remains fit for use after the test.

**Four NDT methods:**
1. **Ultrasonic Testing (UT)** — Uses high-frequency sound waves.
2. **Magnetic Particle Testing (MPT)** — Uses magnetic flux and iron particles.
3. **Liquid Penetrant Testing (LPT/DPT)** — Uses capillary action of dye.
4. **Radiographic Testing (RT)** — Uses X-rays or gamma rays.

*(Other valid methods: Eddy Current Testing, Acoustic Emission, Visual Testing, Leak Testing.)*

---

### Q2. Why is a couplant used in ultrasonic testing? What are the common couplants used? **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**

**Model Answer:**

A couplant is used to **exclude the air gap** between the probe and the test piece. Without it, nearly 100% of the ultrasonic energy is reflected at the probe–air interface due to the massive acoustic impedance mismatch ($Z_{\text{air}} \approx 0$ vs $Z_{\text{steel}} \approx 46.5$ MRayl). The couplant has acoustic impedance closer to the test piece, allowing sound waves to transmit into the specimen.

**Common couplants:** Water, **SAE 30 motor oil**, glycerin, commercial ultrasonic gel, and petroleum jelly. For high-temperature applications, special high-temperature greases or molten metals are used.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 Scheme, every Part-B question carries 14 marks and offers internal choice. You write EITHER OR between two alternatives.

### QUESTION A — 14 Marks (Module Choice Set 1)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Pattern]**

**Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of **Pulse-Echo Ultrasonic Testing**. Derive the expression for defect depth in terms of wave velocity and time of flight. **[7 marks]**

**Q. (b)** A 60 mm thick steel plate is inspected by ultrasonic pulse-echo testing using a 4 MHz probe. The back-wall echo is observed at 20.3 μs and a defect echo at 8.0 μs. Determine: (i) the longitudinal wave velocity in the plate, and (ii) the depth of the defect from the top surface. Take the velocity of sound in steel as 5920 m/s for verification. **[7 marks]**

#### MODEL ANSWER — Q(a)

**Working Principle (Block Diagram):**

```
Pulser/Receiver → Probe (Piezoelectric) → Specimen (with couplant)
        ↑                                     ↓
        └── Echo signal returns ← ← ← ← ← Reflection
        ↓
   Display (A-Scan on CRO)
```

**Step-by-step working:**

1. The **pulser-receiver** generates a high-voltage electrical pulse (typically 100–400 V, nanosecond duration).
2. The pulse is applied to a **piezoelectric crystal** (PZT or quartz), which converts it into mechanical vibrations at its resonant frequency (here 4 MHz).
3. The vibrations pass through a **couplant** (oil/gel) into the test specimen, since direct contact would have air gaps that reflect nearly 100% of energy.
4. The sound waves travel through the specimen. When they encounter a **boundary** (back wall or defect) with different acoustic impedance, a portion of the energy is **reflected back**.
5. The reflected wave returns to the same crystal (acting as a receiver now), which converts it back to an electrical signal.
6. The receiver amplifies and sends this to the **display** (A-scan on a CRO or digital screen).
7. The **horizontal position** of the echo peak indicates the **time of flight**, and thus the **depth of the reflector**. The **height** of the peak indicates the **size** of the reflector.

**Derivation of Defect Depth:**

Let $d$ = depth of defect, $v$ = velocity of sound in the specimen, $t_d$ = time of flight for the defect echo.

The wave travels down a distance $d$ and back up a distance $d$, so total distance is $2d$.

$$2d = v \cdot t_d$$

$$\boxed{d = \frac{v \cdot t_d}{2}}$$

**Equation for back-wall verification:**

For back wall at depth $D$ with echo time $t_{BW}$:

$$D = \frac{v \cdot t_{BW}}{2}$$

#### MODEL ANSWER — Q(b)

**Given:**
- Plate thickness, $D = 60$ mm $= 0.060$ m
- $t_{BW} = 20.3$ μs $= 20.3 \times 10^{-6}$ s
- $t_d = 8.0$ μs $= 8.0 \times 10^{-6}$ s
- Standard velocity, $v = 5920$ m/s

**(i) Longitudinal wave velocity from back-wall echo:**

$$v = \frac{2D}{t_{BW}} = \frac{2 \times 0.060}{20.3 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$v = \frac{0.120}{20.3 \times 10^{-6}} = 5911.3 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\boxed{v \approx 5911 \text{ m/s}}$$

This matches the standard value of **5920 m/s** for mild steel — confirming calibration.

**(ii) Defect depth:**

$$d = \frac{v \cdot t_d}{2} = \frac{5911.3 \times 8.0 \times 10^{-6}}{2}$$

$$d = \frac{0.04729}{2} = 0.02364 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{d \approx 23.65 \text{ mm from the top surface}}$$

The defect lies at about 23.65/60 = **39.4%** of the plate thickness from the top, well within the 60 mm plate.

---

### QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Module Choice Set 1 — Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Pattern]**

**Q. (a)** Explain the working principle of **Magnetic Particle Testing** with a neat sketch. Discuss why it is limited to ferromagnetic materials. List the steps to demagnetize a component after testing. **[7 marks]**

**Q. (b)** Explain the **Dye Penetrant Testing** procedure step-by-step. A penetrant has surface tension 0.022 N/m, density 850 kg/m³, and zero contact angle on steel. Calculate the depth of penetration into a surface crack of width 5 μm. **[7 marks]**

#### MODEL ANSWER — Q(a)

**Working Principle:**

When a ferromagnetic specimen is magnetized (using a yoke, coils, or direct current), magnetic flux lines flow through the material. Where there is a **crack or discontinuity** (essentially an air gap), the flux lines **cannot pass through** and must bulge out of the surface, creating a **leakage field**. When fine iron particles (dry or suspended in liquid) are sprinkled on the surface, they are attracted to the leakage field and accumulate at the defect, making it visible as a dark line or cluster.

**Sketch:**

```
   Magnetic Flux Lines (in steel)
   ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━╲
   ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━╲╲  ← Leakage field
   ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━╲╲┐  above crack
       Surface ●●●●●●●●●●●●  ← Iron particles cluster
        ═══════Crack════════
   ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
   ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
```

**Limitation to Ferromagnetic Materials:**

The method depends on a **huge contrast in relative permeability ($\mu_r$)** between the parent material and the defect:
- Steel: $\mu_r \approx 100$ to $5000$
- Air (in crack): $\mu_r \approx 1$

This contrast (factor of 100–5000) creates a strong leakage field. In non-magnetic materials like aluminum, copper, or austenitic stainless steel, the parent material itself has $\mu_r \approx 1$ — same as air. So no leakage field forms, and the method fails completely.

**Demagnetization Procedure (Mandatory Post-Test):**

1. Pass the component through a **decaying AC magnetic field** (commercial demagnetizer).
2. The field starts above the saturation level and gradually reduces to zero in alternating direction.
3. Alternative: Heat the part above its **Curie temperature** (not practical for most components).
4. Verify demagnetization using a **gaussmeter** or by checking attraction to a steel needle.

> [!WARNING]
> **Failure to demagnetize** can cause iron-filing accumulation in service, arc blow in subsequent welding, and erroneous readings in instruments.

#### MODEL ANSWER — Q(b)

**Six-Step Procedure of DPT (write as bullet points):**

1. **Pre-cleaning:** Remove all oil, grease, paint, rust, and dirt using solvent, alkaline cleaner, or vapor degreaser.
2. **Penetrant application:** Apply the penetrant (red dye or fluorescent) by spraying, brushing, or dipping. Ensure full coverage of the test area.
3. **Dwell time:** Allow 5 to 60 minutes (typically 10–30 min) for the penetrant to be drawn into surface cracks by capillary action.
4. **Excess penetrant removal:** Remove the surface penetrant using a clean cloth or water spray. **Do not** over-wash, as this can remove penetrant from inside the defects.
5. **Developer application:** Apply a thin, even layer of developer (white powder). It acts as a blotter, drawing the trapped penetrant out of the defect and spreading it as a visible indication.
6. **Inspection and interpretation:** Inspect under appropriate lighting (white light for visible dye, UV-A 365 nm for fluorescent). Record the indications and interpret their type.

**Calculation of Penetration Depth:**

**Given:**
- Surface tension, $\gamma = 0.022$ N/m
- Density, $\rho = 850$ kg/m³
- Contact angle, $\theta = 0°$ (so $\cos\theta = 1$)
- Crack width, $w = 5 \text{ μm} = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ m
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Formula (modified Jurin's law for a slit):**

$$h = \frac{2\gamma \cos\theta}{\rho g w}$$

**Substitute:**

$$h = \frac{2 \times 0.022 \times 1}{850 \times 9.81 \times 5 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$h = \frac{0.044}{0.04169}$$

$$\boxed{h \approx 1.055 \text{ m} \approx 1055 \text{ mm}}$$

**Interpretation:** The capillary force is so strong that the penetrant can theoretically rise more than a meter into such a narrow crack — this is why even sub-micron cracks are detected by DPT. In practice, the depth is limited by the amount of penetrant available and the dwell time.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Read this section carefully — these are the top reasons students LOSE marks:**

### Pitfall 1: Forgetting to divide by 2 in Pulse-Echo
The wave travels **down AND up**. If you write $d = v \cdot t$ instead of $d = v \cdot t / 2$, you will be **wrong by a factor of 2**. This is the #1 error in UT numericals.

### Pitfall 2: Wrong unit conversion
$t = 8.0 \text{ μs} = 8.0 \times 10^{-6}$ s, **not** $8.0 \times 10^{-3}$ s. Many students lose 2–3 marks for unit errors.

### Pitfall 3: Confusing DPT with MPT
DPT works on **any non-porous material** (including non-magnetic). MPT works **only on ferromagnetic**. Mixing these up shows conceptual failure.

### Pitfall 4: Not mentioning couplant in UT
If a question asks for "precautions" or "procedure" in UT, **always mention couplant**. Examiners specifically look for this.

### Pitfall 5: Skipping demagnetization
If a question on MPT asks about "post-test procedure" or "disadvantages if not done properly," you MUST mention demagnetization.

### Pitfall 6: Capillary equation units
The capillary rise formula $h = 2\gamma\cos\theta/\rho g r$ — the symbol $r$ in the **circular** formula becomes $w$ (slit width) for a **crack**. Mixing the two is a frequent mistake.

### Pitfall 7: Block diagram
If the question says "with a neat sketch," you must draw a diagram. A description without a block diagram loses 2 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!TIP]
> **Print this section and read it just before entering the exam hall.**

- ☐ **NDT = testing without damaging the part.** Serviceability is preserved.
- ☐ **Three methods** in this module: **UT, MPT, DPT** (US-Mag-Dye).
- ☐ **UT principle:** Piezoelectric crystal generates ultrasound. Echoes from defects and back wall. Uses **couplant** to bridge air gap.
- ☐ **Velocity in steel:** $v_L \approx 5920$ m/s (longitudinal), $v_S \approx 3255$ m/s (shear).
- ☐ **Defect depth (Pulse-Echo):** $d = v \cdot t / 2$ — **never forget the /2**.
- ☐ **Snell's Law:** $\sin\theta_1/v_1 = \sin\theta_2/v_2$. Critical angle is when $\theta_2 = 90°$.
- ☐ **Acoustic impedance:** $Z = \rho v$. Reflection coefficient $R = (Z_2 - Z_1)^2/(Z_2 + Z_1)^2$.
- ☐ **Air gap reflection** is ~100% — couplant is **mandatory** in UT.
- ☐ **MPT works only on ferromagnetic materials** (steel, iron, Ni, Co) because of high $\mu_r$ contrast.
- ☐ **Magnetization methods:** Longitudinal (finds transverse defects), Circular (finds longitudinal defects), Yoke (portable).
- ☐ **Always magnetize in two perpendicular directions** to detect all defect orientations.
- ☐ **Demagnetization is mandatory** after MPT — use decaying AC field.
- ☐ **DPT works on any non-porous material** (metals, ceramics, glass, plastics).
- ☐ **Six steps of DPT:** PREP-DEP (Pre-clean, Remove, Examine, Penetrant, Develop, Post-clean).
- ☐ **Capillary rise (slit model):** $h = 2\gamma\cos\theta/(\rho g w)$.
- ☐ **Fluorescent penetrants** (under UV-A 365 nm) are more sensitive than visible red dyes.
- ☐ **UT = internal & volumetric; MPT = surface & near-surface; DPT = surface only.**
- ☐ **ASTM standards:** E114 (UT), E709 (MPT), E165 (DPT).
- ☐ **Real-world applications:** UT for boiler tubes, aircraft, nuclear vessels; MPT for welds, castings, forgings; DPT for turbine blades, valve castings, welds.
- ☐ **Always draw a block diagram** when a question says "with a neat sketch."
- ☐ **Always state units** in the final answer (mm, m/s, MRayl, Tesla, etc.).

---

> **END OF NOTES — MODULE EXPERIMENT 2 — NDT METHODS**
> *These notes are aligned with the KTU 2024 Scheme, NEP 2020 framework, and Revised Bloom's Taxonomy (Levels L1–L4: Remember, Understand, Apply, Analyze).* 
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measurement of Angle Using Sine Bar
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUL407 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Measurement of Angle Using Sine Bar

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Sine Bar** is a precision metrological instrument used in engineering metrology for the accurate measurement of angles of workpieces, or for the setting of workpieces at a predetermined angle with high accuracy. It operates on the fundamental trigonometric principle of the sine function, where a workpiece of unknown angle is supported on two cylindrical rollers of equal diameter, and the assembly is tilted until the top surface of the workpiece becomes perfectly parallel to a reference surface plate. The angle is then computed from the height of slip gauges placed under one roller and the known center distance between the two rollers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board-Examiner Definition**
> A sine bar is essentially a hardened, ground, and lapped steel bar with two precision cylinders (plugs) of equal diameter mounted at exactly known center distances (typically **100 mm**, **200 mm**, or **300 mm**), used to establish or measure an angle by the trigonometric relationship:
> $$\sin\theta = \frac{h}{L}$$
> where $\theta$ is the angle of inclination, $h$ is the height of the slip gauge stack, and $L$ is the effective length of the sine bar (center-to-center distance between the two rollers).

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are trying to measure the slope of a ramp. The simplest way would be to push a wedge under one end until the ramp is perfectly level. If you know the length of the ramp and the height of the wedge, you can calculate the angle of the ramp using basic trigonometry. **A sine bar works on exactly this principle!**

- The **ramp** = Workpiece with the unknown angle
- The **wedge** = Stack of slip gauges
- The **floor** = Precision surface plate
- The **trigonometry** = $\sin\theta = h / L$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Intuition**
> Think of a carpenter's inclinometer or a digital protractor on a smartphone. Both of these measure angles directly. However, when manufacturing high-precision moulds, dies, gauges, and cutting tools, the angle requirements are often within **± 1 minute (1/60th of a degree)** of accuracy. A standard protractor cannot achieve this. The sine bar, in conjunction with slip gauges and a dial indicator, achieves this level of accuracy, which is why it remains the gold standard in tool rooms worldwide.

### Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Bold Highlighted)

- **Standard Sine Bar Lengths (L):** **100 mm, 200 mm, 300 mm, and 500 mm**
- **Roller Diameter:** Typically **20 mm** (must be perfectly equal and parallel)
- **Reference Surface:** **Grade 0 or Grade 1 Surface Plate** (Cast Iron or Granite)
- **Slip Gauge Accuracy:** **Grade 0 or Grade 1** (as per IS 2984)
- **Measurement Accuracy:** Up to **± 1 minute** of arc
- **Formula Precision:** $\sin\theta = h / L$
- **Common Inspection Instrument:** **Dial Indicator (DTI)** with least count **0.01 mm** or **0.001 mm**

> [!TIP]
> **Why a Sine Bar and not a Vernier Bevel Protractor?**
> A Vernier Bevel Protractor has a least count of only **5 minutes (5')**, whereas a sine bar can measure angles to an accuracy of **1 minute (1')** or even better when slip gauges and a precision DTI are used. This 5× improvement in accuracy is critical in the production of jigs, fixtures, and precision gauges.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The sine bar exploits the geometric relationship of a right-angled triangle formed when one roller is raised by slip gauges above the surface plate.

1. **Step 1 — Setup the Reference Plane:** The sine bar is placed on a perfectly flat, clean **surface plate** (acting as the datum / hypotenuse reference).
2. **Step 2 — Establish the Inclination:** A precisely calculated stack of **slip gauges** of height $h$ is placed under one of the two rollers, lifting it above the surface plate.
3. **Step 3 — Place the Workpiece:** The workpiece is clamped to the sine bar using suitable clamping straps. Its surface, whose angle $\theta$ is to be measured, now rests against the raised roller and the lower roller.
4. **Step 4 — Check Parallelism with a DTI:** A **Dial Test Indicator (DTI)** is moved along the top surface of the workpiece. Slip gauges are added or removed until the DTI reads the same value at both ends — indicating the surface is **perfectly parallel to the surface plate**.
5. **Step 5 — Apply Trigonometry:** At the instant of perfect parallelism, the angle subtended by the sine bar with the surface plate equals the angle $\theta$ of the workpiece surface. From the right-angled triangle:
   - Hypotenuse = $L$ (length of sine bar)
   - Opposite side = $h$ (height of slip gauges)
   - Therefore: $\sin\theta = h / L$
6. **Step 6 — Solve for the Angle:** $\theta = \sin^{-1}(h / L)$

### The Core "Why" and "How"

- **Why two rollers of equal diameter?** Equal diameters ensure that the center distance $L$ between the roller centers remains constant regardless of how the sine bar is placed — this guarantees the geometric reliability of the measurement.
- **Why use slip gauges?** Slip gauges are the only length-measuring standards with sub-micron accuracy (up to **± 0.5 µm**). They define the height $h$ with absolute precision.
- **Why a DTI for alignment?** The DTI removes the last trace of error by mechanically verifying the parallelism condition. Without it, the eye cannot resolve deviations smaller than 5 minutes of arc.
- **How does the system remain traceable?** Every measurement is reducible to a primary standard of length (the slip gauge), making the system SI-traceable and acceptable for ISO 9001 quality audits.

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Units | Remarks |
|---|---------------------|-------------------------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Fundamental Sine Bar Formula | $\sin\theta = h / L$ | Dimensionless ratio | $h$ = slip gauge height, $L$ = sine bar length |
| 2 | Height of Slip Gauges | $h = L \cdot \sin\theta$ | mm | Used to *set* a known angle |
| 3 | Angle to be Measured | $\theta = \sin^{-1}(h / L)$ | Degrees (°) or Radians (rad) | Used to *measure* an unknown angle |
| 4 | Sine Bar Effective Length (L) | **100 / 200 / 300 / 500** | mm | Standard nominal center-to-center distances |
| 5 | Correction for Roller Diameter | $h_{corrected} = h_{calculated} \pm d \left(1 - \cos\theta\right)$ | mm | $d$ = roller diameter (applied when angle is measured from top of roller) |
| 6 | Convert Radians to Degrees | $\theta_{deg} = \theta_{rad} \times \frac{180}{\pi}$ | degrees | $\pi \approx 3.14159265$ |
| 7 | Convert Degrees to Radians | $\theta_{rad} = \theta_{deg} \times \frac{\pi}{180}$ | radians | Required for direct calculation |
| 8 | Sensitivity / Limiting Error | $\delta\theta_{min} = \frac{\delta h}{L \cdot \cos\theta}$ | Radians | Smallest detectable angular change for slip gauge resolution $\delta h$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility in Real-World Practice**
> Sine bars are routinely used in:
> - **Tool Rooms** for grinding cutters, drills, and reamers at precise angles
> - **Inspection Departments** for checking angle gauges and templates
> - **CNC Setup** for aligning fixtures at compound angles
> - **Quality Control** for acceptance testing of bevels on machined components

### Limiting Errors and Best Practices

- **Always use slip gauges wrung together** to minimize air-film errors (wringing reduces the effective height by a few micrometres; modern wrung stacks are calibrated for this).
- **Never use slip gauges from rusted or scratched sets** — any surface defect introduces height error.
- **The sine bar must be supported only on the rollers**, not on the bar body.
- **Keep the surface plate and rollers clean** — even a fingerprint on a roller can cause measurement deviation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation 1 — Fundamental Geometric Relationship

Consider the geometric configuration of a sine bar supporting a workpiece at an angle $\theta$ to the horizontal surface plate.

**Step 1 — Define the Geometry:**

Let the lower roller touch the surface plate at point A, and the upper roller (supported by a slip gauge stack of height $h$) touch the surface plate at point B. The center-to-center distance between the rollers is $L$.

**Step 2 — Construct the Right-Angled Triangle:**

Drop a perpendicular from the center of the upper roller to the surface plate. The vertical leg of the right triangle is exactly equal to the slip gauge height $h$ (because the lower roller touches the plate directly).

**Step 3 — Apply Trigonometry:**

In the right-angled triangle formed by the two roller centers and the slip gauge:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sin\theta &= \frac{\text{Opposite side}}{\text{Hypotenuse}} \\
\sin\theta &= \frac{h}{L} \\
\therefore \theta &= \sin^{-1}\left(\frac{h}{L}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for Slip Gauge Height (Inverse Case):**

If the angle $\theta$ is given and we need to *set* the sine bar:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sin\theta &= \frac{h}{L} \\
h &= L \cdot \sin\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

### Derivation 2 — Roller Diameter Correction

When the angle of the workpiece is referenced from the top tangent line of the rollers, the effective hypotenuse of the right triangle increases slightly. The corrected formula accounts for this.

**Step 1 — Identify the Geometric Shift:**

If the roller diameter is $d$ and the angle is $\theta$, the center of the upper roller rises vertically by an amount $d(1 - \cos\theta)$ above the tangent point on the workpiece surface.

**Step 2 — Modify the Height Equation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_{corrected} &= h_{calculated} - d\left(1 - \cos\theta\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Verification with a Worked Numerical Example:**

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Worked Problem 1 (Typical KTU Board Numerical)**
>
> **Question:** A sine bar of length **200 mm** is used to measure an angle. Slip gauges of total height **76.50 mm** are placed under one roller. Calculate the angle of the workpiece in degrees and minutes.
>
> **Step-by-Step Model Solution:**
>
> Given:
> - Length of sine bar, $L = 200$ mm
> - Height of slip gauges, $h = 76.50$ mm
>
> Apply the fundamental sine bar equation:
>
> $$\sin\theta = \frac{h}{L} = \frac{76.50}{200} = 0.3825$$
>
> Solve for $\theta$:
>
> $$\theta = \sin^{-1}(0.3825) = 22.494°$$
>
> Convert the decimal part (0.494°) into minutes:
>
> $$0.494° \times 60 \text{ min/deg} = 29.64 \text{ min} \approx 29' \, 38''$$
>
> Final Answer:
>
> $$\boxed{\theta = 22° \, 29' \, 38''}$$
>
> **KTU Board Answer Format:** $\theta = 22°29'38''$ (3 marks for formula, 2 marks for substitution, 2 marks for final answer in deg-min-sec format)

### Derivation 3 — Setting the Sine Bar for a Required Angle

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Worked Problem 2 (Reverse Problem — Setting Angle)**
>
> **Question:** An angle of **15°30′** is to be set on a **300 mm** sine bar. Calculate the height of slip gauges required.
>
> **Solution:**
>
> Step 1: Convert the angle entirely into decimal degrees:
>
> $$\theta = 15° + \frac{30}{60} = 15.5°$$
>
> Step 2: Convert decimal degrees into radians (since calculator $\sin$ function uses radians):
>
> $$\theta_{rad} = 15.5° \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 0.27053 \text{ rad}$$
>
> Step 3: Apply the height formula $h = L \cdot \sin\theta$:
>
> $$h = 300 \times \sin(15.5°) = 300 \times 0.26724 = 80.173 \text{ mm}$$
>
> Step 4: Select a combination of standard slip gauges to build **80.173 mm**:
>
> - 1.003 mm (standard slip gauge combination block)
> - 1.170 mm
> - 78.000 mm
> - Total = **80.173 mm** ✓
>
> Final Answer:
>
> $$\boxed{h = 80.173 \text{ mm}}$$

### Derivation 4 — Sensitivity Analysis (Why Long Sine Bars are Better)

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Worked Problem 3 (Sensitivity Calculation)**
>
> **Question:** Two sine bars of lengths **100 mm** and **300 mm** are available. If slip gauge resolution is **0.001 mm**, compute the smallest measurable angular change (sensitivity) for a workpiece angle of 30°.
>
> **Solution:**
>
> Differentiate $\sin\theta = h / L$ with respect to $h$:
>
> $$\cos\theta \cdot \delta\theta = \frac{\delta h}{L}$$
>
> Solve for $\delta\theta$:
>
> $$\delta\theta = \frac{\delta h}{L \cdot \cos\theta}$$
>
> **For L = 100 mm:**
>
> $$\delta\theta = \frac{0.001}{100 \times \cos(30°)} = \frac{0.001}{86.6025} = 1.1547 \times 10^{-5} \text{ rad}$$
>
> Convert to seconds: $1.1547 \times 10^{-5} \times \frac{180}{\pi} \times 3600 = 2.38''$ (2.38 seconds of arc)
>
> **For L = 300 mm:**
>
> $$\delta\theta = \frac{0.001}{300 \times \cos(30°)} = \frac{0.001}{259.808} = 3.85 \times 10^{-6} \text{ rad} = 0.79''$$
>
> **Inference:** The longer sine bar (300 mm) gives **3× better sensitivity** than the 100 mm bar. This is why precision tool rooms prefer long sine bars for fine-angle measurements.

### Derivation 5 — Combining Slip Gauges (Carey-Foster-Like Logic)

When a value of $h$ is non-standard, we build it from a minimum number of slip gauges. For a height of **80.173 mm**, we use the standard wringing sequence:

| Step | Slip Gauge Block Value (mm) | Cumulative Height (mm) |
|------|---------------------------|------------------------|
| 1 | 1.003 | 1.003 |
| 2 | 1.170 | 2.173 |
| 3 | 78.000 | 80.173 |

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Hall Tip:** Always quote slip gauge values to **3 decimal places** (e.g., 1.003, not 1.00) because slip gauges themselves are calibrated to 0.001 mm accuracy. Omitting decimals is a common 1-mark deduction in KTU valuation.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram A — Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture of Sine Bar Measurement

The following Mermaid diagram maps the functional architecture of a sine bar measurement system, illustrating the interaction between components, instruments, and the trigonometric computation chain.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Workpiece with Unknown Angle] --> B[Mounting on Sine Bar Top Surface]
    B --> C{Sine Bar Geometry Check}
    C --> D[Lower Roller Rests on Surface Plate]
    C --> E[Upper Roller Raised by Slip Gauge Stack h]
    D --> F[Right-Angled Triangle Formation]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Slip Gauge Stack Selection h equals L sin theta]
    G --> H[Dial Test Indicator DTI Sweep]
    H --> I{Surface Parallel to Plate}
    I -->|No| J[Add or Remove Slip Gauges]
    J --> G
    I -->|Yes| K[Read Final Slip Gauge Height h]
    K --> L[Apply Formula sin theta equals h divided by L]
    L --> M[Compute theta in degrees and minutes]
    M --> N[Final Angle Output with Accuracy plus minus 1 arc minute]
    P[Slip Gauge Calibration Block] -.->|SI Traceability| G
    Q[Surface Plate Grade 0 or 1] -.->|Datum Reference| D
    R[Ambient Temperature 20 plus minus 1 degree Celsius] -.->|Error Control| P
```

### Diagram B — Mermaid Component Inventory and Data Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HW[Hardware Components]
        A1[Surface Plate Grade 0]
        A2[Sine Bar L = 200 mm]
        A3[Slip Gauge Set Grade 1]
        A4[Workpiece]
        A5[Dial Test Indicator 0.01 mm LC]
        A6[Clamping Straps]
    end
    subgraph PROC[Measurement Process]
        B1[Step 1 Wipe Surface Plate]
        B2[Step 2 Build Slip Gauge Stack]
        B3[Step 3 Place Sine Bar and Workpiece]
        B4[Step 4 DTI Sweep and Adjust]
        B5[Step 5 Record h and Apply Formula]
    end
    subgraph OUT[Output Stage]
        C1[Angle theta in deg min sec]
        C2[Validation with Bevel Protractor]
        C3[Final Report and Traceability Log]
    end
    A1 --> B1
    A3 --> B2
    A2 --> B3
    A4 --> B3
    A6 --> B3
    A5 --> B4
    B1 --> B3
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> B4
    B4 --> B5
    B5 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
```

### Diagram C — Block-Level Conceptual Schematic of the Trigonometric Triangle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M[Hypotenuse equals Sine Bar Length L] --> N[Opposite Side equals Slip Gauge Height h]
    M --> O[Adjacent Side equals Horizontal Projection L cos theta]
    N --> P[Angle theta at Lower Roller End]
    O --> P
    P --> Q[sin theta equals h divided by L]
    Q --> R[theta equals sin inverse h divided by L]
    R --> S[Result in Degrees Minutes and Seconds]
    S --> T[Compare with Specification Tolerance Band]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note for Students**
> The diagrams above intentionally avoid attempting a literal 2D drawing of the sine bar (since Mermaid is not a CAD tool). Instead, the **block-level functional architecture** precisely captures:
> - **Hardware inventory** (surface plate, sine bar, slip gauges, DTI, clamps)
> - **Process flow** (wipe → build stack → mount → DTI sweep → compute)
> - **Data flow** (height $h$ → trigonometric formula → angle output)
> - **Metrology controls** (calibration, datum reference, temperature compensation)
>
> This abstraction is exactly what KTU examiners expect in answer scripts when asked to "draw a labelled sketch of a sine bar."
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
Define a sine bar. State the formula used for angle measurement with a sine bar and explain its significance.

**Model Answer:**
A sine bar is a precision metrological instrument used to measure or set angles with high accuracy. It consists of a hardened steel bar with two cylindrical rollers of equal diameter, fixed at a precise center distance $L$ (100, 200, 300, or 500 mm).

The fundamental formula is:

$$\sin\theta = \frac{h}{L}$$

where $h$ = height of slip gauges, $L$ = length of sine bar, and $\theta$ = angle of the workpiece.

**Significance:** The sine bar is the most accurate method of angle measurement in tool rooms, capable of resolving angles to **± 1 minute of arc**, far surpassing the Vernier Bevel Protractor (± 5 minutes). It is essential for producing precision gauges, jigs, fixtures, and cutting tools.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
Why is a dial test indicator (DTI) used along with a sine bar during angle measurement? What is its typical least count?

**Model Answer:**
A Dial Test Indicator is used to **verify perfect parallelism** between the top surface of the workpiece and the reference surface plate. Once this parallelism is achieved (indicated by zero deflection of the DTI needle when swept across the surface), the trigonometric condition $\sin\theta = h/L$ becomes valid. Without the DTI, the human eye cannot detect deviations smaller than 5 minutes of arc, which would defeat the entire purpose of using a sine bar.

Typical least count of a DTI used in metrology labs: **0.01 mm** (10 µm). High-precision DTIs used in calibration labs have a least count of **0.001 mm** (1 µm) or **0.0005 mm** (0.5 µm).

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

**Q3 (A). [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** A sine bar of length **200 mm** is used to measure an angle. The slip gauges used have a total height of **65.42 mm**. Calculate the angle of the workpiece in **degrees, minutes, and seconds**. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Explain with a neat functional block diagram, the procedure for measuring an unknown angle of a workpiece using a sine bar. List the instruments and standards used. (7 Marks)

---

**Model Solution to Q3(A)(a):**

Given:
- Sine bar length, $L = 200$ mm
- Slip gauge height, $h = 65.42$ mm

Step 1: Apply the fundamental sine bar equation:

$$\sin\theta = \frac{h}{L} = \frac{65.42}{200} = 0.3271$$

Step 2: Compute $\theta$ in decimal degrees:

$$\theta = \sin^{-1}(0.3271) = 19.0914°$$

Step 3: Separate integer and fractional degrees:

- Integer part = 19°
- Fractional part = 0.0914°

Step 4: Convert fractional degrees into minutes:

$$0.0914° \times 60 = 5.484 \text{ min} \approx 5'$$

Step 5: Convert fractional minutes into seconds:

$$0.484 \text{ min} \times 60 = 29.04'' \approx 29''$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{\theta = 19° \, 5' \, 29''}$$

---

**Model Solution to Q3(A)(b):**

**Instruments and Standards Required:**
1. Precision surface plate (Grade 0 or 1) — acts as the datum
2. Sine bar (200 mm or 300 mm) — known length $L$
3. Slip gauge set (Grade 1, calibrated) — defines height $h$
4. Dial Test Indicator (DTI, LC 0.01 mm) with magnetic base
5. Clamping straps to hold the workpiece
6. Clean lint-free cloth and rust preventive oil

**Procedure (Mapped to Block Diagram):**
1. **Cleaning:** Wipe the surface plate, sine bar rollers, and slip gauges with a clean cloth.
2. **Mounting:** Place the sine bar on the surface plate with the workpiece clamped on top.
3. **Initial Guess:** Assume an approximate angle and calculate $h = L\sin\theta$.
4. **Stack Slip Gauges:** Wring the required slip gauge combination to a total height $h$.
5. **Raise One Roller:** Place the slip gauge stack under one of the sine bar rollers.
6. **DTI Sweep:** Mount the DTI on a stand and sweep its tip along the top surface of the workpiece.
7. **Iteration:** If the DTI shows deviation, add or remove slip gauges (typically ± 0.001 mm) until the DTI reads the same value at both ends.
8. **Record and Compute:** Note the final slip gauge height $h_{final}$, substitute into $\sin\theta = h_{final}/L$, and solve.
9. **Verification:** Cross-check the measured angle with a calibrated bevel protractor.
10. **Documentation:** Record values in a traceability log with date, operator, ambient temperature, and instrument IDs.

**Block Diagram Reference:** See SECTION_4, Diagram B — which shows the full hardware-to-output flow.

---

**Q3 (B). [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2, 14 Marks — Internal Choice Alternative]**

**(a)** An angle of **23°45′** is to be set on a **300 mm** sine bar. Determine the height of the slip gauge stack required and list the slip gauge combination to build this height. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Compare the sine bar with a Vernier Bevel Protractor with respect to least count, accuracy, principle, and application. (7 Marks)

---

**Model Solution to Q3(B)(a):**

Step 1: Convert angle to decimal degrees:

$$\theta = 23° + \frac{45'}{60} = 23.75°$$

Step 2: Convert to radians:

$$\theta_{rad} = 23.75° \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 0.41449 \text{ rad}$$

Step 3: Apply the height formula:

$$h = L \cdot \sin\theta = 300 \times \sin(23.75°) = 300 \times 0.40282 = 120.847 \text{ mm}$$

Step 4: Slip gauge combination to build 120.847 mm:

| S.No. | Block Value (mm) | Cumulative Height (mm) |
|-------|------------------|------------------------|
| 1 | 1.007 | 1.007 |
| 2 | 1.840 | 2.847 |
| 3 | 8.000 | 10.847 |
| 4 | 110.000 | 120.847 |

**Final Answer:** $h = 120.847$ mm, built from 4 slip gauge blocks.

---

**Model Solution to Q3(B)(b):**

| Parameter | Vernier Bevel Protractor | Sine Bar |
|-----------|-------------------------|----------|
| Least Count | 5 minutes (5') | Up to 1 minute (1') or better |
| Accuracy | ± 5 minutes | ± 1 minute (limited by slip gauge precision) |
| Principle | Direct comparison using graduated scale and vernier | Trigonometric — $\sin\theta = h/L$ |
| Standard Required | Self-contained graduated scale | Slip gauges + Surface plate + DTI |
| Cost | Lower | Higher (requires full metrology setup) |
| Application | Workshop-level, medium-accuracy angle checks | Tool room, calibration lab, high-precision components |
| Best for | Angles up to 360° | Angles typically 0° to 45° (limited by available slip gauge height) |
| Operator Skill | Moderate | High (requires wringing, calculation, DTI interpretation) |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark Deduction Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert decimal degrees to deg-min-sec:** Examiners specifically ask for "angle in degrees and minutes" — failing to convert results in a **2-mark deduction**.
> 2. **Using wrong sine bar length in the formula:** Students sometimes confuse $L$ with the roller diameter $d$. Always use **center-to-center distance** for $L$.
> 3. **Skipping the slip gauge combination step:** If the question asks for the height, the examiner also expects the actual slip gauge block combination. Omitting this costs **1 mark**.
> 4. **Sign errors with roller correction:** When the angle is measured from the top of the rollers, the correction term $d(1 - \cos\theta)$ must be **subtracted** from $h$, not added.
> 5. **Not stating the ambient temperature:** Metrology experiments require specifying 20 ± 1 °C. Examiners may deduct **0.5 mark** in lab viva for not mentioning this.
> 6. **Using $\tan\theta$ instead of $\sin\theta$:** This is a fundamental conceptual error. Always derive the formula geometrically before using it.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Sine bar = precision angle measurement using trigonometry**
- **Core formula:** $\sin\theta = h / L$ — burn this into memory
- **Inverse formula:** $h = L \cdot \sin\theta$ (used to *set* an angle)
- **Standard lengths:** 100, 200, 300, 500 mm — choose the **longest available** for best sensitivity
- **Roller correction** (advanced): $h_{corrected} = h - d(1 - \cos\theta)$
- **Instruments required:** Surface plate (Grade 0/1), Sine bar, Slip gauges (Grade 0/1), DTI (LC 0.01 mm or finer), Clamping straps
- **Sensitivity formula:** $\delta\theta = \delta h / (L \cos\theta)$ — longer $L$ = better sensitivity
- **Limitations:** Only suitable for angles typically **0° to 45°**; beyond this, the slip gauge stack becomes impractically tall
- **Always quote angles in Degrees-Minutes-Seconds (DMS)** format for KTU exams
- **Always specify slip gauge values to 3 decimal places** (e.g., 1.003 mm, not 1.00 mm)
- **Always mention 20 ± 1 °C** as the working temperature in lab records
- **Always wring slip gauges** — never stack them loosely
- **Common mistake to avoid:** Confusing $L$ (center distance) with $d$ (roller diameter)
- **Why sine bar is preferred:** It is **SI-traceable** via slip gauges, gives **± 1'** accuracy, and is **ISO 9001 audit-friendly**
- **Typical exam time allocation:** 7 marks = ~12 minutes; 14 marks = ~25 minutes in KTU ESE

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Strategy for KTU 2024**
> 1. Memorize the formula and its inverse cold — examiners give easy 2–3 marks just for writing the formula.
> 2. Practice at least 3 numerical problems on decimal-to-DMS and DMS-to-decimal conversion.
> 3. Draw a labelled block diagram of the sine bar setup at least once before the exam.
> 4. Be ready to write a 5-line comparison between sine bar and bevel protractor — this is a **favourite 7-mark question**.
> 5. Remember: the **roller correction formula** is asked as a 2-mark sub-part in almost every KTU paper — never skip it.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measurements of Surface Roughness Using Tally Surf / Mechanical Comparator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUL407 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Measurements of Surface Roughness Using Tally Surf / Mechanical Comparator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Surface Roughness?

**Surface roughness** is a measure of the texture of a manufactured surface. It is quantified by the deviations of the surface profile from its ideal (nominal) form along a defined measurement path. In fluid mechanics and hydraulic machinery contexts, surface roughness is a **critical parameter** because it directly influences:

- **Frictional resistance** in pipes and channels (Moody diagram input)
- **Boundary layer development** over hydraulic surfaces
- **Cavitation susceptibility** of turbine blades and pump impellers
- **Head loss** in conveyance systems
- **Performance efficiency** of hydraulic machines

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> *Surface roughness is the arithmetic average of the absolute vertical deviations of a real surface from its ideal form, measured normal to the surface, over a specified sampling length. It is the most widely used international parameter, denoted as **Ra** (Roughness Average), expressed in micrometres (µm) or micro-inches (µin).*

## 1.2 The Tally Surf (Talysurf) — Stylus Profilometer

The **TalySurf** (a trade name originally by Taylor Hobson, now used generically) is a **contact-type stylus profilometer** used to map the microscopic peaks and valleys of a surface with extreme precision.

**Key Components:**
1. **Diamond Stylus Tip** — radius typically **2 µm** or **10 µm** (per ISO 4287)
2. **Traversing Skid or Datum Reference** — provides a sliding reference plane
3. **Pickup (Transducer)** — converts vertical motion into an electrical signal
4. **Amplifier & Filter Stage** — applies **Gaussian filter (0.8 mm cutoff)** to separate roughness from waviness
5. **Display / Recorder** — plots the magnified profile and computes Ra digitally

## 1.3 The Mechanical Comparator

A **mechanical comparator** (also called a **profilograph** or **stylus comparator** in some KTU lab manuals) is a simpler, purely mechanical instrument. It uses a **lever-and-dial mechanism** to magnify the up-and-down movement of a stylus tracing the workpiece.

- **Magnification:** typically **1000× to 10,000×** (mechanical/optical)
- **Output:** analog dial gauge reading (in µm) or a magnified profile on a chart paper
- **Use:** quick shop-floor assessment, not as detailed as TalySurf

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Fingerprint Reader of a Machine"**
> Imagine running your fingertip slowly across a freshly painted wall versus a sheet of glass. Your nerves detect microscopic bumps on the wall but nothing on the glass. A TalySurf is essentially an **electronic "fingertip"** — but instead of nerves, a tiny diamond needle feels the bumps and an amplifier translates them into a number (**Ra**). The mechanical comparator is the older, more "mechanical" version of the same idea, like using a **magnifying glass with a pin** pressed against a record to feel its grooves.

## 1.4 Why Two Different Instruments?

| Feature | TalySurf (Electronic) | Mechanical Comparator |
|---|---|---|
| Resolution | **0.001 µm** | **0.1 – 1.0 µm** |
| Output | Digital Ra + profile plot | Dial reading / chart |
| Filter | Built-in ISO filter | None (raw signal) |
| Best for | Lab-grade measurement | Workshop quick check |
| Principle | Piezoelectric / Inductive transducer | Lever magnification |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Lab Relevance:** Hydraulic machine lab manuals commonly use both — the **TalySurf** for precision roughness of pipe samples and turbine blade sections, and the **mechanical comparator** for quick verification of turned/machined surfaces.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Stylus Profilometer — Operating Principle

The TalySurf operates on the **contact stylus method (ISO 4287 / ISO 13565)**:

### Step-by-step Logic:

1. **Stylus Engagement:** A diamond tip of known radius (e.g., **2 µm**) is brought into contact with the test surface under a small, constant **static force (typ. 0.7 – 1.0 mN)**. This low force prevents surface damage.
2. **Horizontal Traverse:** The stylus is drawn across the surface at a constant speed (typ. **0.5 mm/s** or **1 mm/s**). The vertical displacement of the stylus follows the surface peaks and valleys.
3. **Transduction:** Vertical motion is converted by a **piezoelectric** or **inductive (LVDT)** pickup into a proportional electrical signal.
4. **Signal Conditioning:**
   - The raw signal is split into **roughness profile (R)** and **waviness profile (W)** using a filter.
   - The standard filter is the **Gaussian filter with 0.8 mm cutoff** (λc) for general engineering surfaces.
5. **Averaging:** The instrument computes **Ra** (or other parameters) over the evaluation length and displays it.

## 2.2 Cut-off (Sampling) Length — A Critical Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why a Cut-off is Needed:**
> A single tiny peak should not be allowed to dominate the average. The surface is split into **N equal sampling lengths (lr)**, and Ra is calculated as the **mean of the Ra of each segment**.

- **Standard cutoff values (per ISO 4288):**
  - **0.08 mm** — for very fine surfaces (Ra < 0.2 µm)
  - **0.25 mm** — fine surfaces (0.2 < Ra < 0.8 µm)
  - **0.8 mm** — most common for general engineering (0.8 < Ra < 3.2 µm)
  - **2.5 mm** — coarse surfaces (Ra > 3.2 µm)

- **Evaluation length (ln)** = **5 × lr** (typically 5 sampling lengths averaged)

## 2.3 Key Surface Roughness Parameters (KTU High-Yield)

| Symbol | Parameter Name | Definition | Formula | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Ra** | Arithmetic Mean Roughness | Mean of absolute vertical deviations from mean line | $R_a = \dfrac{1}{l_r}\displaystyle\int_0^{l_r} \vert y(x) \vert \, dx$ | µm |
| **Rz** | Mean Peak-to-Valley Height | Mean of 5 highest peaks + 5 lowest valleys over lr | $R_z = \dfrac{(Rp_1 + \dots + Rp_5) - (Rv_1 + \dots + Rv_5)}{5}$ | µm |
| **Rq** | Root Mean Square Roughness | RMS of vertical deviations | $R_q = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{l_r}\displaystyle\int_0^{l_r} y^2(x) \, dx}$ | µm |
| **Rt** | Total Roughness Height | Max peak-to-valley in evaluation length | $R_t = Rp_{max} - Rv_{min}$ | µm |
| **Rsk** | Skewness | Asymmetry of profile (negative = bearing surface) | $R_{sk} = \dfrac{1}{R_q^3}\cdot\dfrac{1}{l_r}\displaystyle\int y^3(x)\,dx$ | — |
| **Rku** | Kurtosis | Sharpness of peak distribution | $R_{ku} = \dfrac{1}{R_q^4}\cdot\dfrac{1}{l_r}\displaystyle\int y^4(x)\,dx$ | — |
| **λc** | Cut-off wavelength | Filter cutoff | (per ISO 4288 table) | mm |

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind Ra Dominance:**
> Ra is the **most universally quoted** parameter because it is statistically robust, easy to compute, and correlates well with most functional performance metrics. Rz is preferred in some countries (Germany, Japan) for surfaces like cylinder bores where peak height is more critical.

## 2.4 Mechanical Comparator — Operating Principle

The mechanical comparator uses **purely mechanical amplification**:

1. Stylus rides on the surface.
2. Vertical motion is transferred to a **pivoted lever** (often with a 10:1 or 100:1 ratio).
3. The amplified motion moves a **dial indicator needle** or **chart pen**.
4. Reading is taken as the **peak-to-valley difference** (often Rz or Rt equivalent) directly.

**Disadvantage:** No electronic filtering — waviness and dirt can corrupt the reading.
