## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hydraulic pipes:** Ra determines Darcy-Weisbach friction factor **f** via the relative roughness **ε/D**, where **ε ≈ 3–6 × Ra** for commercial pipes.
- **Turbine blades:** Surface finish affects **boundary layer transition** and **cavitation inception**.
- **Pump impellers:** Smooth surfaces improve **hydraulic efficiency** and reduce **NPSHr**.
- **Seal surfaces:** Ra < 0.4 µm typically required for hydraulic seals.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Ra (Arithmetic Mean Roughness)

Let the surface profile be a continuous function **y(x)** over a sampling length **lr**. The **mean line** is defined such that the areas above and below it are equal:

$$
\int_0^{l_r} y(x) \, dx = 0
$$

The **arithmetic mean deviation** is the average of the **absolute** vertical distances from this mean line.

$$
R_a = \frac{1}{l_r} \int_0^{l_r} \vert y(x) \vert \, dx
$$

**Discrete (sampled) form** for N measurement points:

$$
R_a = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \vert y_i \vert
$$

### Steps explained:
1. Sample the profile at **N equally spaced points** along lr.
2. Read each vertical deviation **yi** from the mean line.
3. Take the **absolute value** of each (peaks and valleys both contribute positively).
4. **Sum** them.
5. **Divide by N** to get the average.

## 3.2 Derivation of Rz (Mean Peak-to-Valley Height)

In the **sampling length lr**, identify the **5 highest peaks** (Rp1 … Rp5) and the **5 deepest valleys** (Rv1 … Rv5). The peak-to-valley height for that segment is:

$$
R_{zi} = \frac{(Rp_1 + Rp_2 + Rp_3 + Rp_4 + Rp_5) - (Rv_1 + Rv_2 + Rv_3 + Rv_4 + Rv_5)}{5}
$$

Then average **Rzi** over the **5 sampling segments** of the evaluation length **ln = 5 lr**:

$$
R_z = \frac{1}{5} \sum_{i=1}^{5} R_{zi}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion relationship (approximate, for reference only):**
> For a Gaussian-distributed profile, $R_z \approx 4 \times R_a$ to $R_z \approx 7 \times R_a$, with $R_z \approx 5.6\,R_a$ as a typical midpoint. KTU exam questions often use **Rz ≈ 4 Ra** as a quick rule of thumb.

## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example — KTU Exam Style

### **Problem:**
A pipe sample is tested on a TalySurf using a **cutoff lr = 0.8 mm** over an evaluation length **ln = 4.0 mm**. The absolute deviations $\vert y_i \vert$ (in µm) at 10 sampled points in one segment are:

**0.4, 0.9, 1.2, 0.6, 1.5, 0.8, 1.1, 0.5, 1.3, 0.7**

Assume the mean line is correctly set (∑ yi = 0 implied by symmetry of absolute values).

**(a)** Calculate **Ra** for this segment.
**(b)** If the pipe diameter **D = 50 mm**, estimate the **equivalent sand-grain roughness ε** and the **relative roughness ε/D** for use in the Moody chart.

### **Solution:**

**Part (a) — Ra calculation:**

$$
R_a = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \vert y_i \vert
$$

$$
R_a = \frac{1}{10} (0.4 + 0.9 + 1.2 + 0.6 + 1.5 + 0.8 + 1.1 + 0.5 + 1.3 + 0.7)
$$

$$
R_a = \frac{1}{10} \times 9.0
$$

$$
R_a = 0.9 \, \mu m
$$

**Part (b) — Equivalent sand-grain roughness and relative roughness:**

The empirical relationship between measured **Ra** and the **equivalent sand-grain roughness ε** (used in Moody chart) is:

$$
\varepsilon \approx 6 \times R_a
$$

$$
\varepsilon \approx 6 \times 0.9 \, \mu m = 5.4 \, \mu m = 5.4 \times 10^{-3} \, mm
$$

Now compute relative roughness:

$$
\frac{\varepsilon}{D} = \frac{5.4 \times 10^{-3} \, mm}{50 \, mm} = 1.08 \times 10^{-4}
$$

### **Result Summary (Analytical Matrix):**

| Quantity | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Arithmetic mean roughness | $R_a$ | **0.9** | µm |
| Cutoff length | $l_r$ | 0.8 | mm |
| Evaluation length | $l_n$ | 4.0 | mm |
| Equivalent sand-grain roughness | $\varepsilon$ | **5.4** | µm |
| Relative roughness | $\varepsilon / D$ | **1.08 × 10⁻⁴** | — |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Bonus Insight:** With ε/D ≈ 10⁻⁴, the pipe is in the **"transitionally rough"** regime of the Moody chart. The friction factor **f** will depend on Reynolds number, but a typical value at Re = 10⁵ would be **f ≈ 0.022** (Colebrook-White equation).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — TalySurf Signal Flow

The following Mermaid diagram shows the **functional architecture flow** of a TalySurf stylus profilometer — from the physical contact at the diamond tip all the way to the digital Ra display.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[workpiece surface] --> B[diamond stylus tip radius 2 micron]
    B --> C[stylus arm with constant force 0.7 mN]
    C --> D[transducer LVDT or piezoelectric]
    D --> E[signal amplifier stage]
    E --> F[gaussian filter cutoff 0.8 mm]
    F --> G[microcontroller ADC sampling]
    G --> H[Ra and Rz computation unit]
    H --> I[LCD display digital readout]
    H --> J[profile plot on paper or screen]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#333
    style B fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#333
    style C fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#333
    style D fill:#e8f5e1,stroke:#333
    style E fill:#e8f5e1,stroke:#333
    style F fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#333
    style G fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#333
    style H fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#333
    style I fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#333
    style J fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#333
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Mechanical Comparator Working Principle

The mechanical comparator uses **purely mechanical amplification** with no electronics, as shown in the Mermaid flow below.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[test specimen on datum table] --> B[stylus rides on surface]
    B --> C[pivoted lever 10 to 1 ratio]
    C --> D[amplified vertical motion]
    D --> E1[dial indicator needle]
    D --> E2[chart pen on rotating drum]
    E1 --> F[manual reading peak to valley in micrometres]
    E2 --> G[visual magnified profile trace]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#333
    style B fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#333
    style C fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#333
    style D fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#333
    style E1 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#333
    style E2 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#333
    style F fill:#e8f5e1,stroke:#333
    style G fill:#e8f5e1,stroke:#333
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Filter Decomposition of a Surface Profile

The total surface profile **P** is mathematically split into **roughness R**, **waviness W**, and **form F** by applying filters of different cutoff wavelengths. The Mermaid diagram below shows this conceptual hierarchy.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P[primary profile P total measured signal] --> S1[short wavelength filter lambda c 0.8 mm]
    P --> S2[long wavelength filter lambda f 2.5 mm]
    S1 --> R[roughness profile R peaks and valleys only]
    S2 --> W[waviness profile W broad undulations]
    R --> SUB[subtract R and W from P]
    W --> SUB
    SUB --> F[form profile F overall shape]
    R --> RA[compute Ra arithmetic mean]
    R --> RZ[compute Rz peak to valley]
    R --> RQ[compute Rq RMS]
    RA --> OUT[digital display]
    RZ --> OUT
    RQ --> OUT

    style P fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#333
    style R fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#333
    style W fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#333
    style F fill:#e8f5e1,stroke:#333
    style OUT fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#333
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Read this diagram from top to bottom:** A real surface contains **all three components** simultaneously. The filter is what isolates the **roughness (R)** — the small-scale peaks and valleys we care about for friction and wear.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Define surface roughness. What is the significance of the parameter Ra in fluid flow applications?**

**Model Answer:**

**Surface roughness** is a measure of the fine irregularities present on a manufactured surface, quantified as the arithmetic mean of the absolute vertical deviations of the surface profile from its ideal (mean) form over a specified sampling length.

**Significance in fluid flow:**

- Ra directly determines the **equivalent sand-grain roughness ε** of the pipe wall, where $\varepsilon \approx 6\,R_a$.
- ε enters the **Colebrook-White equation** to compute the Darcy friction factor **f**:

$$
\frac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -2 \log_{10} \left( \frac{\varepsilon / D}{3.7} + \frac{2.51}{Re\sqrt{f}} \right)
$$

- This **f** then gives the **head loss** $h_f = f \dfrac{L}{D} \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ in pipelines carrying fluid in hydraulic machines and conveyance systems.
- A smoother surface (lower Ra) reduces pumping cost and energy consumption.

---

### **Question 2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish between the TalySurf and a mechanical comparator as used in surface roughness measurement.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | TalySurf | Mechanical Comparator |
|---|---|---|
| Principle | Stylus + electronic transducer | Stylus + mechanical lever |
| Output | Digital Ra and profile plot | Analog dial / chart trace |
| Filter | Has built-in Gaussian filter | No electronic filter |
| Resolution | ~0.001 µm | ~0.1 µm |
| Application | Laboratory precision | Workshop quick check |
| Cost | High | Low |

The TalySurf provides **quantitative digital output** with ISO-standard filtering, while the mechanical comparator is a **simpler, rugged tool** for shop-floor inspection where high accuracy is not critical.

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> *For each of the following main questions, **two alternative 14-mark questions (Q-A and Q-B)** are provided, with internal sub-parts at 7 marks each.*

---

### **MAIN QUESTION: Surface Roughness Measurement**

#### ✅ **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a **TalySurf** instrument for measuring surface roughness. State the role of the Gaussian filter and the standard cutoff value used.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A pipe of diameter **100 mm** is tested on a TalySurf. The measured Ra over a sampling length of 0.8 mm is **1.2 µm**. Estimate the **equivalent sand-grain roughness ε**, the **relative roughness ε/D**, and predict whether the flow will be in the **hydraulically smooth**, **transitional**, or **fully rough** regime at **Re = 1 × 10⁵**. Use Moody chart reasoning.

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A:**

**(a) Working of TalySurf — 7 marks**

1. **Stylus Contact (1 mark):** A diamond stylus of tip radius **2 µm** is lowered onto the surface under a light, constant force (~0.7 mN). The low force prevents damage.
2. **Horizontal Traverse (1 mark):** The stylus is drawn across the surface at a uniform speed (~0.5 mm/s). Vertical deflection follows the peaks and valleys.
3. **Transduction (1 mark):** An **LVDT (Linear Variable Differential Transformer)** or **piezoelectric pickup** converts the vertical motion into a proportional electrical signal.
4. **Signal Conditioning — Filter (2 marks):** A **Gaussian filter with cutoff wavelength λc = 0.8 mm** is applied. This filter separates the **roughness profile R** (short wavelength, fine irregularities) from the **waviness profile W** (long wavelength, broad undulations) and **form F** (overall shape). Without the filter, low-frequency components would artificially inflate Ra.
5. **Computation and Display (1 mark):** The instrument samples the filtered signal digitally, computes **Ra = (1/N) Σ|yi|** over **5 consecutive sampling lengths** (evaluation length ln = 5 lr = 4 mm), and displays the result.
6. **Profile Plot (1 mark):** Simultaneously, the amplified profile is traced on graph paper or shown on a screen for visual inspection.

**Role of Gaussian filter:** Removes low-frequency noise (vibration, form errors) so that only true microscale roughness contributes to Ra. The 0.8 mm cutoff is the **ISO 4288 standard** for the most common engineering surface range (0.8 µm < Ra < 3.2 µm).

---

**(b) Numerical — 7 marks**

Given:
- Pipe diameter D = 100 mm = 0.1 m
- Measured Ra = 1.2 µm = 1.2 × 10⁻³ mm
- Re = 1 × 10⁵

**Step 1 — Equivalent sand-grain roughness (1 mark):**

$$
\varepsilon \approx 6 \times R_a = 6 \times 1.2 \, \mu m = 7.2 \, \mu m = 7.2 \times 10^{-3} \, mm
$$

**Step 2 — Relative roughness (1 mark):**

$$
\frac{\varepsilon}{D} = \frac{7.2 \times 10^{-3} \, mm}{100 \, mm} = 7.2 \times 10^{-5}
$$

**Step 3 — Regime determination using Moody chart boundaries (5 marks):**

From the Moody chart, the **boundary between hydraulically smooth and transitional** flow at Re = 10⁵ occurs at approximately:

$$
\left(\frac{\varepsilon}{D}\right)_{boundary} \approx \frac{100}{Re} = \frac{100}{10^5} = 10^{-3}
$$

The **boundary between transitional and fully rough** at Re = 10⁵ is at:

$$
\left(\frac{\varepsilon}{D}\right)_{boundary} \approx \frac{1500}{Re^{0.9}} \approx \frac{1500}{10^{4.5}} \approx 4.7 \times 10^{-2}
$$

Compare our value:
- Our relative roughness: **7.2 × 10⁻⁵**
- Smooth–transitional boundary: **10⁻³**
- Since **7.2 × 10⁻⁵ < 10⁻³**, the flow is in the **hydraulically smooth regime**.

**Conclusion:** The flow is **hydraulically smooth** at Re = 10⁵, meaning viscous sublayer fully covers the roughness elements, and the friction factor depends only on Re (Blasius formula applies).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the × 6 factor:** Many students directly use Ra as ε. The Moody chart uses **equivalent sand-grain roughness ε**, NOT Ra. Always apply $\varepsilon \approx 6\,R_a$.
> 2. **Wrong unit conversion:** Ensure mm-µm consistency. Mixing units gives a relative roughness off by 1000×.
> 3. **Skipping regime analysis:** KTU examiners explicitly award marks for stating **smooth / transitional / fully rough** based on the chart.

---

#### ✅ **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define the following surface roughness parameters: **Ra, Rz, Rq, and Rt**. State the standard sampling and evaluation lengths as per ISO 4288.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **mechanical comparator method** of measuring surface roughness. How does its accuracy compare with the TalySurf? Mention two practical applications in a hydraulic machines laboratory.

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B:**

**(a) Definitions — 7 marks** *(1.5 marks each + 1 mark for ISO 4288)*

- **Ra (Arithmetic Mean Roughness):** The arithmetic mean of the absolute values of the vertical deviations of the roughness profile from the mean line, measured over a sampling length lr.

  $$R_a = \frac{1}{l_r} \int_0^{l_r} \vert y(x) \vert \, dx$$

- **Rz (Mean Peak-to-Valley Height):** The mean of the sum of the 5 highest peaks and 5 deepest valleys within a single sampling length, averaged over 5 sampling segments of the evaluation length.

  $$R_z = \frac{1}{5} \sum_{i=1}^{5} \left[ \frac{\sum_{j=1}^{5} Rp_{j} - \sum_{j=1}^{5} Rv_{j}}{5} \right]$$

- **Rq (Root-Mean-Square Roughness):** The square root of the mean of the squares of the vertical deviations.

  $$R_q = \sqrt{\frac{1}{l_r} \int_0^{l_r} y^2(x) \, dx}$$

- **Rt (Total Roughness Height):** The maximum peak-to-valley height observed anywhere in the evaluation length ln.

  $$R_t = Rp_{max} - Rv_{min}$$

**ISO 4288 Standards (1 mark):**
- Sampling length lr is selected from {0.08, 0.25, 0.8, 2.5 mm} based on Ra range.
- Evaluation length ln = 5 × lr.
- Most common cutoff for general engineering: **lr = 0.8 mm**, ln = 4.0 mm.

---

**(b) Mechanical Comparator — 7 marks** *(3 marks description, 2 marks comparison, 2 marks applications)*

**Description:**
A mechanical comparator consists of:
1. A **datum (reference) skid** that rides on the surface, ignoring high-frequency peaks.
2. A **stylus** mounted on a **pivoted lever** that amplifies the vertical motion (typically 10:1 to 100:1).
3. A **dial indicator** or **chart recording drum** at the lever's amplified end.
4. As the comparator is traversed manually, the stylus follows the surface, the lever magnifies the motion, and the dial records the peak-to-valley excursion (often reported as Rz equivalent).

**Comparison with TalySurf:**

| Parameter | Mechanical Comparator | TalySurf |
|---|---|---|
| Resolution | 0.1 – 1 µm | 0.001 µm |
| Filter | None | Gaussian (0.8 mm) |
| Output | Manual reading | Digital + profile |
| Accuracy | ± 10% of reading | ± 2% of reading |
| Cost | Low | High |

**Two Applications in a Hydraulic Machines Lab:**
1. **Quick roughness check of machined pump impeller surfaces** before final assembly.
2. **Verification of pipe sample finishes** in laboratory friction-loss experiments (Darcy apparatus), where relative roughness must be characterized before Moody-chart-based friction factor calculations.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Question B:**
> 1. **Confusing Ra with Rz formulas:** Many students write the Rz formula incorrectly. Remember — **Ra uses absolute deviations**, **Rz uses the difference between 5 peaks and 5 valleys** averaged over 5 segments.
> 2. **Missing the "5 segments" averaging in Rz:** The averaging is over **5 separate sampling lengths**, not a single one.
> 3. **Omitting ISO numbers:** KTU 2024 scheme questions on roughness MUST cite **ISO 4287 / 4288** for full marks.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ **Surface roughness** = measure of fine vertical irregularities on a surface, parameterised as **Ra** (most common), Rz, Rq, Rt.
- ✅ **Ra definition:** $R_a = \dfrac{1}{l_r}\displaystyle\int_0^{l_r}\vert y(x)\vert\,dx$ — arithmetic mean of absolute deviations.
- ✅ **TalySurf** = contact-type stylus profilometer with diamond tip (radius **2 µm**), LVDT/piezo transducer, **Gaussian filter with λc = 0.8 mm** (ISO 4288), digital Ra output.
- ✅ **Mechanical comparator** = purely mechanical lever amplification, no electronic filter, lower accuracy (~0.1 µm resolution).
- ✅ **Cutoff selection** (per ISO 4288): 0.08 mm / 0.25 mm / 0.8 mm / 2.5 mm — chosen by Ra range.
- ✅ **Evaluation length ln = 5 × lr** (standard averaging).
- ✅ **Moody chart input** (CRITICAL for fluid mechanics): $\varepsilon \approx 6\,R_a$, then $\varepsilon / D$ used in Colebrook-White equation.
- ✅ **Regime boundaries (Moody chart):** Smooth if $\varepsilon / D < 100/Re$; fully rough if $\varepsilon / D > 1500/Re^{0.9}$; transitional in between.
- ✅ **For KTU lab record:** Always state the **stylus tip radius**, **cutoff**, **evaluation length**, **Ra value**, and the **estimated ε** in your experimental result table.
- ✅ **SI units:** Ra in **µm**; ε in same unit as D (mm); relative roughness ε/D is **dimensionless**.
- ✅ **Most common KTU mistake to avoid:** Do NOT use Ra directly in the Colebrook equation — convert to **ε** first.
- ✅ **Cross-relation:** For Gaussian profile, $R_z \approx 4$ to $7\,R_a$; rule of thumb used in KTU: $R_z \approx 4\,R_a$.
- ✅ **Hydraulic relevance:** Smoother surfaces (low Ra) → lower friction factor → less pumping power → higher hydraulic machine efficiency.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of Tool Signature Using Profile Projector
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUL407 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of Tool Signature Using Profile Projector

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tool Signature** is a standardized numerical code or sequence of angles (and nose radius) that completely and uniquely defines the geometry of a **single-point cutting tool** in the **American Standards Association (ASA) system**. It provides a compact "fingerprint" of the tool's geometry, enabling the machinist to specify, manufacture, and reproduce a tool without ambiguity.

A single-point cutting tool has an infinite number of points on its surface, but its **functional geometry is defined by 7 critical elements** (6 angles + 1 nose radius). The tool signature is the convention used to express these 7 elements in a fixed sequence.

The **ASA tool signature** is read in the order:

$$\text{Tool Signature} = (\alpha_b \; \alpha_s \; \alpha_e \; \alpha_s' \; C_e \; C_s \; r)$$

Where the parameters are defined with respect to three reference planes:
- **Reference Plane (Machined Surface)**
- **Cutting Edge Plane**
- **Tool Axis / Base Plane**

## 1.2 The Three Reference Planes of a Single-Point Tool

| Reference Plane | Definition | Role |
|---|---|---|
| **Base / Tool Axis Plane** | Plane containing the tool shank base, perpendicular to the tool axis | Reference for rake and clearance angles |
| **Cutting Edge Plane (Reference Plane)** | Tangent to the cutting edge, perpendicular to the base plane | Reference for cutting edge angles |
| **Machined Surface Plane** | Theoretically the surface generated on the workpiece | Reference for orthogonal plane geometry |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Face Print" of a Cutting Tool

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Think of a cutting tool as a human face. Just as a "faceprint" records key features (nose length, eye width, jaw angle, brow angle…) in a fixed code to uniquely identify you, the **tool signature** records the critical geometry of a tool in a fixed code (7 numbers) to uniquely identify and reproduce it.
>
> A carpenter uses a chisel, a lathe operator uses a HSS tool, a CNC machine uses a carbide insert — every time, only those **7 numbers** dictate how the tool will cut. Change one number, and the chip formation, cutting force, surface finish, and tool life all change.

## 1.4 Tool Signature Systems Used in Practice

| System | Full Form | Where Used |
|---|---|---|
| **ASA** | American Standards Association | Standard single-point tools (lathes, planers) |
| **ORS** | Orthogonal Rake System | Simplification for force analysis |
| **NRS** | Normal Rake System | Form tools and complex inserts |

> [!NOTE]
> For this experiment, we will focus on the **ASA Tool Signature** as it is the most common system referenced in KTU manufacturing and metrology labs.

## 1.5 The 7 Elements of ASA Tool Signature

1. **αb — Back Rake Angle** (measured in a plane parallel to the longitudinal axis and perpendicular to the base of the tool)
2. **αs — Side Rake Angle**
3. **αe — End Relief Angle / End Clearance Angle**
4. **αs' — Side Relief Angle / Side Clearance Angle**
5. **Ce — End Cutting Edge Angle**
6. **Cs — Side Cutting Edge Angle**
7. **r — Nose Radius** (in mm)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle of the Profile Projector (Optical Comparator)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **Profile Projector** (also called an **Optical Comparator** or **Shadowgraph**) is a non-contact optical measuring instrument that projects a **magnified shadow/profile** of a small component onto a large viewing screen. The magnified image is then measured using cross-hair lines, micrometer dials, or digital encoders.

### Functional Principle

The component (here, a single-point cutting tool) is placed on the **glass work stage** between a **light source** and a **projection lens**. Light from below passes around/through the tool silhouette, and the objective lens focuses and **magnifies the profile** onto a ground-glass screen containing reference cross-lines and protractor scales.

$$M = \frac{\text{Size of image on screen}}{\text{Actual size of object}}$$

Typical magnifications available: **10×, 20×, 50×, 100×**.

## 2.2 Optical System of a Profile Projector (Block Logic)

The optical train is:
1. **Light Source** (Halogen / Tungsten lamp, 24V)
2. **Condenser Lens** → collimates and concentrates light onto the workpiece
3. **Work Stage (X-Y table)** → holds the tool, has micrometer feeds (0.01 mm resolution)
4. **Objective Lens** → produces magnified image
5. **Projection Screen** → ground glass with **cross-hairs, rotary protractor (0°–360°) and linear scales**
6. **Measuring System** → micrometer heads / digital readouts

## 2.3 Why Use a Profile Projector for Tool Signature?

> [!NOTE]
> - **Non-contact** → no scratching of the sharp cutting edge.
> - **High magnification** → angles on the order of 0°–15° can be measured with high precision.
> - **Shadow image** is unobstructed and can be **rotated** by tilting the tool or rotating the screen protractor.
> - Faster than a **toolmaker's microscope** for routine inspection.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Symbol | Quantity | Sign Convention | Typical Range | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | αb | Back Rake Angle | Positive if rake face slopes downward from cutting edge toward shank | $-10°$ to $+25°$ | degrees |
| 2 | αs | Side Rake Angle | Positive if slope is toward chip flow direction | $-10°$ to $+20°$ | degrees |
| 3 | αe | End Relief Angle | Always positive; prevents tool flank rubbing | $5°$ to $15°$ | degrees |
| 4 | αs' | Side Relief Angle | Always positive | $5°$ to $15°$ | degrees |
| 5 | Ce | End Cutting Edge Angle | Angle of end cutting edge w.r.t. machined surface | $5°$ to $30°$ | degrees |
| 6 | Cs | Side Cutting Edge Angle | Angle of side cutting edge w.r.t. machined surface | $10°$ to $30°$ | degrees |
| 7 | r | Nose Radius | Radius of tool nose | 0.4 to 3.2 | mm |

### Key Conversion / Identity Equations

$$M_{projector} = \frac{d_{screen}}{d_{actual}}$$

$$\text{True angle} = \text{Angle measured on screen protractor}$$

(Note: Projection is geometrically similar, so angles are **preserved** under magnification — only linear dimensions scale.)

$$\text{True length} = \frac{\text{Measured length on screen}}{M}$$

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **tool signature directly controls**:
> - **Cutting forces** (rake angles reduce Fc)
> - **Chip flow direction** (rake angle sign redirects chip)
> - **Surface finish** (nose radius and side cutting edge angle)
> - **Heat dissipation** (clearance angles control contact area)
> - **Vibration chatter** (relief angles prevent rubbing)

Industries that depend on precise tool signature specification: **automotive (engine blocks, crankshafts), aerospace (turbine shafts), die & mold making, general turning and milling operations**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Tool Signature as a Mathematical Vector of Geometry

A single-point tool's functional geometry can be represented as:

$$\vec{T} = \left[ \alpha_b,\; \alpha_s,\; \alpha_e,\; \alpha_s',\; C_e,\; C_s,\; r \right]^T$$

For example, a commonly used tool for mild steel turning:

$$\vec{T} = \left[ +8°,\; +14°,\; 6°,\; 6°,\; 15°,\; 15°,\; 0.8\,\text{mm} \right]^T$$

## 3.2 Derivation of Angle Preservation in Optical Projection

Consider a small element of the tool surface subtending an angle $\theta$ at the cutting edge. After projection through a thin lens of focal length $f$, the image subtends an angle $\theta'$ on the screen.

For a thin lens imaging system:

$$\frac{1}{v} - \frac{1}{u} = \frac{1}{f}$$

For linear magnification:

$$M = -\frac{v}{u}$$

Now consider two lines meeting at angle $\theta$ at the object. Their images are parallel rays meeting at the **image plane**. Because the projection is a **similarity transformation** (scaling only, no shear), angles are preserved:

$$\theta' = \theta$$

> **Therefore, the angle read directly on the projector's protractor screen is the TRUE angle on the tool.** No correction factor is needed for angles.

For linear measurements (such as the **nose radius**):

$$r_{true} = \frac{r_{screen}}{M}$$

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Conversion — Reading a Sample Tool

**Given:** A HSS tool is mounted on the profile projector. At 20× magnification, the operator measures the following on the screen:
- The linear distance across the nose on the screen = **18 mm**
- The side clearance angle shown on the protractor = **6°**
- The end clearance angle = **6°**
- The end cutting edge angle = **15°**
- The side cutting edge angle = **15°**
- The back rake angle (tool rotated) = **+8°**
- The side rake angle (tool rotated) = **+14°**

**Step 1:** Calculate the actual nose radius:

$$r = \frac{r_{screen}}{M} = \frac{18\,\text{mm}}{20} = 0.9\,\text{mm}$$

**Step 2:** Since angles are preserved, the measured angles are the true angles.

**Step 3:** Write the tool signature in the ASA convention order:

$$\text{Tool Signature} = (8°,\; 14°,\; 6°,\; 6°,\; 15°,\; 15°,\; 0.9\,\text{mm})$$

This corresponds to a **positive rake, sharp-nosed, mild-steel-finishing tool**.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Reading Each Angle on the Projector

| Angle on Tool | How Tool is Oriented on Projector Stage | What You Read on Screen |
|---|---|---|
| **αb (Back Rake)** | Tool held with **side cutting edge horizontal** on screen; view the **rake face from the side** | Angle of rake face w.r.t. horizontal |
| **αs (Side Rake)** | Tool rotated 90° (or use a special fixture); view the rake face from the **end** | Angle of rake face w.r.t. tool base |
| **αe (End Relief)** | Side cutting edge horizontal, observe **end flank (primary clearance face)** | Angle between end flank and horizontal (machined surface) |
| **αs' (Side Relief)** | End cutting edge horizontal, observe **side flank (secondary clearance face)** | Angle between side flank and horizontal |
| **Ce (End Cutting Edge)** | Side cutting edge horizontal, observe tool tip region | Angle of end cutting edge w.r.t. side cutting edge / horizontal |
| **Cs (Side Cutting Edge)** | End cutting edge horizontal | Angle of side cutting edge w.r.t. reference (machined surface) |
| **r (Nose Radius)** | Magnified profile of the nose | Measure the curved arc on screen, divide by M |

## 3.5 Calibration Check Before Measurement

> [!NOTE]
> Always verify the **stage zero** and **protractor zero** before taking readings:
> 1. Switch on the lamp, allow **5 minutes warm-up**.
> 2. Place a **calibration reticle / gauge block** on the stage.
> 3. Focus the screen image using the focusing knob.
> 4. Align the reticle cross-lines; verify **0° – 0° coincidence** on the protractor.
> 5. Confirm the **magnification dial** is locked at 10× / 20× / 50× as required.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of a Profile Projector

```mermaid

    B --> C[WORK STAGE X-Y Table with Micrometer]
    C --> D[OBJECTIVE LENS Magnification 10x 20x 50x 100x]
    D --> E[ROTARY PROTRACTOR SCREEN 360 deg]
    E --> F[CROSS HAIR REFERENCE LINES]
    F --> G[MEASUREMENT READOUT]

    subgraph OPTICS [Optical Train]
      A
      B
      D
    end

    subgraph STAGE [Mechanical Stage]
      C
    end

    subgraph SCREEN [Viewing and Measurement Screen]
      E
      F
      G
    end
```

## 4.2 Tool Signature Measurement Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Start Experiment] --> P2[Switch ON Profile Projector]
    P2 --> P3[Allow 5 min Warm Up]

    P5 --> P6[Choose Magnification 10x or 20x]
    P6 --> P7[Focus the Tool Image on Screen]

    P8 --> P9[Read End Relief Angle alpha e]

    P8 --> P14[Read Side Rake Angle alpha s]
    P8 --> P15[Measure Nose Radius on Screen]

    P9 --> P16[Tabulate all 7 Values]
    P10 --> P16
    P11 --> P16
    P12 --> P16
    P13 --> P16
    P14 --> P16
    P15 --> P16

    P16 --> P17[Convert Linear Measurements using M]

```

```mermaid

      D4 --- D5[End Cutting Edge Angle Ce]
      D5 --- D6[Side Cutting Edge Angle Cs]
      D6 --- D7[Nose Radius r]
    end

    D1 -.measured on.-> P[PROFILE PROJECTOR SCREEN]
    D2 -.measured on.-> P
    D3 -.measured on.-> P
```

```mermaid
    R1 --- SA[alpha s Side Rake]
```
